COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

waiter and searcher and acting collector of customs in Grenada, from 1838 to 1843; member of the House of Assembly there, from 1843 to 1859; also member of the committee for auditing public accounts, a member of the board of works, chairman of the roads board and board of health, Parish St. George; J.P., 1856; a member of executive council and of the board of education; Feb. 1859, appointed police magistrate, Western District, Grenada; appointed May, 1860, police Magistrate, Abaco, Bahamas; is also coroner and notary public.
BROWNE, FIELDING.—Commissioner of insolvent estates and court of requests, Hobart Town, Oct. 1, 1859.

BROWNE, COLONEL GORE, C.B. (Military).—Commanded the 41st regiment during the whole of the campaign in Afghanistan, and was present at nearly all the engagements with the enemy; for this service he was made a brevet lieut.-colonel, and a companion of the Bath; appointed governor and commander-in-chief of St. Helena, 1851; governor, &c., of New Zealand, 1854 to 1861, and governor of Tasmania, March, 1862.

BROWNE, J. C., F.R.G.S —Superintendent of government schools, Mauritius; is inspector of elementary schools, and secretary ex-officio to the council of education; was appointed organizing master at Mauritius, October, 1860, and superintendent, &c., February, 1862.
BROWNING, G. W.—Sub-collector of customs, Port Elizabeth, Cape of Good Hope, appointed April, 1865. Has been in the customs' department since 1845.

BROWNLOW, G.—Graduated B.A. at Oxford in 1855; joined the Cape colonial office, Jan. 1856; transferred to British Kaffraria, June, 1857; appointed secretary to the lieutenant-governor, Nov. 1860; and secretary to the government of British Kaffraria, Sept. 1861; appointed auditor, Sept. 10, 1862; resigned, 1865.
BROWNRIGG, M. E.—Police inspector, Mauritius, February, 1866.
BRUMELL, J.—Police magistrate, Georgetown, British Guiana, 1858; was stipendiary magistrate in that colony, from 1855 to 1858; is also sheriff of Demerara.

BRYANT, J. H.—Entered civil service of Cape Colony, as clerk in the audit office, in Sept. 1852; transferred to British Kaffraria and appointed to survey department, as 1st clerk, in June, 1856; in charge of survey department from July, 1858; acting registrar of deeds from Oct. 1858 to Dec. 1861; appointed surveyor-general of British Kaffraria in Dec. 1861.
BUDGE, WILLIAM.—Appointed registrar of the courts, Trinidad, provisionally, July, 1866.
BUDGEN, T.—Assistant inspector of roads and bridges, Mauritius, November, 1865.
BULL, CHARLES.—Appointed colonial chaplain, Falkland Islands, in April, 1860.

BULLER, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1848).—SIR ARTHUR WILLIAM BULLER.—Educated at Edinburgh College and Trinity College, Cambridge, where he graduated M.A. 1834; was called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1834, and went the western circuit; was Queen's advocate in Ceylon from 1840 to July, 1848, when he was appointed a judge of the supreme court of Calcutta, and knighted; retired 1858; has been M.P. for Devonport since June, 1859.
BULWER, HENRY ERNEST, C.M.G.—Educated

at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A. 1859; accompanied Lieut.-Gov. Dundas as private secretary to Prince Edward Island, June, 1859; appointed resident of Paxo, Ionian Islands, Oct. 1860; resident of Cerigo, January, 1862, which post he held until the withdrawal of the British protectorate in 1864; named a companion of the Order of St. Michael and St. George, Sept. 1864; receiver-general, Trinidad, 1866.
BURNET, JOHN.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Aliwal North, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1855.

BURNSIDE, JOHN B.—Was assistant private secretary and clerk of works, Bahamas, 1848; private secretary to Governor Gregory, 1850; and appointed resident justice at Inagua, Bahamas, 1854; police magistrate, Harbour Island district, 1865.

BURT, ARCH. PAULL.—Called to the bar at the Middle Temple, Nov. 1845; was attorney-general of St. Christopher from 1849 to 1860; and was a member of the administrative committee of that island; appointed civil commissioner and chairman of quarter sessions in Western Australia, July, 1860; and appointed chief justice, 1861.

BURT, AND. P.—Member of Assembly, 1865; cemetery commissioner, 9th Sept. 1864; manager of savings bank, Dec. 1864; Her Majesty's approval of appointment as counsel learned in the law, 15th Feb. 1865; barrister-at-law of St. Kitts; appointed Queen's counsel at Virgin Islands, August, 1861.
BURTON, H.—Emigration agent for Mauritius at Madras; appointed 1859.
BURY, F. M.—Revenue officer Essequibo, appointed 1861; inspector of police, 1863.
BUSHBY, ARTHUR T.—Appointed clerk to the judge of British Columbia and acting registrar of the supreme court, Feb. 1859. Promoted to be registrar-general, August, 1861.
BUSH, H. G.—Inspector of police, Trinidad; appointed 1859. Is also inspector of prisons.

BUSH, J. SCORR.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin; private secretary to the Right Hon. Lord Harris, governor of Trinidad, 1852; private secretary to Sir Charles Elliot, 1855; escribano of the court of intendant, Trinidad, 1853 to 1859; appointed colonial secretary, Trinidad, provisionally, 1859; confirmed, 1861.
BUTLER, ROBERT.—Appointed chief clerk in the receiver-general's office at Bahamas, 1856. Was assistant clerk to the house of assembly, 1853, was second clerk to Treasurer, Aug. 1855 to 1856.
BUTTIE, CHAS. R.—Appointed first clerk to stipendiary magistrate, Grand Port, Mauritius, March, 1846.
BUTTS, H. GROSVENOR.—Health officer of the Port of Georgetown, British Guiana; was acting surgeon-general, 1861-62.
BYNG, HENRY.—Appointed Government clerk Falkland Islands, May, 1863.
BYRNES, HENRY.—Assistant clerk to the magistrate, Pamplemousses, Mauritius, July, 1856; joint clerk, April, 1860.

BYRNE, HENRY (C.E.).—Appointed assistant in surveyor-general's department, Ceylon, and assistant civil engineer and surveyor from Feb. 1842; was admitted into the civil service of that colony, Nov. 1844; appointed assistant civil engineer, commissioner of roads, and surveyor, 1851; acted as 1st assistant civil engineer and commissioner of roads, 1859 to 1863.
BYRNE, J.—Appointed clerk in the audit office, Natal, 1859; clerk in treasury, 1863.
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

CADIZ, C. FITZWILLIAM, B.A.—Of Pembroke College, Oxford, was called to the bar by the Honourable Society of Lincoln's Inn in 1855; acting clerk of council of the island of Trinidad in 1858; member of privy council of the Island of Tobago; appointed stipendiary magistrate in the said island, April, 1862; and coroner and visiting justice of the gaol; member of the executive committee, 1860.
CAIRD, H. W.—Educated at Oriel College, Oxford. 2nd master of the Queen's collegiate school, Trinidad; appointed 1859.

CAIRNS, W. W.—Appointed a writer in the civil service of Ceylon, Dec. 1852; acting assistant government agent, Galle, 1853; assistant government agent, Trincomalee, 1856; assistant government agent, Matura, 1859; acting postmaster-general, 1864; assistant government agent, Badulla, 1865, and acting postmaster-general.
CALDER, ALEX.—Clerk in treasury, British Colombia, appointed Feb. 1860, late surveyor to the British North American Boundary Commission.
CALDER, JAMES ERSKINE.—Surveyor-General and commissioner of crown lands, Tasmania, Sept. 1, 1859.
CALDERWOOD, H.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Victoria East, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1847.

CALDWELL, JAMES.—Appointed master government Schools, Mauritius, February, 1848; professor in the Royal College at Mauritius, June, 1849; and interpreter for French and English creole in the law courts of that island, June, 1856.
CALEY, J. A.—Appointed an assistant commissioner of roads to the government of Ceylon, March, 1846.

CALLAGHAN, THOMAS J.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin, took honours in classics, held the appointment of Barrington Lecturer on Political Economy to the Dublin Statistical Society; was counsel to the attorney-general for Ireland; appointed chief magistrate, Hong Kong, 1860. Called to the Irish bar, 1854; appointed governor of Labuan, July, 1861; is consul at Borneo.
CALLANDER, ROBERT J.—Served in the secretary's department of the admiralty from beginning of 1854 till the beginning of 1866, when he was appointed auditor-general of Ceylon.

CAMERON, DAVID.—Appointed in January, 1854, to the provisional office of Judge of the supreme court of civil justice of Vancouver's Island. Confirmed and appointed as chief justice, April 1856; resigned, 1865.
CAMERON, E. H.—Lieut. Royal Artillery, private secretary and A.D.C. to Mr. Walker, governor-in-chief of the Windward Islands, 1861.
CAMMERER, T. C.—Professor Royal College, Mauritius, April, 1864.
CAMPBELL, SIR ALEXANDER, 2ND BART.—Appointed superintendent of police in Western Australia, Sept. 1857; resident magistrate of Albany in that colony, 1861.
CAMPBELL, A.—Employed in the establishment of the civil engineer and commissioner of roads, Ceylon, March, 1847; appointed assistant civil engineer and commissioner of roads, 1858.

CAMPBELL, JOHN.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Port Elizabeth, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1857. Also visiting magistrate of convicts; was clerk of the peace at Colesburg, 1837 to 1851; clerk of the peace for Albany, July, 1851 to 1857.
CAMPBELL, JOHN A.—Appointed clerk in
the post-office at Mauritius, May, 1858. First clerk in medical department, Feb. 1859.

CAMPBELL, F. H.—Appointed commissioner of the court of requests, Putlam, Ceylon, 1856; held the same office at Jaffna, 1857; and acting district judge of Matura, March, 1860; resumed duties at Jaffna, 1861; acting district judge at Batticoloá, Sept. 1864.
CAMPBELL, H.—Appointed second clerk in the registrar of deeds office, Natal, April 1861; and second clerk audit office, Jan. 1863.
CAMPBELL, WM. HUNTER, LL.D.—Clerk of the supreme court of criminal justice, Demerara, and Essequibo, British Guiana, 1847.
CAMPBELL, JOHN PATERSON.—Clerk, second class, treasury, Tasmania, Jan. 20, 185.
CAMPBELL, JOHN WILLIAM.—Appointed April, 1852, to the customs at Hobart Town, Tasmania; holds the Office of senior landing waiter in that colony.
CANN, JOHN.—Assistant receiving 2clerk, Flacq district, Mauritius, April, 1850; chief clerk to stipendiary magistrate, Rivère du Rampart, Feb. 1857.
CANNING, JOHN.—Appointed assistant collector of revenue, Newfoundland, 1861; formerly landing and tide surveyor of the customs.

CAPE TOWN, 1st BISHOP OF. Founded 1847.— RIGHT REV. ROBERT GRAY, D.D.—Educated at Eton and at University College, Oxford, where he graduated B.A. 1831, M.A. 1834; ordained 1833; appointed perpetual curate of Whitworth, Durham, 1834; vicar of Stockton-on-Tees, 1845; honorary canon of Durham Cathedral, 1846; and 1st Bishop of Cape Town, 1847. The diocese at its foundation included the whole of the Cape of Good Hope, Port Natal, and St. Helena, but in 1853 it was confined to the western districts with the island of St. Helena, and made metropolitan in jurisdiction, while two new dioceses were cut off and made suffragans, vide Graham's Town and Natal, to which have been added the dioceses of St. Helena and those for Central Africa and the Free State.
CARBONE, CARL, LL.D.—Appointed a magistrate of judicial police, Malta, May, 1863; served in Gozo in the same capacity from October, 1837.
CARDIN, J.—Treasury clerk, St. Kitt's; appointed March, 1857.

CARDWELL, EDWARD, RT. HON.—Educated at Balliol Coll., Oxford, of which he became a fellow; took a double first-class degree in 1835, and was made an honorary D.C.L. 1863. Called to the bar at the Inner Temple 1838. Was secretary to the Treasury from Feb. 1845 till July 1846. President of the Board of Trade from Dec. 1852 till Feb. 1855; chief secretary for Ireland from June 1859 till July 1861, when he was appointed Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster; a liberal conservative; sat for Clitheroe from 1842 (when he was seated on petition) till July 1847; for Liverpool from the last date till July 1852, when he was an unsuccessful candidate for that town and for Ayrshire; for Oxford city from Jan. 1853 till April 1857, when he was an unsuccessful candidate there; again returned for Oxford July following; secretary of state for the colonies, April 4, 1864, to July 6, 1866.
CAREY, T. C.—Was appointed assistant surgeon, Western Australia, 1862.
CARGILL, J. F.—Assistant judge of the supreme court of Jamaica; appointed 1856. Is also judge of the circuit and insolvent debtors courts; called to the bar, Middle Temple, June, 1841;
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
appointed a revising barrister, Jamaica, 1848; was acting chairman of quarter sessions, 1855.
CARLETON, HUGH.—Chairman of committees, New Zealand.

CARNARVON, 4th EARL of (Creat. 1793); Baron Porchester, 1780 (Gt. Brit.).—HENRY HOWARD MOLYNEUX HERBERT.—Succeeded his father in 1849; educated at Christ Church, Oxford, where he was 1st class in classics, 1852; appointed a deputy-lieut. of Hants, and capt. Hants yeomanry 1853; constable of Carnarvon Castle, 1854; high steward of the University of Oxford, 1859; was under-secretary of state for the colonies during Lord Derby's 2nd administration, 1857 to 1859; secretary of state for Colonies in Lord Derby's third administration, 6 July, 1866.
CARR, D. L.—Appointed third writer in secretary's office, Gold Coast, 1861.

CARR, JOHN.—Educated at University College, London; obtained the first certificate of honour and the prize in history, 1838, and the first certificate of honour and the first prize in English law in 1839; graduated bachelor of laws, 1839, University of London; called to the bar at Gray's Inn, May, 1840; was appointed queen's advocate at Sierra Leone, May, 1840; administered the government as acting governor from April to Sept. 1841; appointed chief justice, Aug. 1841. Is judge of the vice-admiralty court, judge of the court for the relief of insolvent debtors, and sole judge of the supreme court. Is also senior member, and in the absence of the governor, president of the council of the colony.
CARTER, P. W.—Stipendiary magistrate of St. John's, Newfoundland; appointed 1815. Is also registrar of the vice-admiralty court in that colony.
CARTER, SIR J. (Created 1859).—Appointed chief justice, New Brunswick, Jan. 1857.

CARVER, W. J.—Graduated at Magdalen College, Cambridge, and called to the bar of the Inner Temple, 1856; appointed acting deputy Queen's advocate for southern circuit, Ceylon, in Oct. 1858; acting police magistrate, Damboul, Jan. 1861; acting commissioner of requests, Harrispattoo, Ceylon, 1862; appointed to be registrar of the supreme court, Feb. 1864. Is a justice of the peace, and coroner.
CARY, G. H.—Called to the bar at the Inner Temple, June, 1854; appointed attorney-general of British Columbia, March, 1859; and attorney-general of Vancouver's Island, April, 1861.
CAUFOR, C. C.—Appointed superintendent of police, Sierra Leone, 1861.

CAULFEILD, H. C.—Appointed writer to the government of Ceylon, Dec. 1859; acting police magistrate, Avishamelle, April, 1863; Harrispattoo, June, 1863; and assistant to government agent, Kandy, November, 1863; commissioner of requests and police magistrate at Harrispattoo, 1865; assistant government agent, Mulletvoe, 1866.

CHALLENGER, RICHARD.—Notary, St. Kitt's, 1830; justice of peace, 1838; stipendiary magistrate, Anguilla, 1842; coroner, 1846, to present time; a member of administrative committee, Dec. 1858; member of executive council, 1858.
CHALMERS, W. B.—Entered the Kaffrarian civil service, Jan. 1856; magistrate Transkeian territory, Sept. 1861; ditto at Hopetown, 1864.
CHALMERS, CATHCART.—Crown surveyor, British Guiana, appointed 1857.
CHANT, JAMES.—Was appointed clerk to the South Australian colonization commission, Jan. 1837; and clerk to the colonial land and emigration commissioners, July, 1841.
CHANTRELL, H. W.—Secretary Royal College, Mauritius, May, 1863; clerk, Treasury, May 1864.
CHAPLYN, REV. GEORGE, M.A.—Appointed chaplain, Virgin Islands Oct. 1, 1849.

CHAPMAN, HENRY SAMUEL.—Barrister. Appointed judge of the supreme court of New Zealand, June, 1843; continued to hold the office until March, 1852, when he was appointed colonial secretary of Van Diemen's Land, now Tasmania. In November of the same year he vacated his office. In October, 1854, he commenced practice in Melbourne; in February, 1855, was elected a member of the legislative council; and in March, 1857 (the New Constitution having been introduced in the meantime), he became attorney-general, but the cabinet of which he was a member only held office two months. In March, 1858, being then member of the assembly in St. Kilda, he was called upon by Sir Henry Barkly to form a ministry, in which he succeeded, he himself resuming his former office of attorney-general, which he retained till October, 1859, when the ministry of which he formed part was defeated. In 1862-3, during the absence, on leave, of Sir Redmond Barry, one of the judges of the supreme court, Mr. Chapman filled his seat on the bench, sitting as equity judge. This office he vacated on the return of Sir Redmond Barry, in March, 1863. For several years during the interals of office, he filled the chair of law lecturer at the Melbourne University, which office he continues to hold. He was for many years a writer in the Westminster, London , and other quarterly reviews; contributed some articles to the seventh edition of the "Encyclopædia Britannica;" and still furnishes articles on subjects of colonial law to the Law Magazine .
CHAPMAN, W.—Appointed clerk in the office of secretary of state for the colonies, June, 1824; retired on a pension, 1863.

CHAPMAN, WM.—Was employed under the board of public works in Ireland, 1851; entered the colonial service at Hong Kong, 1852; was clerk in colonial secretary's office, 1853; clerk to the auditor-general, 1854; afterwards employed in the post-office, and on the transfer of the post-office from the control of the imperial to that of the colonial government was appointed post-master-general, May, 1860; retired on a pension, 1861.
CHARLTON, WILLIAM.—Appointed clerk in the audit office at Mauritius, March, 1856; and senior examiner in that department, Jan. 1860.
CHARSLEY, WM. PARKER, M.D., M.R.C.S.E.—Was appointed to the civil medical department in Ceylon, June, 1846; principal civil medical officer, May, 1859.

CHASTEAUNEUF, JOHN AUGUSTUS.—Was appointed clerk in the registration office at Mauritius, August, 1840; examiner and comptroller of revenue, audit office, July, 1847; chief clerk, June, 1854; officer in charge of audit office, May, 1861; and acting collector of internal revenue, Oct. 1861; acting protector of immigrants, Oct. 1862; resumed duties as chief clerk, audit, May, 1864.
CHASTELLIER, DR.—Surgeon-superintendent Cannonier Point, Mauritius, 1866.

CHENARD, AUGUSTE.—Appointed a clerk in the medical department at Mauritius, January, 1828; clerk in the customs, September, 1837; accountant and purveyor and collector of hospital dues in that island, December, 1839.
CHILD, W. A.—Stipendiary magistrate at Tobago; appointed 1835.
CHILDERS, R. C.—Educated at Wadham College, Oxford.—Appointed a writer to the govern-
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

ment of Ceylon, 1860; and private secretary to Sir C. MacCarthy, on his appointment as governor of that colony; acting assistant to government agent, Kandy, April, 1863; commissioner of requests and police-magistrate, Harrispattoo, June, 1863 (to continue to act at Kandy); resigned 1865.

CHOPPIN, JAMES CLEMENT.—Attorney-general of St. Vincent. Appointed 1863. Was called to the bar at the Middle Temple, 1843; first appointment in colonial service in 1845 as acting police and stipendiary magistrate in St. Vincent; appointed solicitor-general in 1853; was for some years speaker of assembly; appointed to legislative council in 1863.

CHRISTCHURCH (New Zealand), 1st BISHOP of. Founded 1856.—RIGHT REV. HENRY JOHN CHITTY HARPER, D.D.—Born at Gosport, 1807; educated at Hyde Abbey, Winchester, and at Queen's College, Oxford, where he graduated B.A. 1826, M.A. 1840. After having been private tutor to the sons of Sir Charles Coote, he officiated for many years as "conduct" or chaplain to Eton College, by which body he was presented in 1840 to the vicarage of Stratfield Mortimer, near Reading, whence he was appointed, in 1856, first Bishop of Christchurch. The diocese includes a portion of the middle island of New Zealand, the whole of the southern island, the Auckland islands, and other smaller islands.

CHRISTIE, WILLIAM H.—Late major 80th Regt.; was educated at Rugby, and at the Royal Military Academy, Woolwich, obtaining a certificate of the first class as a student at the Royal Military College, Sandhurst, in 1832; was appointed ensign, 1825; lieutenant, 1827; captain, 1833; major, 1839. Major Christie's colonial appointments date as follows: engineer of Liverpool stockades, 1837; assistant police magistrate, 1839; agent of church and school lands, 1842; serjeant-at-arms, legislative council, 1847; secretary to board of education, 1848; postmaster-general of New South Wales, 1852.

CHURCHILL, J. F.—Appointed an assistant civil engineer and commissioner of roads, Ceylon, 1856; acted as civil engineer &c. of the island, 1859 to 1861, when he resumed his own duties as assistant civil engineer, Colombo; proceeded to England, July, 1861, where he was employed, under the direction of the secretary of state, on duty connected with the proposed Ceylon railway; resumed his duties in Ceylon April, 1863.

CLARK, J. S.—Appointed treasury clerk, Labuan, June 1, 1854; interpreter to the general court in Malay in March, 1861; acting registrar of the general court in April, 1862; officiated as harbour and postmaster from June 4 to August 1, 1862; was acting chief constable and bailiff, from April 25 to August 21, 1862, and has been acting again in the same capacities since July 12, 1863.
CLARKE, Rev. J. K.—Presbyterian colonial chaplain, Galle, Ceylon, 1847.

CLARKE, ROBERT, L.R.C.S.E., May 20, 1834.—Appointed assistant colonial surgeon at Sierra Leone, 1837; held subsequently the offices of colonial apothecary and acting colonial surgeon for Freetown till 1854; suggested important alterations and improvements in the Freetown prison, which were approved; appointed surgeon on the Gold Coast, Oct. 1854; discharged the duties of civil commandant, magistrate, postmaster, &c., without pay; appointed sub-collector of customs, April, 1856; acting judicial assessor, July, 1857, to May, 1858; member of executive and legislative councils of the Gold Coast; stipendiary magistrate of Cape Coast; pensioned owing to ill-

health in 1860. Author of "Manners and Customs of Liberated Africans," "Notes on the Prevailing Diseases in the Colony of Sierra Leone," read before the British Association, 1855; "Medical Report on the Gold Coast for 1858" ( vide parliamentary reports of Her Majesty's colonial possessions), "Remarks on Topography of the Gold Coast," &c.

CLARKE, SIR ROBERT BOWCHER, C.B. and B.C.L.—Educated at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he graduated LL.B. 1827; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1827; was solicitor-general of Barbados from 1837 to 1842, and speaker of the house of assembly there; appointed chief justice of Barbados in 1842. Was also chief justice of St. Lucia from 1848 to 1859; was knighted by patent, 1840, in recognition of his services in promoting the termination of the apprenticeship system and other measures connected with the emancipation of the slaves; created a C.B. in 1848.

CLARKE, W. D., LL.D.—Was classical master in the Colombo academy, Ceylon, from March, 1844 to Oct. 1855; was appointed commissioner of requests and police magistrate of Bentotte, in that colony, 1846; acting district judge, &c. Kurnegalle, 1853; district judge, &c. of Badulla, 1859; retired 1863.

CLARKE, WILLIAM PHILLIPS.—Entered the colonial secretary's office Barbadoes, May, 1853; appointed chief clerk, 1855; was private secretary to Governor Walker during his administration of the government of Trinidad, 1860-61; acted at the same time as clerk of the council of government in that island; returned to Barbados, March, 1861, and resumed his duties in the colonial secretary's office; private secretary to Mr. Hollyar, who administers the Government of St. Kitts, 1864-5-6.
CLAUDET, F. G.—Appointed chief assayer in the assay office at New Westminster, British Columbia, Oct. 1859.
CLAWSON, EDWARD G.—Appointed harbour and shipping-master, Barbados, May, 1855.
CLAXTON, PHILIP C.—Appointed a clerk in emigration commissioners' office, London, Nov. 1854.
CLEAVELAND, J.—Medical assistant, Ceylon, 1858, has been in the service since 1832.

CLEMENTS, JOHN.—Educated at Winchester. Entered H. M. 2nd dragoon guards (queen's bays), as cornet, by purchase in 1848; was promoted to be lieutenant by purchase, Aug. 1850; was appointed adjutant to the regiment in April, 1852; resigned the adjutancy, and exchanged to the 15th king's hussars in 1854; retired from the army by the sale of his commission in 1855; was a captain in the royal Sherwood foresters in 1856-58; appointed inspector-general of police in Sept. 1858; and colonel and adjutant-general of militia in Barbados, Feb. 1860.
CLERIHEW, GEORGE, M.D.—Chief medical officer, Mauritius; appointed April, 1854; is deputy inspector-general and a commissioner in lunacy.
CLEVERLY, C. ST. GEO.—Appointed assistant engineer in 1843; acting surveyor-general in 1844; surveyor-general of Hong Kong, 1847; is a member of the legislative council; retired on a pension, 1865.

CLIFTON, L. W.—Was clerk in the colonial secretary's office in Western Australia, from 1857 to 1859, when he was removed to the customs' department, as first clerk; appointed acting collector of customs in that colony, 1862; appointed collector, 1863.
CLIFTON, W. J.—Chief clerk, colonial secretary's office, Western Australia, appointed 1856.
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

CLOETE, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1854); C.B. 1848; K.C.H. 1836.—SIR ABRAHAM JOSIAS CLOETE.—Entered the army in 1809; became a major-general in 1855; was for many years deputy-quarter-master-general at the Cape of Good Hope; appointed commander of the forces in the Windward and Leeward Islands, with the local rank of lieut.-general, 1855; relieved by Major-general Brooke.

CLOETE, H.—Was a member of the legislative council of the Cape Colony, 1836; H.M's commissioner for the Natal territory, May, 1843; recorder for that province, Nov. 1845; and appointed 2nd puisne judge of the supreme court, Cape of Good Hope, May, 1858.

COAKES, C. J.—Was appointed messenger to the magistrates' court, Pietermaritzburg, Natal, 1851; clerk to the resident magistrate, Upper Umcomas, 1859; and clerk in the post-office, 1861; and postmaster at D'Urban, March, 1863.

COCHRANE, K.C.B., 1847; KNT. BACHEL., 1825.—SIR THOMAS JOHN COCHRANE.—Attained the rank of admiral of the white in 1857; served in the expeditions against Belleisle, Ferrol, Cadiz, and Egypt, &c.; was governor of Newfoundland from 1825 to 1834; was M.P. for Ipswich from 1837 to 1841; unsuccessfully contested Westminster in 1835; received the honour of knighthood, having been proxy for his father at the installation of the Bath in 1812; was commander-in-chief on the East India station from 1842 to 1846.
COCHRANE, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1845.)—SIR JAMES COCHRANE.—Admitted at the Inner Temple, 1818, and called to the bar there, 1829; is chief justice of Gibraltar.

COCKBURN, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1841.)—SIR FRANCIS COCKBURN.—Received the honour of knighthood by patent; became a general in the army in 1860; colonel of the 95th foot, 1853; has served in Canada, and was governor at Honduras; was, in 1837, governor and commander-in-chief of the Bahama Islands.

COCKBURN, SAMUEL.—Held several appointments in Grenada: viz., interpreter-general, 1844; inspector of prisons, 1845; inspector-general of schools, 1853; provost-marshal, 1854; stipendiary magistrate, 1855; immigration agent, 1856; president of Montserrat, 1860 and 1862; police-magistrate, British Honduras, June, 1862.
COCKLE, JAS.—Called to the bar at the Middle Temple, 1846; special pleader, middle circuit; appointed chief justice of Queensland, Nov. 1862.

CODRINGTON, K.C.B. (Creat. 1855.)—SIR WILLIAM JOHN CODRINGTON.—Entered the army in 1821; became lieut.-col. coldstream guards in 1836; commanded an infantry brigade in the early portion of the Crimean campaign; and subsequently commanded the light division at the capture of Sebastopol; appointed commander-in-chief of the British forces in the East, with the local rank of general, 1855, and received the brevet of lieut.-general in 1856 "as a mark of royal approbation;" made a K.C.B. 1855, grand cross of the Sardinian order of Savoy, and commander of the legion of honour, 1856, for his services against the Russians; M.P. for Greenwich since Feb. 1857 to May, 1859; appointed governor and commander-in-chief at Gibraltar, May, 1859.
COKER, WM.—Appointed clerk of the supreme court, judicial assessors' court, and magistrates' court, at Cape Coast Castle, 1857; first writer in colonial secretary's office, 1860.
COLE, E. M.—Was civil commissioner and resident magistrate, Somerset division, Cape of Good Hope, Feb. 1844; civil commissioner and
resident magistrate of Caledon, 1849; and appointed auditor-general of the colony, 1859.
COLIN, G. B.—2nd puisne judge, supreme court, Mauritius, December, 1862.
COLIN, JULES LOUIS.—District magistrate, Rivière du Rempart, Mauritius; appointed 1857.
COLLINS, JAMES.—Chief clerk in treasury office, Hong-Kong, 1843; appointed first clerk in the magistrates' office, 1846.

COLOMBO, 1st BISHOP OF. Founded 1845.—RIGHT REV. JAMES CHAPMAN, D.D.—Educated at King's College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A. 1823, M.A. 1826; was ordained in 1824; became one of the masters of Eton, and fellow of King's College; appointed rector of Dunton-Waylett, Essex, in 1834; consecrated 1st Bishop of Colombo in 1845; thereupon received the degree of D.D. from Cambridge, and was admitted ad eundem at Oxford. Retired on a pension, 1861, and elected fellow of Eton College, 1862.

COLOMBO, 2nd BISHOP OF. RIGHT REV. PIERS CALVELEY CLAUGHTON, D.D.—Educated at Repton and at University College, Oxford, where he was first class in classics and gained the English Essay prize; tutor of his college and rector of Elton in Huntingdonshire from 1845 to 1859; consecrated 1st bishop of St. Helena, 1859; translated to Colombo, 1861.
COLQUHOUN, SIR P. (LL.D.) KNT. (Creat. 1861.)—Called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1838; appointed a member of the supreme council of justice at Corfu, Dec. 1858.
COLUMBIA, BRITISH.—1st BISHOP. Founded 1859.—RIGHT REV. GEORGE HILLS, D.D.—Was perpetual curate of St. Nicholas, Great Yarmouth, 1848; and honorary canon of Norwich cathedral, 1850.

COMISSIONG, J. T.—Was appointed second clerk customs' department, Grenada, 1826; collector of customs, Tobago, 1843; and collector of customs Sierra Leone, 1850; which office he at present holds, and is in addition a member of the legislative council of that colony. Appointed also assistant judge of the court of the recorder, 1852; immigration agent, 1853; receiver of unrepresented estates, 1858; and a commissioner for settling claims to lands, 1860.
COMISSIONG, T. W.—Was appointed clerk to the chief justice of Sierra Leone on 1st May, 1854; supervisor of the government press, 1855; and government printer, 1860.
CONNAL, M. C. E.—Chief assistant engineer, Mauritius railways, April, 1862; engineer in charge, 1866.
CONNELL, J.—Clerk in the colonial secretary's office, British Columbia, appointed May, 1859.

CONNOR, H., L.L.B., Dublin University.—Barrister-at-law; was chief justice and judicial assessor at the Gold Coast, 1854; was for some time acting governor of that settlement, and appointed puisne judge, Natal, 1858; acting chief justice, 1864.
CONOLLY, P. W.—Writer, Ceylon Civil Service, 1866.
CONRAN, EDWARD, Lt.-Col., 4th W. I. R. Regt.—Commander of the forces, Gold Coast, 1864.

COOMBES, GEORGE.—Was appointed to the police force at the Seychelles, Feb. 1821; clerk of the stores in the civil engineer department at Mauritius, 1827; officer of passports, December, 1850; and appointed shipping master, April, 1851.
COOPER, CLARENCE, M.D., M.C.P.L.—Acting colonial secretary, Labuan, April, 1862; member of the legislative council; is an assistant-surgeon in the Madras army.
COOPER, HENRY.—Was appointed clerk in the
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
customs at Mauritius, Sept. 1833; acting chief clerk, 1857; appointed chief clerk, Oct. 1858; collector, May, 1863.

COOPER, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1857.)—SIR CHARLES COOPER was senior judge of the supreme court of South Australia, and was created chief justice by the governor and legislative council there, June, 1856; knighted in England after that appointment.

COOPER, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1857), Bt. 1863. —SIR DANIEL COOPER.—Elected to the legislative council of New South Wales, 1849; chosen 1st speaker of the legislative assembly under the new constitution, 1856; is member of the senate of the Sydney University, and president of the Bank of New South Wales; knighted by patent.
COOPER, JOHN.—Chief clerk and cashier in the Treasury of British Columbia, appointed March 1859.

COOPER, JAMES.—Member legislative council, Vancouver's Island, 1851, subsequently elected to the house of assembly, but resigned on being obliged to reside in British Columbia; appointed harbour-master, British Columbia, Sept. 1858.

COOPER, RICHARD B.—Was clerk to the South Australian colonization commission from 1839 to 1843, when he was appointed a clerk in the emigration commissioners' office; promoted to be assistant secretary April, 1864.
COOPER, CAPTAIN W. S.—Harbour-master at Kingston, Jamaica; appointed 1856. Is also island storekeeper; and receives half-pay as captain in the R.N.

CORNER, R. J.—Chief justice and judicial assessor at the Gold Coast; appointed April, 1858. Called to the bar at the Inner Temple, Nov. 1840. Appointed chief justice of Honduras, 1862; and judge of the vice-admiralty court, 1864.
COULL, J.—Appointed 2nd police magistrate, Antigua, 1864, has resided in the island for many years.
COURTOIS, ALFRED.—Appointed clerk of stamps, internal revenue department at the Mauritius, Nov. 1829; and cashier of the same department, Jan. 1838.
CONTI, GIOVANNI, LL.D.—Appointed one of Her Majesty's judges, Malta, Dec. 1856. Was reporter to the superior court from Oct. 1842 to 1856.

COVINGTON, M.—Lic. Med. and Surg.: Bengal College. Appointed medical sub-assistant to the government of Ceylon, 3rd class, June 1849; 2nd class, 1855; medical assistant, 1858, and assistant colonial surgeon, 1859.
COWAN, WALKINSHAW.—Appointed resident magistrate of York district, Western Australia, 1863. For many years was guardian of Aborigines, in the colony.

COWPER, CHARLES.—Colonial secretary and vice-president of the executive council of New South Wales—and premier; was premier in the second ministry formed after the introduction of responsible government, from Aug. 26, 1856, to Oct. 2, 1856; again from Sept. 7, 1857, to Oct. 20, 1859. Took office as colonial secretary, and represented the government in the legislative council, in the ministry formed by Mr. Robertson on March 9, 1860, and having been elected to represent the city of Sydney in the legislative assembly, became premier on Jan. 10, 1861.
COWPER, WILLIAM MACQUANE, M.A., of Magdalen Hall, Oxford.—Dean of Sydney, New South Wales. Is son of the late Archdeacon Cowper of Sydney.
COX, AUSTIN W.—Immigration agent, Hon-

duras, 1865; member of the executive council; surveyor of shipping, July, 1855; shipping master, Feb. 1864; coroner, Feb. 1864; speaker of the House of Assembly, from 1861; and performed the duties of colonial secretary, from Aug. 1864 to Oct. 1865, in Honduras.

COX, CHARLES.—Appointed to the colonial office, 1829; was private secretary to the following under-secretaries of state—to Mr. G. B. Hope, from 1841 to 1845; Lord Lyttelton, from 1845 to 1846; and Sir B. Hawes, from 1846 to 1851; was Her Majesty's commissioner for the New Zealand company's affairs, 1848 to 1850; promoted to a senior clerkship, 1860.
COX, C. B.—Was a clerk in the colonial office at the Cape of Good Hope, 1852; appointed civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Humansdorp division of the colony, Jan. 1859.

COX, JOHN.—Acting treasurer, and comptroller of customs and navigation laws, registrar of shipping and shipping master; appointed 1862. First appointment in colonial service as acting provost-marshal of St. Vincent; appointed clerk in treasury department in that island, 1856; deputy-treasurer, 1859.
COX, NICHOLAS.—Inspector-general of police force, British Guiana; has been acting stipendiary magistrate.
CRAMER, STEPHEN, M.D.—Surgeon to the gaol and police in Berbice; was formerly surgeon to the penal settlement River Massaruni.
CRAW, J.—Appointed secretary to the immigration board, Natal, 1863.

CREAGH, COLONEL C.—Inspector-general of police, and adjutant-general, and inspecting field-officer of militia and volunteers of St. Vincent. Late 80th regiment, captain and bt.-major; served in India under the late Field-Marshal Viscount Hardinge, in the campaigns of the Sutlej and Punjub, was present at the general actions of Moodkee, Ferozeshuhur, and Sabraon; was specially mentioned by Lord Hardinge at the battle of Ferozeshuhur; as a captain, commanded his regiment for ten days on service; served twice on the Staff and was appointed on special service as major during the Crimean war; is a lieut.-colonel in the Turkish Army; and has received a medal and three clasps for his Indian services, and the Turkish Crimean war medal.
CREASE, HENRY P. PELLEW.—Graduated at Clare Cottage, Cambridge, B.A., 1847; called to the bar at the Middle Temple, June, 1849; appointed attorney-general of British Columbia, July, 1861.

CREASY, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1860).—SIR EDWARD SHEPHERD CREASY.—Educated at Queen's College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A. 1835, M.A. 1838; called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1837; was for some years professor of ancient and modern history at University College, London; author of "Poems" and of some historical works; appointed chief justice of Ceylon, 1860, and on that occasion was knighted; was previously deputy assistant judge at the Middlesex sessions.

CRESPIGNY, CLAUDE, A.C., F.R.G.S.—Entered the R. Navy as naval cadet in 1844; commanded the gun-boat 'Snap' in the Baltic, during the Russian war. Has a Baltic medal. Resigned his commission in the R. Navy in 1859; appointed harbour-master, post-master, and store-keeper, registrar of shipping, &c., in the colony of Labuan, Aug. 1862.
CROMAR, G.—Was clerk to the 1st puisne judge at the Cape of Good Hope, 1843 to 1850; clerk to the chief justice, 1851 to 1854; clerk of
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
the peace of Swellendam division, 1854 to 1859; appointed civil commissioner and resident magistrate at Albert, 1859.

CRONIN, JAMES.—Entered the civil service on 16th January, 1860; appointed assistant sworn clerk in the registrar's office of Berbice, 17th January, 1863; sworn clerk and notary public, on the certificate of their honours the judges of the supreme court of civil justice, on the 2nd April, 1864; acting registrar of the county of Berbice, 16th November, 1864; a commissioner for administering oaths to affidavits 23rd November, 1864; and registrar of Berbice, 31st October, 1866.
CROOKS, ROBERT.—Inspector-general of police at Tobago. Appointed 1854.

CROSBY, JAMES.—Educated and graduated at Trinity College, Cambridge; called to the bar by the honourable society of the Middle Temple, and went the Hampshire sessions and the Western circuit; went to St. Vincent in the West Indies, on a special retainer; and in May, 1844, became police magistrate for the Kingstown police district in that island, with liberty to practise at the bar in all cases except those in which he had acted as a magistrate; continued as such police magistrate until March, 1857, except during the short periods of the termination of one Police Act and the enactment of another Police Act, during one, in 1853, he became speaker of the house of assembly, of which for many years he had been a member; and subsequently, in 1854, became acting treasurer of the island, until the Police Act of 1853, of which he was the author, came into operation. While in St. Vincent was the author of a Code of Laws, giving summary jurisdiction to justices of the peace, for the establishment of a police force, and for the consolidation of offences punishable by summary convictions, which code, having been successfully carried through the house of assembly, he afterwards, as speaker, signed on being passed. In March, 1857, became a stipendiary justice of the peace in and for the colony of British Guiana; and on the 1st July, 1858, was appointed immigration agent general during the absence in China of Mr. John Gardiner Austin, now lieutenant-governor of the Honduras. On the return of Mr. Austin from China, he became, from the 1st October, 1862, acting immigration agent general; and in February, 1864, on the promotion of Mr. Austin, was confirmed in the appointment of immigration agent general in and for the colony of British Guiana; and on the 8th November, 1863, was appointed acting second puisne judge of British Guiana and so continued until 22nd August, 1866, when he assumed his appointment of immigration agent general. During the early part of his residence in St. Vincent, he commanded, as lieutenant-colonel, the southern regiment of militia, and was aide-de-camp to the governor-in-chief of the Windward Islands and to the lieutenant-governor of St. Vincent; and was also the author of several other Acts of the Legislature of St. Vincent, giving a permanent revenue to that colony.
CROUCH, THOMAS JAMES.—Under-sheriff, Hobart Town, Tasmania, June 11, 1836.

CROWLY, T. G.—Was appointed clerk in the audit office, Natal, Feb. 1849; clerk to resident magistrate, Pietermaritzburg, in addition to former office, July, 1849; clerk to colonial treasurer, Dec. 1852; second clerk colonial office, Sept. 1853; acting auditor, Dec. 1855; acting postmaster of Natal, March, 1857; registrar of the supreme court, Feb. 1859; first clerk, colonial office, Oct. 1859; clerk to the executive council, and chief
clerk, colonial office, Aug. 1860; and chief clerk colonial office, Natal, Aug. 1862.
CULBERT, J. A.—Was 2nd clerk in the treasury, Bahamas, from 6th Dec. 1856, to August, 1863, when he was appointed warehouse-keeper in that colony.

CUMMINS, GEORGE.—Educated at St. John's College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A. 1821; M.A. 1838; appointed assistant-curate, Trinity Church, Trinidad, 1823; rector 1824, and Archdeacon of Trinidad, 1842.

CUMMINS, T. J.—Appointed casual receiver, Barbados, in June, 1836; police magistrate for the parish of St. George, in Jan. 1832; received H.M's mandamus, as a member of the executive and legislative council in Apr. 1840; served in that capacity nine years; presided over the criminal court of sessions in Dec. 1840; appointed commissioner of probates in March, 1847; and police magistrate for the city of Bridgetown in April, 1849.
CUNNINGHAM, P.—Inspector of police, Honduras; appointed Dec. 1860.
CUNNINGHAM, B.—Immigration agent at Tobago.

CURGENVEN, C. R.—Acted as commissioner of requests at Calpentyne, Ceylon, in 1859 and 1860; was appointed commissioner of requests, &c., at Avishawelle, in that colony, 1861; assistant-government-agent, Komegalle, 1866.
CURRIE, SIR WALTER, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1860).—Appointed commandant of the armed mounted police at the Cape of Good Hope, 1855.

CUYLER, SIR CHAS. H., BART.—Held an appointment in the customs' department at Jamaica under the imperial treasury from 1846 to 1852; appointed presiding magistrate of the Bay Islands, 1852; treasurer of British Honduras, 1855; and receiver-general of Trinidad, 1861.
DALE, L., LL.D.—Superintendent-general of education, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1859.

D'ALMADA E CASTRO, J. M.—First clerk colonial secretary's office, Hong Kong; appointed 1845. Entered the public service, Jan. 1841, in the office of the chief superintendent of British trade in China; appointed 3rd clerk in colonial secretary's office, 1843.

D'ALMADA E CASTRO, L.—Chief clerk colonial secretary's office, Hong Kong; appointed 1843. Is also clerk of the council. Entered the public service in the office of the chief superintendent of British trade in China in January, 1837.
DALMAS, G.—Clerk to the commissary of the Monte di Pietà, Malta.
DALTON, EDWARD HENRY GORING.—Second sworn clerk and notary public in registrar's office of Demerara and Essequibo.
DALTON, E. T. E.—Postmaster of British Guiana; appointed 1837. Also vendor of stamps.
DALTON, EZRA.—Appointed assistant civil engineer, Ceylon, December, 1856; superintending officer, 1st March, 1866.

DALY, SIR DOMINICK, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1856).—Was appointed secretary to the province of Lower Canada in 1827 (having previously held the office of assistant secretary in that province for two years), on the union of the provinces of Upper and Lower Canada in 1840; was appointed secretary of the province of Canada, which office he held until 1848; in 1849 was appointed a commissioner of inquiry into the claims, &c. &c., of the New and Waltham Forests, which he held until the close of the commission in 1850-1; appointed lieut.-governor of Tobago, 1851, transferred to the government of Prince Edward's Island in 1854, which he held until 1859; and appointed governor

and commander-in-chief of South Australia, Nov. 1861.
DALY, JOHN.—Administrator-general of Demerara and Essequibo; appointed 1851. Paid by fees. Was appointed harbour-master at Berbice, 1827.
DALY, JOHN, jun.—Stipendiary magistrate, British Guiana; was inspector of police and also a sworn clerk in the registrar's office.

DALZIEL, JOHN.—Entered the army in 1813; embarked with his regiment, 71st light infantry, for the West Indies, Jan. 1815; but the destination of the regiment being altered, landed in Flanders; served during the campaign of 1815; was present at the battle of Waterloo, and subsequent operations; was present at the surrender of Paris, served with the army of occupation, and returned to England in 1818; served with the 78th highlanders in Ceylon, 1826-37; was appointed superintendent of police at Colombo, 1839; police magistrate, coroner, and justice of the peace, Colombo, 1844; he also acted for three months in 1857, as commissioner of requests in Gampolla, and for the same period as district judge in Caltura, 1858-9; retired 1864.
DAMPIER, FREDERICK ELLIOT.—Superintendent of rivers and creeks, Essequibo, British Guiana 1860; Stipendiary Magistrate, 1865.
DANIELL, G. W.—Police magistrate, and member of the legislative and executive councils of Nevis.

DANIELL, WM. MEADE.—Entered the colonial service in 1839, as clerk in the governor's office; Antigua, and served in that capacity under three successive governors-in-chief; was private secretary to President Daniels, when administering the government of Antigua; appointed a landing-waiter of the customs establishment in 1844, and on its abolition was nominated to a clerkship in the treasury; harbour-master of Parham in 1857; and re-appointed to the treasury, 1862.

D'ARCY, COLONEL GEO.—Appointed governor and commander-in-chief of the Gambia, June, 1859; was adjutant of the 94th regiment, in which corps he served 16 years, promoted by purchase as lieutenant-colonel in the 3rd West India regiment; after commanding the corps for three years, agreeable to royal warrant gazetted as brevet-colonel, July 7, 1857; sold out, May, 1858; appointed governor of the Gambia in June, 1859; served in the southern Mahratta war, 1845-6, East Indies; organized an expedition against the powerful robber king of Badiboo, and commanded the royal Gambia militia artillery throughout the successful campaign which followed during the months of Feb and March, 1861; was aide-de-camp from 1839 to 1848, to five successive governors of Bombay, and also to General Sir Willoughby Cotton, G.C.B., 1850-51, when he commanded the Bombay army; author of a work on Light Infantry Drill.

DARLING, SIR CHARLES HENRY, K.C.B. (Creat. 1862.)—Entered the army as ensign on the 7th Dec. 1826, by recommendation from the Royal Military College, Sandhurst, after public examination; became in 1827, assistant private secretary to the late General Sir Ralph Darling, then governor of New South Wales and major-general commanding the forces in that colony and Van Diemen's Land; and was also appointed military secretary to that officer in 1830. In 1833, while a student in the senior department of the Royal Military College, was appointed secretary to the late Lieut.-General Sir Lionel Smith, upon that officer's nomination to be governor and commander-in-chief of Barbados and the Windward Islands, and was employed in that capacity until 1836,

Sir Lionel Smith having been in that year nominated to the government of Jamaica, Mr. Darling was appointed governor's secretary for that colony, and retained the appointment until the termination of Sir Lionel Smith's government in 1839; in that year obtained an unattached company, and retired from the army in 1841; in 1843, appointed by the Earl of Elgin, then governor of Jamaica, agent-general of immigration for the colony, and held also the office of adjutant-general of militia, was a member of the assembly and of several executive boards; was again appointed governor's secretary during the ad-interim administration of the government of Jamaica by Major-General Sackville H. Berkeley, and continued to hold that office during the earlier period of the government of the Right Hon. Sir Charles Grey in 1846-47; in 1847 was appointed lieutenant-governor of the island of St. Lucia; in 1851, appointed lieutenant-governor of the Cape of Good Hope, an office specially created for the conduct at Cape Town of the civil government while Sir George Cathcart the governor and commander-in-chief, might be engaged in his military and civil duties on the frontier. After the departure of Sir George Cathcart, administered the government of the Cape from May, 1854, to Dec. 1854, during which period the parliamentary constitution of the settlement was inaugurated and established; was nominated to the government-in-chief of Antigua and the Leeward Islands, previously to his sole administration of the government of the Cape; but never assumed the duties of that government, having shortly after his arrival in England, been required to proceed to Newfoundland as administrator of the government of that colony, of which he was subsequently appointed governor and commander-in-chief; in Feb. 1857, was appointed captain-general and governor-in-chief of Jamaica, an office which embraces the general superintendence of the affairs of British Honduras and the Turks' Islands, and at that time included also the government-in-chief of the Bay Islands; received the order of the bath (K.C.B.), 1862, for his "long and effective public services." Appointed governor of Victoria, 1863; recalled, March, 1866.

DARRELL, JOHN H.—Chief justice of Bermuda; appointed 1856. Formerly of Trinity College, Cambridge; a barrister of Lincoln's Inn. Is a member of the executive and legislative councils; has been in the colonial service since 1819; was attorney-general from 1834 to 1856. Published an edition of the Laws of Bermuda in 1861.
DARRELL, NATHANIEL JOSEPH.—District commissary of taxation, British Guiana.
DARRELL, R. J.—Auditor of the public accounts and postmaster, Turks Islands; appointed 1852, and auditor 1858; registrar of births and deaths.
DARRELL, THOMAS A.—Appointed receiver-general and commissioner of customs and navigation laws, Bermudas, 1859. Is a member of the executive and legislative councils.
DARRELL, W. H.—Clerk of the assembly, and clerk of the assize court at Bermuda; appointed 1843.
DASENT, BURY IRWIN, F.R.C.S.L.—Appointed consulting surgeon of the colonial hospital, and police surgeon, Trinidad, 1857.

DASHWOOD, G. F., LIEUT. R.N.—Entered the Royal Navy, 1819; lieutenant, 1833; member of first legislative council in South Australia, 1843, 1844; acting commissioner of police, 1847 to 1849; stipendiary magistrate at Port Adelaide from Aug. 1849 to Nov. 1850; commissioner of police, 1850

to 1852; collector of customs, 1852 to 1858, ex-officio member of the legislative council, 1852 to 1855; immigration agent for South Australia in England, from Aug. 1858 to Dec. 1860; stipendiary magistrate for the eastern districts, November, 1861.

DAVEREN, J. E.—Served for 8 years in the ordnance survey; transferred to the surveyor-general's department in Ceylon in 1856; and employed on the unpaid establishment from February, 1857, till February, 1864. Appointed assistant surveyor, 1864.
DAVIDS, J. W. R.—Writer, Ceylon civil service, 1866; acting police magistrate, Kaigalle and Arvisawella, in the same year.

DAVIDSON, J. C.—Civil commissioner, clerk of the peace, and resident magistrate of George division, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1856; clerk in secretary's office from 1848 to 1853; appointed treasurer of the Cape, Nov. 1864.
DAVIES, CHARLES LANGDON.—Superintendent of the bullion office, Hong Kong mint, 1864; has been for some time employed in the Royal mint, and was appointed Feb. 21, 1864, assistant clerk to Mr. Kinder.

DAVIES, J.—Appointed clerk to the resident magistrate, D'Urban, Natal, 1854; and clerk of the peace for the counties of D'Urban and Victoria, and division of the Lower Umkimanzi, Natal, May, 1863; acting resident magistrate, D'Urban, 1864.

DAVIS, GATEWARD COLERIDGE.—Called to the bar at the Inner Temple, Jan. 26, 1860; appointed secretary of the executive council and administrative committee in Antigua, June 13, 1860; and private secretary of the governor-in-chief of the Leeward Islands, April, 1861; and appointed acting attorney-general of Antigua and crown law officer, Montserrat, July, 1862.

DAVIS, 1st BART. (United Kingdom, Created 1845); K.C.B. (Civil) 1854.—SIR JOHN FRANCIS DAVIS.—Was for some years British plenipotentiary and chief superintendent of British trade in China, governor and commander-in-chief of the colony of Hong Kong; received his baronetcy for services in China; is author of a general description of China and its inhabitants; was appointed a deputy-lieutenant of Gloucestershire, 1852.

DAVIS, W.—Appointed acting third writer in office of the colonial secretary of Sierra Leone, March, 1857; land tax clerk, 1858; acting third writer, 1858; acting fourth writer, 1859; acting second writer, 1860; and clerk of police, Dec. 1860.
DAVIS, WM. H.—Appointed superintendent of police at St. Christopher 1857; police magistrate for rural districts, July, 1863.
DAWSON, A. R.—Writer, Ceylon civil service, 1866.
DAY, CHARLES H.—Colonial engineer, Honduras; appointed 1860.
DEALTRY, W.—Educated at Eton and Trinity college, Cambridge. Appointed clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, April, 1837; promoted assistant clerk, April, 1854.
DEANE, FREDERICK.—Was employed in the office of registrar of joint stock companies, Nov. 1845, and appointed clerk to emigration commissioners, Jan. 1847.
DEANE, W. M.—Graduated at Trinity College, Cambridge, B.A., 1862; elected student interpreter, Hong-Kong, March 28, 1862; acting registrar-general, June, 1865.
D'ARCH, HENRY.—Collector of customs, Hobart Town, July 28, 1848.
DE BROSSARD, LOUIS AMEDEE.—Colonial Se-

cretary, Montserrat. On the 4th March, 1835, he was nominated to the office of solicitor-general in St. Lucia; on the 5th Feb. 1836, was appointed an official member of the legislative council; he discharged, at different periods, the duties of attorney-general, viz., in the years 1836, 1840, and 1841, and was finally confirmed by Her Majesty in that office on the 24th January, 1842; in the same year he was appointed to the office of stipendiary magistrate, and on the 4th May, 1847, provisional puisne judge of the royal court; in the month of April, 1854, was appointed immigration agent; on the 30th March, 1863, he was nominated colonial secretary, treasury, comptroller of customs and navigation laws, clerk of the crown, registrar of deeds, and registrar-general of births, deaths, and marriages, in Montserrat; is a barrister-at-law.

DEERING, SAMUEL.—Appointed by the lords of the Treasury clerk in the census office, London, 1851; appointed clerk in audit office, South Australia, 1855; chief clerk, 1859; clerk of executive council, 1863.

DEFFELL, GEORGE HIBBERT.—Appointed a commissioner of the court of claims in New South Wales, Aug. 25, 1856, and subsequently the master in equity of the supreme court of New South Wales, April 1, 1857. Educated at Harrow, and Trinity College, Cambridge; called to the bar, Inner Temple, Nov. 1846.
D'EMMÈREZ DE CHARMOY, P. F.—District and stipendiary magistrate, Grand Port, Mauritius, 1857; acting officer of the civil status, Port Louis, Sept. 1860; appointed district magistrate, Sept. 1861.
DEIGHTON, H.—Principal of the Queen's collegiate school, Trinidad; appointed 1860. From the Cambridge university.

DE JOUX, C. M.—Was appointed a clerk in the government schools office at Mauritius, May, 1854; head clerk in D. Branch, colonial secretary's office, August, 1857; and secretary to council of that island, January, 1862.

DE JOUX, JOHN R. G.—Was principal of the Dominica College from 1844 to 1847; first professor of mathematics at the Royal College, Mauritius, January, 1848; appointed civil chaplain, August, 1853, and bishop's commissary, 1857.
DE LANEY, J.—Postmaster, Newfoundland, 1861; formerly inspector of roads, under board of works, and many years a member of the assembly.

DE LAROCHE, CHARLES.—Was appointed clerk in the audit office at Mauritius, July, 1830; inspector of distilleries, Oct. 1845; superintendent of same, Oct. 1848, and chief clerk of internal revenue department, Oct. 1860.
DELOMEL, C. J.—Deputy registrar of St. Lucia; appointed 1857. Was clerk of the police from 1845.
DE MALHERBE, LOUIS CHARLES.—Appointed an extra clerk in the internal revenue office at Mauritius, May, 1838; senior clerk in the customs' department, Feb. 1859.
DEMPSEY, JOHN.—Appointed inspector of prisons at Mauritius, April, 1863.

DENISON, K.C.B. (Civil, Creat. 1856); KNT. BACHEL. 1846.—SIR WILLIAM THOMAS DENISON.—Became lieut.-col. royal engineers in 1855; was appointed lieut.-governor of Van Diemen's Land in June, 1846, and on that occasion knighted; governor-general of New South Wales, Sept. 1854 to 1860, when he was appointed governor of the Madras presidency.
DERBY, 14th EARL of (England, Creat. 1485) BARON STANLEY, 1832 (United Kingdom); BART. 1627 (England); Privy councillor, 1830.—EDWARD

GEOFFREY SMITH-STANLEY.—Succeeded his father in 1851; was educated at Christ Church, Oxford, where he gained the Latin Verse prize in 1819 (subject Syracuse ); was under-secretary for the colonies during a part of the Goderich administration; chief secretary for Ireland from 1830 till March, 1833; secretary of state for the colonies from March, 1833 till July, 1834, and from Sept. 1841 to Dec. 1845; first lord of the treasury from Feb. to Dec. 1852, and from Feb. 1858 to June, 1859; lord warden of the cinque ports, temporarily, from Oct. 1852 to Jan. 1853. Was elected lord rector of Glasgow University in 1834; and chancellor of the University of Oxford, Oct. 1852; was M.P. for Stockbridge in 1820, and for Preston from 1826 to 1830; sat for Windsor in 1830 and 1831; for North Lancashire from 1832 to Sept. 1844, when he accepted the Chiltern Hundreds, and was in the next month summoned to the House of Peers in his father's barony of Stanley of Bickerstaffe; is a deputy-lieut. of Lancashire.

DE ROBECK, G. W. B., M.A., Trinity College, Cambridge.—Appointed, after a competitive examination, a clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, Aug. 1860; private secretary to Mr. Chichester Fortescue, 16th February, 1864; to Mr. Adderley, Oct. 1866.
DE RUVIGNES, C. H.—Civil commandant, magistrate, and collector of customs for the district of Anamaboo on the Gold Coast; is also a captain in the Gold Coast artillery; resigned.

DE SARAM, C. H.—Appointed commissioner of requests at Gampolla, Ceylon, 1845; acting commissioner at Kandy, 1849; joint commissioner of requests, and police magistrate, Caltura, 1854; commissioner of the court of requests, Galle, 1857; and commissioner of requests, Colombo, 1859; district judge, Caltura, 1865.

DE SARAM, D. E.—Acting commissioner of requests, &c., Gampolla, Ceylon, 1852; a writer in the service of that colony, 1856; confirmed as commissioner of requests, Gampolla, 1857; commissioner of requests and police magistrate, Kandy, 1862; police magistrate, Colombo, Jan. 1865.

DE SARAM, J. A. H.—Appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, 1852; acting assistant government agent, Galle, 1853; acting assistant government agent, Badulla, 1853; was attached to the government agent's office, Colombo, 1854; commissioner of requests, Pantura, 1858; acting police magistrate, Gampola, 1864; acting district judge, Chilan, 1865; police magistrate, Dambool, Oct. 1865.

DE SARAM, J. H.—Writer to the government of Ceylon, 1865, and acting police magistrate of Matelle and Dambool; police magistrate of Ballepithjmodare, continuing to act at Matelle and Dambool, 1866. Has held an acting appointment as police magistrate of Chavagacherry since 1863.

DESART, 3rd EARL of (Creat. 1793); VISCT. DESART, 1781; VISCT. CASTLE-CUFFE, 1793; BARON OF DESART, 1733 (Ireland).—JOHN OTWAY O'CONNOR CUFFE.—Son of the second earl, succeeded his father in 1820; educated at Christ Church, Oxford; was elected M.P. for Ipswich in June, 1842, but was unseated on petition in the August following; elected a representative peer of Ireland, 1846; was under-secretary for the colonies from March to Dec. 1852.
DESBARRES, W. F.—One of the puisne judges, Nova Scotia.
DE SMIDT, A.—Assistant surveyor-general, Cape of Good Hope, 1863; has served in the department since 1848.
DESVIGNES, WILLIAM.—Clerk of the elective legislative assembly, Tobago, 1862.
DES VŒUX, G. W.—Appointed stipendiary magistrate at British Guiana Aug., 1863; is a justice of the peace.
DEVAUX, H.—2nd revenue officer, St. Lucia; appointed 1863.

DEVENISH, J. G.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Beaufort, division of the Cape of Good Hope colony; appointed Aug. 1857; was clerk to the civil commissioner at Beaufort, 1840 to 1855; resident magistrate at Victoria, West, Nov. 1855 to 1857.
DE VOS, C. P. G.—Many years in Ceylon post-office, promoted to be deputy postmaster-general at Galle, 1866.
DE WET, J. P.—Chief clerk of surveyor-general's department, Cape of Good Hope, 1855; has been 34 years in the colonial service.
DIAS, REV. S. W.—Appointed Singhalese chaplain of Galkisse and Morottoo, Ceylon, March, 1833; Singhalese chaplain, Colombo, 1852.
DIAS, W., M.D., M.R.C.S., L.S.A., London.—Appointed medical assistant, Ceylon, 1862.
DICKENSON, W.—Deputy inspector-general in finance minister's department, Canada.
DICKINSON, JAMES.—Surveyor of roads, St. Kitts, 7th Sept., 1865.
DICKMAN, H.—Was appointed to the civil medical department, Ceylon, 1838; colonial surgeon, 1859.
DICKSON, F.—Appointed assistant collector and landing surveyor at Trincomalee, Ceylon, 1861; acting assistant collector and landing surveyor, 1866.

DICKSON, J. F.—Educated at Westminster and Christ Church, Oxford, where he obtained a studentship, 1855; was first class in classics, moderations. Easter, 1857, and graduated B.A., 1859. Appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, Feb. 1859; attached to the colonial secretary's office Sept. 1859; acting secretary to the school commission, 1860; continuing to do duty in colonial secretary's office, and acted early in 1862 as 2nd assistant colonial secretary; police magistrate at Dambool, Oct. 1862; acting assistant agent in Kaigalle, Oct. 1862; acting police magistrate at Kandy, April 1863; acting 2nd assistant colonial secretary, Dec. 1863; secretary to the commission appointed in June, 1865, to inquire into the military expenditure and establishments of Ceylon; police magistrate of Galle, continuing to act as assistant colonial secretary, Oct. 1865.

DICKSON, HON. WILLIAM GILLESPIE.—Procureur and advocate-general, Mauritius, appointed Dec. 1856. Is a member ex-officio of the executive and legislative councils, Queen's advocate in vice-admiralty court, &c.
DILLET, STEPHEN.—Postmaster at the Bahamas; appointed 1846; and adjutant-general of the militia.

DILLET, T. WM. HENRY.—Educated at King's College, London. Entered as a student of the Inner Temple, 19th Aug., 1838; was for a short time in 1845 acting clerk of the police court at Nassau Bahamas; appointed a lieutenant in the Militia Field Artillery, Feb. 1844; served till its disbandment by the legislature; reappointed when the corps was reorganized in 1845. Called to the bar at the Bahamas, 18th April, 1848; secretary to the Board of Education, 5th June, 1850, to 31st Oct., 1851; all at the Bahamas was acting Queen's advocate at Turks Island from April to Nov. 1852, and admitted a notary public there, May, 1852. Was in the legislative assembly of

Bahamas from 24th March, 1857, to Sept., 1865, when he was called to the legislative council, of which he is now a member. From 28th Oct., 1859, to 17th May, 1860, legal adviser to the military department; acting assistant justice of the general court, Oct. 1864 to Oct., 1865; chief judicial office within the colony, from Aug. till Oct., 1865.

DINGLI, K.C.M.G. — Appointed 1860; C.B., 1859—SIR ADRIAN DINGLI. — Created doctor of law, 1836; received at Malta bar, 1837; elected member of the council of Malta, 1849; appointed crown advocate, legal adviser of the government, and ex-officio member of council, July, 1854. Received the order of St. M. and St. G. for official services.

DIX, MACNAMARA. — Colonial treasurer, St. Lucia; appointed 1860. Is member of the executive and legislative councils, and comptroller of customs and navigation laws; was auditor-general of Dominica from 1849 to 1860, and administered the government of St. Lucia from Oct. 1861 to May 1862.
DOBSON, WILLIAM LAMBERT. — Attorney-gen. at Tasmania, Feb. 11, 1861.
DODD, E. M. — One of the puisne judges in Nova Scotia.
DOMBREU, J. E. — Was clerk to the procureur-general, Mauritius, Feb. 1858 to 1863, when he was appointed district clerk at Pamplemouses.

DONALDSON, KNT. SIR STUART ALEXANDER. — Was a magistrate of New South Wales from 1838 to 1859; elected member of council, 1848; formed the first ministry there responsible to parliament, April, 1856; was member and vice-president of the executive council, first minister and colonial secretary, subsequently colonial treasurer, and commissioner of railways, 1857; knighted by patent, 1860.
DONNAN, J. — Appointed master attendant, Colombo, Ceylon, 1863; was previously commander of government steamer, 'Pearl.'

DOUGLAS, BLOOMFIELD. — Formerly in the R.N. and Coast Guard service; was officially mentioned by Admiral the Hon. Sir H. Keppel, K.C.B., and Admiral Sir E. Belcher, C.B., for services against the pirates in Borneo in 1843-4; naval officer and harbour master, S. Australia, 1854; collector of customs and naval officer, 1858; collector of customs, president of Marine Board, and stipendiary magistrate, 1860.

DOUGLAS, EDWARD. — Appointed clerk in the colonial secretary's office, Mauritius, September, 1856; promoted assistant colonial secretary for superintending the despatch branch, September, 1859; senior assistant secretary, April, 1866
DOUGLAS, FRANCIS. — Late assistant overseer of convicts at Bermuda; appointed superintendent of Victoria Gaol, Hong Kong, 1863.
DOUGLAS, SIR ROBERT PERCY, BART., has been inspector of militia and assistant adjutant-general to the forces; appointed 1864 lieutenant-governor at the Cape of Good Hope.
DOUGLAS, W. — 4th clerk in auditor-general's department, British Guiana; appointed 1862.
DOUGLAS, VERY REV. H. A. — Colonial chaplain, Cape Town, and Dean. Appointed 1855.

DOUGLAS, JAMES, C.B., 1859, K.C.B., 1863. — For many years employed under the Hudson's Bay Company; appointed the first governor of Vancouver's Island, 1851; and on the erection of British Columbia into a colony in 1858, was appointed to administer the combined governments.

DOUGLAS, JOHN. — Appointed clerk in the audit office at Mauritius, Feb. 1859; provisional secretary railway department, Jan. 1862; acting assistant secretary, May, 1865; and railway clerk, March, 1866.

DOUGLAS, SHOLTO JAMES. — Substitute to the procureur and advocate-general, Mauritius; appointed Nov. 1853. Is Queen's attorney in land court, and assistant Queen's advocate in vice-admiralty court; is allowed private practice as a barrister.
DOVETON, H. — Chief clerk in colonial secretary's office, St. Helena; appointed 1836. Was formerly in the company's service, for which he receives a pension of £90 per annum.
DOVETON, JOHN G. — Treasurer of St. Helena; appointed 1844. Was 18 years in the company's service, and receives a pension.
DOWNER, G. P. MAXWELL. — Lieutenant in Her Majesty's army, on half pay, since 1817; appointed health officer at Kingston and Port Royal, Jamaica, 1854.

DOYLE, WM. H. — Called to the bar at the Middle Temple, May, 1846; appointed assistant judge at Bahamas, 1858. Was registrar of the court of bankruptcy from 1847 to 1858; appointed chief justice of Bahamas, July, 1865.
DRAPER, W. H., C.B. — Chief justice of the queen's bench, Canada West.

DRENNING, A. H. — Appointed acting assistant district clerk at Savanne, Mauritius, 1855; clerk in colonial secretary's office, June, 1856; marine clerk, Port Louis, 1858; clerk to the district magistracy, Port Louis, February, 1862.
DREUIL, A. L. — Chief revenue officer, St. Lucia; appointed 1853. Is also government interpreter.
DREW, C. M. — Appointed provincial inspector of police at Kandy, Ceylon, Sept. 1859.
DRUMMOND, ALFRED. — Was clerk to the South Australian colonization committee, Sept. 1837; appointed a clerk in the colonial land and emigration commissioners' department, July, 1841.
DRY, SIR R., Knt. — Member of the legislative council Tasmania; was speaker of the house of assembly.
DRYSDALE, DUNCAN, A. V. — Private secretary to the lieutenant-governor, secretary to the executive committee, and clerk of the privy council, Tobago, Jan., 1864.

DRYSDALE, JAMES J. — Entered colonial secretary's office, St. Lucia, 1854; private secretary to lieut.-governor of Tobago, 1857; acting colonial secretary, Tobago, May 1859, to April 1861; secretary of the executive committee, and clerk to privy council, 1859; appointed colonial secretary previously, Aug. 1863; also registrar of Vice-Admiralty Court, Tobago; provost-marshal of Antigua, 1863.

DRYSDALE, JAMES V. — Was an assistant protector of slaves in the West Indies, 1830; appointed a stipendiary magistrate at St. Lucia, 1832; colonial secretary of that island, 1849; administered the government of St. Lucia from Feb. 1856, until May, 1857; appointed lieut.-governor of Tobago, April, 1857; held that appointment till 1864.
DUCKETT, EDWARD. — Cashier in the receiver-general's office, Nova Scotia.
DUKES, WILLIAM. — Clerk of the works, Mauritius, appointed Aug. 4, 1862.

DUMARESQ, J. D'AUVERGNE. — Clerk to principal officer in the customs-house, Jersey, June, 1848; promoted to be sub-comptroller at Ramsay, Isle of Man, March 10, 1852; comptroller at Cardigan, Aug. 30, 1853; at Ramsgate, Dec. 21, 1854; collector at Deal, Oct. 5, 1855; and appointed receiver-general and treasurer, Bahamas, 1863.
DUNCAN, JAMES. — Appointed director of the Botanical Gardens, Mauritius, May, 1849.
DUNCAN, JOHN CORBETT. — Was clerk in colo-

nial secretary's office at the Mauritius, May, 1855; superintendent of immigration, April, 1860; superintendent of distilleries, Oct. 1860; and appointed chief inspector of licenses, April, 1861.
DUNCAN, JAMES.—Clerk, surveyor-general's department, Mauritius, December, 1855; draughtsman, January, 1860; assistant government surveyor, December, 1862; government surveyor, September, 1865.

DUNCOMBE, A. J.—Was clerk to pilotage board, Bahamas, 1835; admitted a member of the bar, Bahamas, 1837; was judge-advocate; appointed judge of the supreme court, Turks Island, 1840; judge of court of ordinary, 1850; judge-advocate, Turks Island, 1860; member of executive and legislative councils, 1850; assistant justice of the general court, Bahamas, 1865.

DUNCOMBE, FREDERICK, M.D., M.R.C.S., Edinburgh.—Staff-surgeon, N. P. militia, Bahamas, 1844, until the disbandment of the same; acting physician to N. P. asylum and prison, 1844; officiating health officer, 1854; medical inspector of the colony, from July, 1856, to present time; member of house of assembly, 1852; executive councillor, April, 1860.

DUNDAS, GEORGE.—Formerly an officer of the rifle brigade; served in Bermuda, Nova Scotia, and the Mediterranean; retired from the army, 1844; represented the county of Linlithgowshire, N.B., in parliament, from 1847 to 1858; appointed lieut.-gov. of Prince Edward's Island, Jan. 1859.

DUNEDIN, 1st Bishop of, 1864. Rev. Henry Lascelles Jenner, graduate of Trinity Hall, Cambridge; late vicar of Preston next Wingham, near Sandwich. The diocese includes the provinces of Otago and Southland.
DUPONT, IVANOFF.—Appointed clerk to the district magistracy at Mauritius, 1852; district cashier, Grand Port, April, 1866.
DUPUY, C. H. E.—District magistrate, Seychelles, appointed June, 1857; district magistrate, Mauritius, March, 1863.
DURLACHER, ALFRED.—Resident magistrate, Toodyay district, Western Australia; was registrar-general and registrar of deeds, and clerk to the governor, in that colony, from 1851 to 1855.
DURNFORD, CAPT. C. C.—Appointed deputy assistant-commissary, Kandy, Ceylon, 1863; deputy assistant postmaster-general, Kandy, 1866.

DURRANT, OCTAVIUS.—Landing-waiter in customs' department, St. Vincent; first appointed in imperial customs in 1846; transferred to colonial customs, 1848; surveyor of shipping, 1855; is a notary public.

DUSMANI, SIR ANTONIO LERCOCHILO.—COUNT DUSMANI, K.C.M.G.—Entered the service Feb. 16, 1829, assistant in the senate office, Ionian Islands March 6, 1833, under-secretary of the senate; Nov. 3, 1834, secretary of the senate for the political department; Aug. 8, 1853, secretary of the senate for the general department; Dec. 26, 1834, general archivist; March 7, 1836, secretary of the general agricultural society, and directing secretary of its central committee, established by act of parliament; Nov. 17, 1841, secretary of the general commission on public instruction, until 1857, when the act of parliament establishing it was repealed; also member of the same from 1853; 1843, member of a commission to revise the judicial organization and procedure; 1855 and 1856, member of a commission to inquire into all the departments of the state; 1855, member of an extraordinary central sanitary commission during the prevalence of cholera in the Ionian Islands; 1859, member of a commission to examine the establishments and the

departments of the state. May 30, 1844, appointed Companion of the most distinguished order of Saints Michael and George; Dec. 1849, promoted knight commander; Nov. 23, 1852, appointed, under Her Majesty's warrant, officer of arms of the said order.

DVAL, H. J. B.—Superintendent of the water police department, West Australia, and officer to enforce passengers' act, appointed 1864; deputy-superintendent of convict establishment in Western Australia, 1859 to 1863; in the latter year appointed superintendent of water police at Fremantle.

DYETT, EDWARD BOWMAN.—Resident puisne justice and paid magistrate, Montserrat; appointed 1858. Is also coroner and escheator-general; was acting treasurer in 1852, and also from January 31st, 1853, to 10th May; acted as Queen's counsel in 1857, and several times before; first appointed coroner in 1851; is a member of the executive council; and is of the colonial bar.
DYETT, HENRY.—Postmaster, Montserrat; appointed May, 1852.

DYETT, RICHARD H.—Provost-marshal of Montserrat; appointed private secretary to the late Sir John Macgregor, Bart.; president of the Virgin Islands, in March, 1851; was private secretary to Lieutenant-Colonel Chads and Mr. Kortright, in the Virgin Islands; appointed coroner for the Virgin Islands in 1853; was also justice of the peace, and on several occasions acting provost-marshal there; is a notary public for the Virgin Islands; acted on several occasions as provost-marshal of Montserrat, and also as clerk of the council; held the office of coroner of Montserrat from August, 1862, to September, 1865, when he was appointed as provost-marshal; was private secretary to Mr. Robinson, and Capt. Shaw, R.N., in the government of Montserrat; is a non-elective member of the legislative assembly; and a justice of the peace for Montserrat.

DYKE, P. A.—Appointed an assistant to the colonial secretary's office, Ceylon, 1824; assistant to the collector at Jaffna, 1824; fiscal and sitting magistrate at Jaffna, 1825; provincial judge, Trincomalie, 1827; collector of Trincomalee and agent of government, Tamankadum; collector at Jaffna, 1829; government agent, northern province, 1833; was acting auditor-general in 1843, but resumed the duties of government agent, northern province, in Nov. 1843.
EAGLES, E. J.—Assistant receiver-general, Trinidad; appointed 1859. Was employed in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies from 1856 to 1858.

EBDEN, RICH., M.A.—Educated at Christ's college, Cambridge, graduated 1856, was 31st wrangler, Cambridge; appointed, after a competitive examination, a clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, Jan. 1858; private secretary to Sir F. Rogers, Oct. 1864; assistant senior, July, 1866.
EBERT, J. R.—Appointed medical sub-assistant to the government of Ceylon, 1833; second class, 1843; medical assistant, 1858.
EDMUNDS, W.—Surgeon-superintendent general infirmary, Robbin Island, Cape of Good Hope, 1863; was appointed acting surgeon, 1862.
EDWARDES, HON. G. W.—Governor of Labuan; from 1856 to 1861; was auditor-general of St. Helena from 1845 to 1856; formerly a captain in the life-guards.
EDWARDS, SIR B. (Kt. Creat. 1859).—Appointed chief justice and vice-chancellor and judge of the vice-admiralty court, Jamaica, May, 1856; called to the bar at the inner temple, 1825.
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

EDWARDS, C.F., M.R.C.S.—Was principal medical officer in the convict establishment at Bermuda, until the break up of that establishment in 1863; when he was appointed general sanitary inspector at Mauritius.
EDYE, W. M.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Peddie division, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1848. Was a clerk in the office of the master of the supreme court, 1827.

EGAN, CHARLES JAMES.—Graduated as B.A., Trinity College, Dublin, March, 1851; licentiate of midwifery, Dublin lying-in hospital, Feb. 14, 1857; member of the Royal College of Surgeons, London, June 12, 1857; appointed assistant colonial surgeon, British Kaffraria, Sept. 9, 1857.

ELDRIDGE, CHARLES MONROE.— Elected member of assembly for city of St. John's, Antigua, in Sept. 1852, and re-elected in 1859 and 1863; member of the administrative committee in April, 1861, and resigned in 1862; member of the executive council in March, 1863, and reappointed to the administrative committee at the same time; acted for a short time as treasurer; and appointed acting colonial secretary in April, 1865.
