ELIOT, GEORGE.— Appointed resident magistrate of the Wellington district, Western Australia, in 1840.

ELLIOT, K.C.B. (civil), created 1856.— Sir Charles Elliot, son of the late Right Hon. Hugh Elliot, governor of Madras; entered the navy in 1816; vice-admiral, 1862; served at the battle of Algiers; subsequently in the East Indies; was protector of slaves in British Guiana from 1831 to 1833; British plenipotentiary in China, from 1835 to 1841; appointed successively consul-general at Texas, 1842 to 1846; governor of Bermuda, 1847 to 1852; governor of Trinidad, 1853 to 1856; and governor of St. Helena, Aug. 1863.

ELLIOT, E.—Appointed a writer in the Ceylon civil service, 1863; acted as police magistrate, Dambool, until appointed to act as commissioner of requests, Jaffna, Sept. 1864; commissioner of request and police magistrate, Point Pedro, 1865; acting landing tide surveyor, Galle, 1866.

ELLIOT, GEORGE HENRY.—First lieutenant royal marines, entered the service 20 Oct. 1855; promoted 1st lieutenant, 14 April, 1859; appointed to command of marines at the Falkland Islands, 12 Aug. 1863. Receives from the colonial funds a single rate of pay, in addition to imperial pay.
ELLIOT, JOHN.—Surgeon to the gaol and asylum for the poor at Tobago, 1858; is also a member of the privy council.

ELLIOT, T. FREDERICK.—Assistant under secretary of state, colonial department; appointed clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, 5 July, 1825. In July, 1835, named by warrant from the crown to be secretary to commission of inquiry into the state of Canada, composed of the Earl of Gosford, Sir Charles Grey, and Sir George Gipps. In April, 1837, appointed to be the head of an office of emigration, then for the first time created, under the title of Agent General of Emigration. In 1840, this office being expanded into a Board of Advice and Management over Colonial Land and Emigration, made chairman of the same. In 1847 transferred to the colonial department, and appointed assistant under secretary of state for the colonies.
ELLIOTT, ANDREW C., barrister-at-law of Lincoln's Inn.—Appointed a county court judge of British Colombia, Jan. 1860; and assistant gold commissioner and stipendiary magistrate, June 1861.
ELLIOTT, THOS.—Clerk, customs, London, Nov. 1858; clerk, treasury, London, April, 1859; registrar, colonial secretary's office, Mauritius, July, 1860; and chief clerk, April, 1866.

ELLIS, F.—Called to the bar at the Middle Temple, 1838; appointed stipendiary magistrate at Turks Islands, 1851; and Queen's advocate for that colony; formerly held an appointment under the customs; is a member of the executive council.

ELWYN, THOMAS.—Formerly in the army; held the appointment of assistant gold commissioner and stipendiary magistrate of British Columbia, June, 1859 to April, 1863; appointed second in command of gold escort, June, 1863.

EMETT, W. T. L.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Stockenstrom division of Cape Colony; appointed 1859. Was magistrate's clerk at Stockenstrom, 1848 to 1852; clerk to the civil commissioner and magistrate, Fort Beaufort, Dec. 1853 to 1859.

ENGELBACH, ALFRED H.—Was clerk to the Canada commission, August, 1835; attaché to governor-general of Canada, Feb. 1837; clerk to agent-general for emigration, June, 1838; appointed clerk to colonial land and emigration commissioners, Jan. 1840; and accountant, April, 1849.

ENGLEHEART, GARDNER D.—Graduated at Christ Church, Oxford, double second, 1844; called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1849; private secretary to the Duke of Newcastle, June, 1859; and accompanied the Prince of Wales to America, 1860; returning to the colonial office in Nov. of that year; comptroller of the household to their Royal Highnesses Prince and Princess Christian, 1866.

ERSKINE, HON. D.—Was a major in the 21st regiment (fusiliers); having served 23 years in the 51st and 21st regiments in Australia, India, and the Mediterranean, four of which he was stipendiary magistrate in Tasmania; appointed colonial secretary of Natal; and a member of the executive and legislative councils of that colony, Jan. 1858.
ERSKINE, SR. V. W.—Appointed extra clerk, audit office, Natal, 1862; and fourth clerk in the office of the colonial secretary in that colony, Aug. 1863.

EVATT, H. A.—Was formerly in the army; served in the 90th light infantry, and Ceylon rifle regiment; whilst still on full pay acted as an assistant in the commissioner of roads department in Ceylon, from the 4th Feb. 1842 to 16th May, 1848, when he was appointed to act as commissioner of roads, and continued in that office until the 2nd Nov. 1849; when he resumed his duties as an assistant in that department. He sold out of the army in 1851, and was appointed assistant civil engineer, and commissioner of roads in April of that year. He acted as head of the department in August, 1854, and resumed his own duties in July, 1856. He acted as surveyor-general, with a seat in the legislative council, from May, 1858, to March 1859; and again from Nov. 1860 to Sept. 1861; appointed acting civil engineer and commissioner of roads in Oct. 1865.
ESNOUF, CHAS. VICTOR.—Appointed district and stipendiary magistrate at Black River, Mauritius, August, 1854; junior district magistrate at Port Louis, Aug. 1857; master of supreme court, July, 1866.

ESPENT, W. BANCROFT.—First-class clerk, in charge of the dispatch and correspondence branch of the colonial secretary's office, Jamaica; received his first appointment as clerk in the immigration office, from Sir Charles H. Durling, K.C.B., on the 19th May, 1861; transferred by Governor
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

Eyre to the executive committee office, on the 13th July, 1862, as 2nd clerk; appointed chief clerk, provisionally, by Sir Henry Storks, G.C.B., on the 10th April, 1866. On the change in the Constitution, was appointed to his present office, by Sir J. Peter Grant, K.C.B.; acted from 6th February, to the end of 1864, as secretary of police; is a lieutenant in the 2nd regiment of Jamaica militia, 18th July, 1863; and 1st lieutenant of the 2nd St. Catherine's Rifles, 30th August, 1864.
EVANS, C. — Appointed assistant surveyor, Western Australia, 1861.
EVANS, HENRY STEDMAN. — Appointed to be sub-treasurer of the Virgin Islands, 1860.
EVANS, J. B. — Commissioner of requests and police magistrate, Kandy, Ceylon, May, 1851.

EVELYN, G. J. — Served in commissariat from August, 1828, to Dec. 1836; appointed treasury clerk at St. Kitt's, Aug. 1850, sub-treasurer, Sept. 1857; acting treasurer, 1850, 1851, and again in 1862; appointed immigration agent, 1857.
EVELYN, W. T. — Police magistrate, St. Joseph's parish, Barbados.

EYRE, EDWARD JOHN. — Was for some years engaged in explorations in Australia; in 1840-41 commanded an exploring expedition under the auspices of the South Australian government, penetrating to nearly $29^{\circ}$ S. lat., then returning and crossing from the head of Spencer's Gulf to Western Australia, an account of which will be found in a work published by him entitled "Journals of Expeditions of Discovery into Central Australia, and overland from Adelaide to King George's Sound in 1840-41." This journey, in connection with Mr. Eyre's previous ones, completed his transit across the continent of New Holland from east to west, from Sydney to Swan River. For these tracts the Royal Geographical Society awarded him their gold medal in 1842. First appointment under the colonial office, Sept. 1841, as resident magistrate at Moorundee in South Australia, which office he held until Dec. 1846, when he was appointed lieut.-governor of New Ulster and New Munster in New Zealand; on the expiration of his term of service, returned to England, Sept. 1853; appointed lieut.-governor of St. Vincent, Oct. 1854, which he held until the end of March, 1861; administered the government-in-chief of the Leeward Islands from April, 1859 to April, 1860, during the absence of the governor; specially commissioned in Feb. 1862, to administer the general government of Jamaica and its dependencies, during the absence of the governor, Sir Charles Darling, K.C.B.; appointed governor, April 1864. In Oct. 1865, put down an insurrection of the negroes, which was ushered in by the savage butchering of a number of magistrates and other gentlemen at St. Thomas's-in-the-East. Received address of thanks from the legislature, the bishop and clergy, from dissenting ministers, planters, and others, and from the ladies and women of Jamaica. The legislative council, in their addresses, recorded "grateful thanks for the energy, firmness, and wisdom with which your excellency has carried the island through this momentous crisis." The address from the assembly was in equally flattering terms. In Nov. 1865, Mr. Eyre induced the legislative bodies to abrogate their existing functions. The form of government which was afterwards instituted, the appointment of Sir H. Storks, and the reasons which induced Her Majesty's government to recal Governor Eyre, are to be found under the heading of Jamaica. Mr. Eyre returned in Aug. 1866.
FABER, J. H. — Crown surveyor of Honduras;

appointed postmaster, 1845; assistant crown surveyor, 1847; judge of summary court, 1848; magistrate, northern district (Indian war), 1848; and north-western, 1849; crown surveyor, 1853; justice of the peace and magistrate of the southern district, 1860; commissioner of supreme court, 1863.
FACEY, JAMES M. — Chief clerk in the office of the registrar in chancery, Jamaica, appointed 13th March, 1848.
FADELLE, JOSEPH. — Provost-marshal, Dominica; appointed 1849.
FAIRBANKS, S. P. — Commissioner of crown lands, Nova Scotia.
FAIRFIELD, E. D. — Educated at Harrow; appointed, after a competitive examination, clerk in colonial office, Nov. 1866.
FALCONER, WILLIAM ROSE. — Director of public works, and inspector of telegraphs, Tasmania, Jan. 1, 1859.

FALKLAND, 9th VISCOUNT (Scotland, Creat. 1620); BARON HUNSDON, 1832 (United Kingdom), by which title he holds his seat in the House of Lords; Privy Councillor, 1837; G.C.H., 1831. LUCIUS BENTINCK CARY. — Succeeded his father in 1809; appointed a lord of the bedchamber, Dec. 1830; elected a representative peer for Scotland, 1831; was governor of Nova Scotia from 1840 to 1846; captain of the yeomen of the guard from 1846 to 1848; governor of Bombay from Feb. 1848 to Dec. 1853.

FARNUM, CHARLES. — Appointed in 1846; assistant-storekeeper, Trinidad, Sept. 1850; acting deputy assistant commissary-general, 1856 to 1859; assistant-storekeeper, 1860; is fourth clerk in the receiver-general's office, to which post he was appointed in Sept. 1863.

FARQUHARSON, C. A. F. — Assistant district clerk, Mauritius, March, 1852; inspector of distilleries, March, 1854; clerk in colonial secretary's office, Dec. 1855; district clerk at Seychelles, Aug. 1856; district clerk, Pamplemousses, Mauritius, 1858; stipendiary magistrate, Dec. 1864.
FARRINGTON, ISAAC. — Treasurer of the Virgin Islands, appointed Feb. 1866; acting chief justice since April, 1866. Is lieutenant-colonel of militia.
FAURE, A., D.D. — First minister, Dutch reformed church, Cape Town; appointed 1839; was second minister from 1822.
FERENAN, JOHN. — Warehouse-keeper, Hobart Town, Oct. 14, 1842.
FERGUSON, JOHN. — Colonial surgeon, Western Australia; appointed 1846.
FERGUSON, W. — Appointed assistant surveyor, Ceylon, Nov. 1851; in charge of gravel roads, Sept. 1858.

FICHAT, J. — Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of George division of Cape of Good Hope; appointed Dec. 1864; was resident magistrate at Bathurst, June 1855; of Knysna, April 1856, to 1862; of Bathurst, 1862 to 1864.
FINLAISON, C. — Clerk in the customs' department, British Colombia; appointed June, 1860.
FINNEMORE, R. J. — Second clerk in the surveyor-general's office, Natal, 1859.
FINNEMORE, T. — Second clerk in the office of treasurer, Natal, Aug. 1862.

FINNIS, JOHN HENRY. — Was clerk in the police office at Mauritius, Oct. 1840; assistant in the registration department, Aug. 1842; first assistant, Aug. 1848; and appointed receiver of registration dues and conservator of mortgages in that colony, June, 1854.
FIRMIN, J. N. — Queen's advocate, clerk of the peace, and registrar of the supreme and vice-
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
admiralty court of St. Helena; appointed 1839; acting chief justice, 1863. Was 23 years in the East India Company's service.
FIRTH, HENRY A.—Second sub-agent of immigration, British Guiana.
FISCHER, R. L.—Chief clerk, deeds registry, Cape of Good Hope, 1863, has been eighteen years in the colonial service.
FISHER, F. C.—Writer, Ceylon civil service, 1866.

FISHER, SIR JAMES HURTLE.—Appointed first resident commissioner of public lands in South Australia, and arrived in that colony in H.M.S. 'Buffalo,' 1836; member of first executive council; mayor of Adelaide five times; chairman of bench of magistrates, elected 1853; speaker of legislative council prior to change in constitution; president of legislative council under new constitution, 1856; knighted, letters patent, dated June 6, 1860.
FISHER, JOE T.—General manager of railway, Mauritius, Nov. 1864.
FITT, H. A.—Registrar-general, Trinidad; appointed 1860.
FITENI, FRANCIS, LL.D.—Appointed one of Her Majesty's judges, Malta, Sept. 1861.
FITZGERALD, HORACE.—Educated at the Royal Colleges of Caen and Amiens, in Paris, and at Trinity College, Dublin. Is a member of the Irish bar. Appointed a puisne judge of Trinidad in 1862.

FITZGERALD, JOHN PATRICK, M.D.—Member of the R. C. S., London; was appointed assistant colonial surgeon and coroner for Wellington, New Zealand, 1840; colonial surgeon, and magistrate of the province of Wellington, in that colony, 1850; appointed superintendent of native hospitals in British Kaffraria, 1856.

FITZPATRICK, JAMES COLEMAN.—Is a graduate of Dublin University. In 1844, was called to the Irish bar; in 1847, was appointed judicial assessor to the sovereigns and chiefs in the countries adjacent to Her Majesty's forts and settlements of the Gold Coast; in 1853, was appointed chief justice of Her Majesty's settlements on the Gold Coast; in 1857, was called to the English bar, special gratiâ, at Lincoln's Inn; in 1861, was appointed judge of the supreme court of British Kaffraria.

FITZROY, GEORGE HENRY.—Was private secretary to the governor-in-chief of the Leeward Islands from Jan. 1842, till Sept. 1845; was private secretary to the governor-general of the Australian colonies from Sept. 1845 till Jan. 1855; was assistant secretary to the convict prison board in 1857; was attached to the Earl of Elgin's special mission to China from April 17, 1857, till May, 1859; was appointed by the Emperor of China commissioner of His Imperial Majesty's customs at Canton, July 1, 1859.

FLANDERKA, J. L.—Appointed a writer in the civil service of Ceylon, 1846; assistant government agent, Mullettoe, 1847; acting assistant government agent, Jaffna, 1852; held the same office at Nuwerakalawiya, 1854; also at Manaar in 1855; assistant government agent, &c. at Anooradhapoora, 1856. Acted as government agent, fiscal and collector of customs, northern province, from Jan. to Nov. 1861, when he resumed his own duties.
FLEMING, C. W.—Police magistrate, St. George's Point, Barbados.

FLEMING, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1856.)—HON. SIR VALENTINE FLEMING.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin, where he graduated B.A. 1834, and took honours; was called to the bar at Gray's Inn, 1838; appointed commissioner of the insolvent
court for Hobart Town, 1841; solicitor-general of Tasmania, 1844; attorney-general, Jan. 1848; chief justice of the supreme court there, Aug. 1854; and knighted by patent, 1856.
FOLKARD, A. C.—Appointed assistant civil engineer and commissioner of roads, Ceylon, Dec. 1856.

FORD, EDWARD S.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of the Clanwilliam division of Cape Colony; appointed Dec. 1858; was magistrates' clerk at Richmond, 1848 to 1854; clerk to the civil commissioner, Graaff Reinet, Sept. 1854 to 1859.
FORD, H. A.—Appointed deputy postmaster at Kandy, Ceylon, 1847; acting police magistrate, Calpentyn, 1862. Retired on pension, 1866.
FORSTER, JOHN.—Inspector of police, Tasmania, July, 1, 1857.

FORSTER, WILLIAM EDWARD, M.P.—A magistrate and deputy lieutenant for the West Riding of York; unsuccessfully contested Leeds, April, 1859; first elected for Bradford, Feb. 1861; appointed under secretary of state for the colonies, Nov., 1865.

FORTESCUE, CHICHESTER, S., The Rt. Hon., M.P.—Educated at Christchurch, Oxford, where he was first class in classics in 1844, and obtained the chancellor's prize for the English essay in 1846; was a lord of the Treasury from Jan. 1854 to April, 1855; under secretary of state for the colonies, May 1857, to Feb. 1858, and re-appointed June 1859. Has been M.P. for Co. Louth since 1847. Privy counsellor, 1864; chief secretary for Ireland, Nov. 1865.

FORTH, FREDK.—Was formerly a captain in the royal North British fusiliers; appointed visiting magistrate, Van Diemen's Land, 1833; served in that colony as A.D.C. to the governor; officer in command of mounted police; police magistrate, deputy chairman of quarter sessions, commissioner of court of requests; and appointed in 1841 director-general of roads, which office he held until 1848, when he returned to England; was appointed president of the Turks and Caicos Islands from 1848 to 1854; treasurer of Hong Kong, 1857; a member of the legislative council, and acting colonial secretary from Aug. to Nov. 1858.

FOX, CHARLES JAMES.—Clerk, court of appeal, Mauritius, May, 1846, assistant district clerk, Port Louis, Mauritius, 1852; district clerk at Seychelles, Oct. 1858; appointed chief clerk, police court, Mauritius, Aug. 1861.
FOX, REV. GEORGE, A.M.—Principal of Queen's college grammar school, British Guiana.
FRANCIS, H.—Second revenue officer, Tobago, appointed 1860.

FRANKLYN, WILLIAM HALES.—Appointed a magistrate and government agent at Nainaimo, Vancouver's Island, June, 1860; holds an extra certificate of competency as commander in the mercantile marine; commanded the steam transport 'Tonning,' the leading ship in the first division of transports in the expeditionary force landed in the Crimea, Sept. 1855; and during the war with Russia was honourably mentioned by Lord Clyde and other distinguished officers; received a vote of thanks and a silver salver from the North of Europe Steam Navigation Company for his exertions in saving life during the hurricane at Balaclava, Nov. 1855.

FRANKS, CHARLES W.—Educated at Eton, and Christchurch, Oxford, where he took his degree in 1847; was one of the assistant-secretaries on the staff of the commissioners for the international exhibition of 1862; is a fellow of the Royal
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

Geographical Society, and a corresponding member of the Royal Zoological Society. Has edited (in conjunction with Tom Taylor) a catalogue of the works of Sir Joshua Reynolds. Appointed treasurer of British Columbia, April, 1864.
FRASER, J. D.—Stipendiary magistrate of British Guiana; appointed 1848.

FRASER, ROSE DEMPSTER.—Comptroller of customs and navigation laws, and registrar of shipping, Barbados; entered the imperial customs, as landing-waiter and searcher at Bermuda, 1838; appointed senior searcher, 1839; nominated 8th April 1845, by warrant from governor of Bermuda (the late Sir W. Reid), to survey and report on all public moneys in the colonial treasury; nominated acting landing surveyor, 1848; appointed same year landing waiter and searcher at Bridgetown, Barbadoes; appointed landing waiter and searcher at Kingston, Jamaica, 1850; which appointment was cancelled in the year following, at his request, and on nomination as acting landing surveyor, Barbados; appointed first-class landing waiter, colonial customs, Barbados, 1853; landing surveyor, 1855; and comptroller, &c., 1857.

FREDERICTON, 1st BISHOP OF. Founded 1845.—RIGHT REV. JOHN MEDLEY, D.D.—Was educated at Wadham College, Oxford, where he was 2nd class in classics, 1826, graduated M.A. 1830, and D.D. 1845; was appointed vicar of St. Thomas's, Exeter, in 1838; prebendary of Exeter cathedral in 1842; and consecrated 1st Bishop of this see in 1845. Is author of a tract on episcopacy, an address to Sunday-school teachers, and has published several sermons. The diocese includes the province of New Brunswick, and was erected into a bishop's see in 1845.
FREEMAN, J. D.—Emigration agent for Mauritius, at Bombay; appointed July, 1856.

FREEMAN, WILLIAM GARDNER.—Entered the colonial service in 1835; appointed collector of customs at Jamaica, 1854; is a member of the privy council, late member of the legislative council. Is principal comptroller of customs and navigation laws; registrar of shipping and justice of the peace for the parish of Kingston and Port Royal, in the island of Jamaica.
FRENCH, A. LYNCH.—Member of legislative council, St. Christopher, appointed 1851; acting superintendent of police, and police magistrate for rural districts, July, 1862, till July, 1863.
FRENCH, JOHN.—Clerk to the government secretary, British Guiana; appointed 1830; retired 1863.
FULLER, FREDERIC W.—Entered the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, 28th April, 1862, after competitive examination.
FULLER, T. W.—Stipendiary magistrate for the western district, county of St. Patrick, Trinidad; appointed 1855.
FURLONGE, WM. D.—Clerk in the marshal's office, Antigua, appointed in 1864; acting landing-waiter, treasury department, 1852; clerk in the colonial secretary's office, 1862.

FYERS, R.E., CAPT. A. B.—Entered Royal engineers, Dec. 1847; promoted to captain, Aug. 1856; served in Mauritius from 1849 until 1858, during the greater part of which time he was "government meteorological observer;" was stationed at the Curragh camp from 1858 until 1861, and at Malta from 1861 until 1865; appointed surveyor-general of Ceylon 26 April, 1866. Is on the seconded list.
FYNN, W. CHRISTIAN.—Appointed clerk of the courts, Cape Coast Castle, in 1858.
GABOUREL, AUSTIN W.—Appointed clerk in the colonial secretary's office, Honduras, July, 1865.
GAGGIN, J. D.—Was an officer in the Royal Cork artillery militia; appointed stipendiary magistrate of Douglas, in the colony of British Columbia, 1859.
GAIRDNER, GORDON.—Appointed clerk in the office of secretary of state for the colonies, Jan. 1824; promoted chief clerk, Jan. 1860.
GALLWEY, M. H.—Barrister-at-law, Irish bar; appointed attorney-general, Natal, 1857; member of council.
GALT, A. T.—Finance minister, Canada. One of the commissioners appointed to confer on the subject of the defence of Canada with the British ministry.
GANACHAUD, E.—Secretary to the procureur de la reine, Mauritius, Feb. 1842; appointed clerk to the district magistracy, March, 1854.
GARCIA, GEORGE.—Solicitor-general, Trinidad; appointed 1849.

GARCIA, MAURICE.—Civil commissioner and magistrate, Riversdale, Cape of Good Hope, Dec. 1863. Entered the colonial service, 1828. Appointed clerk of the peace, George, 1856; civil commissioner and magistrate, Richmond, 1862.
GARLAND, S. S.—Appointed assistant receiver-general, Turks Islands, March, 1851. Is also assistant police magistrate and coroner; was first appointed to the customs in 1840.
GAUDET, J. M.—Appointed colonial auditor at the Gambia, Aug. 1856; is assistant commissary-general.
GAUTIER, LEONIDAS.—District magistrate, Mauritius, appointed May, 1859.

GEFFROY, NUMA.—Clerk to the advocate general, Mauritius, 1832; fourth judge of the court of assizes, 1833; assistant judge of the court of first instance, 1834; and district magistrate, at Black River, Mauritius, 1852.
GERRARD, JOHN.—Appointed 2nd clerk in the registrar-general's office, Hong Kong, 1860; promoted to 1st clerk 1863.
GERAUDFLE, JEAN D.—Appointed clerk in surveyor-general's office, Mauritius, Oct. 1855; promoted 1861.

GIBBS, GEORGE MEADE, M.A.—Archdeacon of St. Kitt's, Nevis, &c.; educated at Trinity College, Dublin; obtained at entrance recommendation for honours and Hebrew prize; graduated 1847; ordained by the bishop of Lichfield, 1848, for Trinity parish, Derby; appointed to curacy of St. Mary's, Southwark, 1851; Wonston, Hants, 1855; archdeacon of St. Kitt's, and rector of St. George's, Basseterre, by the Duke of Newcastle, on the nomination of the archbishop of Canterbury, Dec. 1861. Member of Her Majesty's executive and legislative councils, St. Kitt's, 1862.
GIBLIN, WM.—Registrar of deeds, Tasmania, Nov. 23, 1860.
GIBRALTAR, 2nd BISHOP OF.—RIGHT REV. WALTER JOHN TROWER, D.D.—Appointed 1863; was bishop of Glasgow and Galloway. The see was founded in 1842.
GIBSON, J. W.—Writer, Ceylon civil service, 1865; acting police magistrate, Harrispastoo, 1866.

GIBSON, T. L.—Held the office of deputy to the Queen's advocate at Kandy, Ceylon, 1842; appointed police magistrate, Bentotte, 1844; and commissioner of requests at that place, 1845; district judge, Badulla, 1846; acted as district judge, Kurnegalle, 1848, in which appointment he was confirmed Feb. 1850; assistant government agent, &c., Ratnapoora, 1854; acting assistant agent, Matura, Aug. 1854; acting postmaster-
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

general, 1855; acting district judge of Galle, 1857; acting government agent, &c., eastern province, 1859; district judge, Kurnegalle, 1861; acted as district judge, Jaffna, March, 1861, in which appointment he was confirmed in 1862; district judge, Kandy, 1865.

GIBSON, W. C.—Appointed to the Ceylon civil service, Sept. 1832; attached to the colonial secretary's office, 1833; also in that year assistant collector of customs, Negombo, and assistant government agent, Colombo; was appointed in 1838 commissioner of loan board, auditor of the supreme court, and chairman of the savings' bank; acting district judge of Colombo, Nov. 1839; assistant colonial secretary, &c., 1840; acting government agent, western province, 1841; resumed duties as assistant colonial secretary, 1843; acting auditor-general, 1846; resumed duties as assistant colonial secretary, 1847; acting government agent, Western province, same year; resumed duties as assistant-colonial secretary, 1848; acting auditor-general, 1849; acting colonial secretary, 1850; auditor-general, 1851; acting colonial secretary, 1855; resumed duties as auditor-general; acting colonial secretary, 1859, in which office he was confirmed, Aug. 1860.

GIBSON, W. F.—Appointed in 1842 junior clerk in the office of Public Works, Ireland; promoted senior clerk in 1848, and chief clerk in 1852. His office having been abolished in 1857, he was placed on the redundant list, 1862; served in 1862 under the census commission for England, and subsequently was transferred to the treasury, from which in 1863 he was appointed registrar and accountant to the Royal Mint at Sydney, New South Wales.

GIDDY, T. H.—Acting clerk to resident magistrate, Queen's Town, Cape Colony, July, 1854; temporary clerk in civil commissioner's office, Queen's Town, April, 1855; clerk to resident magistrate, King William's Town, British Kaffraria, 1855; acting clerk of the peace, Nov. 1857; master of the supreme court, and registrar of deeds, 1862.
GILBERT, ALEX. J. M.—Assistant receiver-general and commissioner of customs at Bermuda; appointed 1854. Was revenue officer at Ireland island, 1848 to 1852, and at Hamilton from 1852 to 1854.
GILBERT, J. T.—Was solicitor-general of British Guiana; appointed 1856; retired 1863.
GILL, CHRISTOPHER.—Appointed police magistrate of Bridgetown, Barbados, 1834.
GILL, T.—Was appointed medical sub-assistant, Ceylon, 1829; second class, 1842; first class, 1847; medical assistant, 1858.
GILL, WM. HENRY.—Clerk, first class, audit office, Tasmania, July 1, 1853.
GILLESPIE, JOHN W.—Late assistant surgeon H. M. 5th Fusileers; appointed surgeon superintendent of quarantine at Flat Island, Mauritius, Oct. 1, 1862.

GILLMAN, H. W.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin, where as undergraduate he obtained science honours (chiefly 1st class), seven times, with simultaneous classical honours (2nd class) five times; appointed an exhibitioner, 1852; graduated gold medallist and moderator in mathematics and physics, 1853; and obtained Bishop Law's mathematical prize, 1854.—Was appointed a writer to the Ceylon government, Sept. 1856; assistant government agent, Jaffna, 1858; assistant government agent, Colombo, 1862; acting landing surveyor; acting deputy collector of customs, Colombo, same year; acting commissioner of re-
quests, Colombo, 1863; confirmed in that appointment May, 1865.
GILSON, A. A.—Second clerk, colonial secretary's office, Natal.

GLADSTONE, Privy Councillor (Gt. Brit.). Appointed 1841.—RIGHT HON. WILLIAM EWART GLADSTONE, M.P.—Was educated at Eton and at Christ Church, Oxford, where he attained a double first class in 1831; graduated M.A. 1834; and received the honorary degree of D.C.L. in 1848; was a lord of the treasury in Dec. 1834; under-secretary for the colonies from Jan. to April, 1835; vice-president of the board of trade, and master of the mint, from Sept. 1841 to May, 1843, when he became president of the board of trade, retaining the office of master of the mint; resigned both Feb. 1845; was secretary of state for the colonies from Dec. 1845 to July, 1846; chancellor of the exchequer from Jan. 1853 to Feb. 1855; lord high commissioner extraordinary to the Ionian Islands, Nov. 1858; reappointed chancellor of the exchequer, June, 1859; is author of "The State in its relations with the Church," "Church Principles considered in their Results," and other works; was M.P. for Newark from 1832 to Jan. 1846, and has sat for the university of Oxford since 1847; defeated 1865, and returned for South Lancashire.

GLEN, THOMAS.—Receiver-general, Newfoundland; appointed 1855. Is a member of the executive council and house of assembly; came in under responsible government in 1855, and went out of office by change of ministry in 1861.

GLENIE, REV. S. O.. M.A., F.L.S.—Classical prizeman, 1830, and elected foundation scholar, 1830, of St. Peter's College, Cambridge; appointed colonial chaplain of St. Paul's, Colombo, Oct. 30, 1834; colonial chaplain, Trincomalie, June 1, 1840; colonial chaplain, Kandy, May 1, 1843; returned, at his own request, to Trincomalee, Feb. 1, 1844; acting colonial chaplain, Kandy, 1848; acting senior colonial chaplain, Colombo, 1848; resumed duties at Trincomalee, Oct. 1, 1851.
GLOVER, Commander, R.N.—Lieut.-Governor of Lagos, 1863; acting governor, owing to death of Mr. Freeman; appointed to be colonial secretary at Lagos, April, 1864.

GOOD, CHARLES, graduated at Exeter College, Oxford, B.A., 1856.—Entered colonial service as chief clerk in office of colonial secretary of British Columbia, April, 1859; acted as private secretary to governor Douglas during 1859, 1860, 1861; was appointed clerk to the legislative council 19th June, 1864, the duties of which office he performs in conjunction with those of chief clerk in the colonial secretary's department.
GOODLAKE, EDW. WALLACE.—Called to the bar at the Inner Temple, Nov. 1855; was special pleader, Oxford circuit; appointed chief magistrate and member of council, Falkland Islands, 1861.
GORDON, ARCHIBALD, M.D., C.B.—Appointed chief medical officer at Mauritius, July, 1860; is a deputy inspector-general of Her Majesty's hospitals.

GORDON, HON. ARTHUR, C.M.G.—Was assistant private secretary to the Earl of Aberdeen when secretary of state for foreign affairs, between September 1841 and July 1846; and also when first lord of the treasury. Was M.P. for Beverley from July 29, 1854, till March, 1857; accompanied Mr. Gladstone on his special mission to the Ionian islands, as lord-high-commissioner extraordinary, in November, 1858; was appointed a companion of the order of St. Michael and St. George in 1859, captain commandant of the 1st
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
Aberdeenshire rifle volunteers, Feb. 25, 1860, and appointed lieut.-governor of New Brunswick, Oct. 1861.
GORDON, J. G.—Provost-marshal, and marshal in chancery and ordinary, Virgin Islands; appointed March, 1845.
GORDON, ROBERT.—Auditor-general, Dominica; appointed 1860. Was previously a revenue officer in the treasury department of that island.
GORDON, ROBERT.—Coroner, Tobago; is also a member of the privy and legislative councils of that island.

GORDON, THOMAS ROBERT.—Sub-comptroller of customs and rum duties of British Guiana; performs also the duties of sub-book-keeper in the county of Burbice; is also sub-administrator for that county; was appointed sub-registrar of population and property in 1856; held the office of sub-commissary of taxation from 1858 to 1863; clerk to the auditor-general of the colony from 1863 to 1865, during which period he held the office of secretary to the board of prisons.

GORE, AUGUSTUS FREDERICK.—Acting private secretary to Sir A. Oliphant, C.B., chief justice of Ceylon, 1850; private secretary to Christopher Temple, second puisne judge of Ceylon, 1851; private secretary to P. E. Wodehouse, superintendent of British Honduras, August, 1851; acting colonial secretary of British Honduras, 1852, and returning officer (sheriff) of the Bay Islands, 1853; private secretary to Governor Wodehouse, C.B., of British Guiana, 1854; assistant government secretary of the colony in 1854 and 1857; acting stipendiary magistrate in 1856; acting circuit stipendiary magistrate in 1854 and 1857; appointed assistant government secretary of the colony in 1857; acted as government secretary in June, 1865; appointed an inspector of prisons, July, 1865. Militia: captain in 1856; present rank, lieut.-colonel; aide-de-camp to Governor Hincks.

GORMAN, W. J.—Captain, late of the Ceylon Rifles; appointed assistant commissary-general, Ceylon, 1858; acting deputy commissary-general, 1863, resumed duties 1864; acting deputy commissioner general, 1866.

GOSSETT, WM. DRISCOLL.—Entered the royal engineers, June, 1840; promoted to be captain, Nov. 1850; appointed surveyor-general of Ceylon, Sept. 1855; and treasurer of British Columbia, Nov. 1858; resigned 1863.

GOULBURN, 1st BISHOP OF.—RIGHT REV. MESAC THOMAS, D.D.—Appointed 1863; was secretary to the colonial and continental church society. Goulburn formed part of the diocese of New South Wales until its separation in 1863.
GOULD, CHARLES.—Geological surveyor, Tasmania, July 18, 1859.
GOYDER, G. WOODROFFE.—Chief clerk, land office, South Australia, 1833; deputy surveyor general, 1855; surveyor general, 1861; also inspector of mines and valuer of runs.

GRAHAM, CYRIL CLERKE.—Was honorarily attached to Lord Dufferin, British commissioner in Syria, from September 10, 1860, till June, 1861; private secretary to the Earl of Carnarvon, secretary of state for the colonies, 6th July, 1866.
GRAHAM, JOHN.—Accountant Clerk in treasury office, British Columbia, appointed April, 1859.
GRAHAM, R.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Albany division, Cape Colony; appointed Sept. 1852.
GRAHAM, THOS.—Crown commissioner, colonial surveyor and civil engineer, Turks Islands; appointed 1853; appointed treasurer of Honduras,

1863; is ex officio member of the executive council; is commandant of the Belize volunteers; acting colonial secretary, June, 1866, was performing the duties of that office from October, 1863, to March, 1864.

GRAHAM'S-TOWN (CAPE OF GOOD HOPE), 2nd BISHOP OF. Founded 1853. RIGHT REV. H. CUTTERELL, D.D.—Educated at St. John's Coll. Cambridge, of which he was elected a fellow; was Bell's scholar, senior wrangler, first Smith's prize-man, and first class in classics; ordained deacon 1835, priest 1836; appointed chaplain to the East India Company at Madras, 1836; principal of Brighton College, 1851; consecrated to this see 1856; author of the "Seven Ages of the Church." This diocese (originally part of the see of Cape Town) comprises the eastern districts of the Colony of the Cape of Good Hope, together with the districts south of the Orange, and east of the Ongar Rivers, and also the territory of British Kaffraria.

GRANT, JAMES MEYER.—Entered the colonial service as crown and colony surveyor, St. Vincent, 1848; appointed clerk in the treasury of that island, 1849, and treasurer, 1854; was a member of the legislative council, 1854, which he resigned at the instance of the government to serve in the assembly; was speaker of the assembly, 1859-60; was an executive councillor, 1856, and assistant justice of the supreme court of judicature, 1860; appointed colonial secretary and administrator of the government of St. Lucia, 1862.
GRANT, JOHN.—Keeper of the common gaol, Tobago.

GRANT, JOHN GLASGOW.—Master in chancery, commissioner of probates, and secretary and actuary of savings' bank, Barbados. Was for fifteen years a member of the local legislature and the several boards and standing committees connected therewith.

GRANT, SIR JOHN PETER, K.C.B. (Creat. 1862.)—Educated at Eton and Haileybury; entered the Bengal civil service, 1828; appointed secretary to the Indian law commission, 1837; secretary to the government of Bengal, 1848; secretary to the governor-general of India in council (home department), 1852; subsequently transferred to the foreign department; a member of the council of India, May, 1854; was lieut.-governor of Bengal from May 1859 to 1862; Governor of Jamaica, June, 1866.
GRAVES, T. N.—June, 1854, clerk, colonial secretary's office, Mauritius; Nov. 1, 1858, clerk, in treasury in that island; May, 1864, joint district clerk.
GRAY, F.—Appointed commissioner of loan board, Ceylon, 1858.
GRAY, JOHN.—Was appointed special police magistrate at St. John's, Antigua, 1843; master in chancery, 1844; puisne judge and member of the council of that island, 1856.
GRAY, S. BROWNLOW.—Called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1847; has since practised at the Bermuda bar; appointed attorney-general of the colony, June, 1861. Is also advocate of admiralty.

GRECH, VINCENZO.—Entered the colonial service in Malta, July, 1812; was clerk in the grain department; chief clerk in the customs department; commissary of the Monte; and appointed collector of customs, Aug. 1861; is ex-officio member of council.
GREENE, HENRY AYLMER.—Appointed a clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, 1845; transferred to the war department as assistant clerk, 1854; promoted senior (2nd class), 1860.
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
GREEN, H.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate, Colesberg, Cape of Good Hope, appointed 1860; was British resident, Orange river Sovereignty, from 1852 to 1854.
GREENSLADE, H. T.—District-commissary of taxation, British Guiana.
GREGORY, F.—Appointed an assistant surveyor in Western Australia, 1849. Has superintended several successful exploring expeditions; but is no longer in the service of that colony.
GREIG, K.C.M.G. (Creat. 1839.)—SIR HECTOR GREIG.—Formerly chief secretary at Malta; filled the office of superintendent of quarantine for some years previously.
GRESHAM, JOHN FOSTER.—Appointed to be chief justice of Grenada, 1863.

GREY, 3rd EARL, (Creat. 1806); VISCt. HOWICK, 1801; BARON GREY, 1806 (Utd. Kgdms.); BART., 1746 (Gt. Brit.); Privy Councillor, 1835.—HENRY GEORGE GREY.—Educated at Trinity College, Cambridge (M.A. 1823); was under-secretary of state for the colonies from 1830 to 1833; under-secretary for the home department from Jan. till July, 1834; secretary at war from April, 1835 to Sept. 1839; secretary of state for the colonies from July, 1846 to Feb. 1852; appointed lord-lieut. of Northumberland, 1847; was M.P. for Winchelsea from 1826 to 1830, for Higham Ferrars in 1831, for North Northumberland from 1831 to 1841, and for Sunderland from Sept. 1841 till July, 1845.

GREY, SIR GEORGE, K.C.B. (Civil.) 1848.—Entered the army as ensign in the 83rd regiment, 18; promoted to be lieut., 1833; and captain, 1839; left England in 1837 with instructions from government to explore the north-west part of Australia, the account of which will be found in a work published by him, entitled "Journals of Two Expeditions of Discovery in North-west and Western Australia, during 1837-8-9;" was for some time resident magistrate at Albany, West Australia; appointed governor of South Australia, Dec. 1840; governor of New Zealand, 1846; governor of the Cape of Good Hope, 1854; and specially reappointed governor of New Zealand, June, 1861; is author of "Polynesian Mythology."

GREY, 2nd BART. (Utd. Kgdms. Creat. 1814); Privy Councillor, 1839; G.C.B. (Civil) 1849.—RIGHT HON. SIR GEORGE GREY.—Educated at Oriel College, Oxford, where he was 1st class in classics, 1821; and graduated M.A. 1824; was called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1826; was under-secretary for the colonies from July to Nov. 1834, and from April, 1835 till 1839; was judge-advocate-general from 1839 to June, 1841; chancellor of the duchy of Lancaster from June to Sept. 1841; secretary of state for the home department from July, 1846, to Feb. 1852; secretary of state for the colonies from June, 1854, to Feb. 1855; again home secretary from March, 1855, to Feb. 1858; reappointed chancellor of the duchy of Lancaster, June, 1859; and again secretary of state for the home department, 1861; was M.P. for Devonport from 1832 to 1847; for Northumberland North from 1847 to 1852, and has sat for Morpeth since January, 1853.

GRIFFIN, WM. HY.—Appointed secretary to the general post office, Canada, on the transfer of the department to the colonial authorities in 1851; appointed deputy postmaster general of Canada, June, 1857.
GRIFFITH, C. D.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Queen's Town, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1859. Was inspector of the armed mounted police, Nov. 1852.
GRIFFITH, WM. B.—Was a member of the assembly of St. Vincent, 1853-54; appointed captain

of the St. George's troop of yeomanry cavalry, Barbados, on its organization in 1859 by governor Walker; elected a member of assembly for the parish of St. George in 1861; appointed auditor-general of Barbados, May 5, 1863.
GRIFFITHS, E. R.—Barrister-at-law, Temple; appointed stipendiary magistrate, Falkland Islands, May, 1863.
GRIFFITHS, W. E. H.—Appointed a clerk in the colonial secretary's office at Mauritius, Mar. 1860; joint clerk at Plaines Wilhems, April, 1861, and second clerk in the registrar's office, Aug. 1862.
GRIMBLE, LIEUT. GEORGE S.—6th Royal regiment. Appointed October, 1866, aide-de-camp to Sir John P. Grant, K.C.B., Governor of Jamaica.

GRINLINTON, J. J.—Was nine years on the ordnance survey of England; gazetted ensign in the 65th regiment; served in the Crimean campaign of 1865-6, as lieutenant in the 4th King's Own regiment, and assistant engineer during the siege of Sebastopol, and was engaged making a military survey of the Allied positions; was mentioned in the dispatches—received the 5th class of the Order of Medjide, the Crimean medal and clasp, and the Turkish medal. Appointed assistant surveyor in Ceylon, on 3rd Sept. 1857, and his appointment confirmed on 9th October, 1858, on retiring from the Army by the sale of his commission.
GRUNGO, PASQUALE, LL.D.—Appointed a judge of the court of appeal, Malta, Oct. 1839.
GUBBETT, POOLE.—Stipendiary magistrate, St. Lucia, appointed 1862.
GUERCE, S. N.—Was clerk in the internal revenue office, Mauritius, September, 1844; clerk in immigration office, May, 1850, and cashier of that department, July, 1854.

GUIANA, 1st BISHOP OF. Founded 1842.—RIGHT REV. WM. PIERCY AUSTIN, D.D.—Educated at Exeter College, Oxford, where he graduated B.A. 1829, M.A. 1835, and was created D.D. 1842; was previously archdeacon of British Guiana, and on the erection of this bishopric under letters patent, in 1842, was consecrated the 1st Bishop of Guiana.

GUPPY, HENRY F. J., F.A.S.L.—Appointed acting 5th clerk in the receiver general's department, Trinidad, Sept. 1858; 4th clerk, 20th Sept. 1859; 3rd clerk, 17th April, 1862; acting 2nd clerk, 24th Feb. 1863; has performed the duties of cashier in the treasury branch of the department since May, 1859.

GUPPY, R. J. LECHMERE, F.G.S., A.L.S.—Appointed junior clerk in colonial secretary's office, Trinidad, 25th July, 1859; clerk of the councils and confidential clerk in the colonial secretary's department, 24th March, 1861; was secretary to the prison discipline commission in 1865; was placed in charge of the post-office department during the investigation of the affairs of the late postmaster-general, 1865; is also secretary and superintending inspector to the general board of health of Trinidad, 31st August, 1866.
GUTHRIE, FRED. B.A.—Appointed professor of chemistry at the Royal College of Mauritius, April, 1861.

GWYNNE, E. CASTRES.—Nominee member of legislative council, S. Australia, from Aug., 1851 to 1856; elected and served as member of the legislative council Oct. 1856, to Oct. 1859; appointed attorney-general, 1857; third judge, March, 1859.
HACKETT, J.—Doctor of medicine of the University of Edinburgh; appointed resident surgeon, Public Hospital, Demerara, 1846, and physician to hospital in New Amsterdam, Berbice, 1848.
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

HACKETT, SIR WILLIAM, KNT.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin; graduated B.A., in 1846; is a member of the Irish bar; called to the bar Lincoln's Inn, Nov. 1851; appointed Queen's advocate on the Gold Coast, Aug. 1861; acting chief justice, Oct. 1861; confirmed as chief justice and judicial assessor, April, 1863; chief justice of Penang, Nov., 1866.
HAGAN, JOSEPH.—Appointed assistant land-waiter, Cape Coast Castle, 1859; and land-waiter, 1860.
HAINING, JAMES LANGFORD.—Appointed clerk in audit department, S. Australia, 1852; assistant and deputy auditor-general, 1856.
HALES, JOHN.—Educated at Rugby and Trinity College, Cambridge; appointed a clerk, after competitive examination, in the office of secretary of state for the colonies, Sept. 1858; junior class, 1862.

HALKSWORTH, W.—Employed in the Colonial office from 19th May, 1828; was private secretary to the late Right Hon. Sir James Stephen, K.C.B., under secretary of state, from 30th April, 1831, to 30th September, 1832, and again, from 1st April, 1837, to 31st December, 1847; was acting private secretary to Lord Glenelg, secretary of state from 28th November, 1838, to 19th February, 1839; appointed a clerk on the establishment 30th Aug. 1840, and librarian 24th Oct. 1859.
HALL, W. J. E.—Chief clerk, government secretary's office, Jamaica, appointed 1st May, 1860.
HALL, W. G.—Was employed on the establishment of the commissioner of roads department, Ceylon, Jan. 1846, till appointed in Sept. 1856, an assistant civil engineer and commissioner of roads.
HALL, JAMES.—Clerk of works, surveyor-general's department, Mauritius, August, 1863.

HALL, WILLIAM J. E.—Clerk of the first class in the colonial secretary's office, Jamaica, appointed 8th September, 1866; was chief clerk in the government secretary's office in the same colony, from 1st May, 1860, to the 7th Sept., 1866.

HALLIDAY, JOHN.—Entered the colonial secretary's office, Antigua, in May, 1859; 2nd clerk in governor's office, Oct. 1859; chief clerk, Jan. 1860; acting private secretary to the governor-in-chief of the Leeward Islands, from April, 1862 to June, 1862; clerk to road commissioners, July, 1862.

HALLILEY, W.—Clerk ecclesiastical establishment, Ceylon, Feb. 1842; acting sub-collector, Barbery, 1843; sub-collector, Manaar, Sept. 1844; chief clerk and warehouse-keeper, Colombo, 1846; Assistant collector and landing surveyor, Trincomalee, Dec. 1855; landing and tide surveyor, Point-de-Galle, Feb. 1861; collector of customs, southern province, Oct. 1862.

HAMILTON, GEORGE.—Entered royal navy, 1828; commissariat, Sydney, N.S. Wales, 1833; audit office, S. Australia, 1847; treasury, 1848; police inspector, 1853; chief inspector, 1860; also visiting justice, hard labour prison, Dry Creek.

HAMILTON, JAMES.—Appointed first revenue officer and warehouse-keeper in treasury department at Tobago, May, 1860; acted as deputy treasurer and controller of customs and navigation laws, from May to Dec. 1860; first entered the treasury department in 1858, as 2nd revenue officer.
HAMILTON, JOHN.—Clerk of the legislative council, Tobago, 1862.

HAMILTON, KER BAILLIE, C.B.—Educated for the army at the Royal Military Academy at Woolwich; entered the Indian military service in 1822; appointed a writer in the civil service of the Mauritius in 1826, and assistant private secretary to

governor Sir Lowry Cole there; appointed clerk of the council, at the Cape of Good Hope in 1829; afterwards acted there as colonial secretary; appointed lieut.-governor of Grenada, in 1846; administrator of the government of Barbados and the Windward Islands in 1851; governor of Newfoundland, in 1852; governor-in-chief of Antigua and the Leeward Islands, in 1855, to Jan. 1863.
HAMILTON, PETER, S.—Chief commissioner of gold, Nova Scotia.
HAMILTON, WM.—Is captain in the royal navy on half pay; appointed police magistrate, Turks Islands, 1851; is also provost-marshal and port officer; appointed 1846.
HAMILTON, WM. AUGUSTUS.—Staff serjeant, R.A., Aug. 1, 1862; 3rd clerk, Treasury, Mauritius.
HAMILTON, W. A. B.—Appointed a junior clerk in the colonial office, May, 1864, after competitive examination. Was educated at Harrow.
HAMLEY, WYMOND.—Appointed collector of customs for the colony of British Columbia, Sept. 1858; is a member of the executive and legislative council.
HAMPTON, G. E.—Private secretary to the governor of Western Australia, and clerk of the councils in that colony; appointed 1862.
HAMPTON, J. S.—Was for some years controller of convicts in Tasmania; appointed governor of Western Australia, Nov. 1861.

HANCE, HENRY F.—Entered the service of the colonial office, Sept. 1, 1844, and filled various offices in the colony of Hong Kong; was appointed 4th assistant in the superintendency of trade at Hong Kong, May 1, 1854, and 1st assistant, Jan. 19, 1857; is vice-consul at Whampoa.
HANKINSON, RIGHT REV., M.A.—Roman catholic bishop, Mauritius, February, 1864.
HANRAHAN, EDMUND.—Surveyor-general and chairman of board of works, Newfoundland; appointed 1855. Is a member of the executive council and house of assembly.
HANSON, RICHARD DAVIES.—Acting advocate-general, S. Australia, 1851; advocate-general, 1853; attorney-general, 1856; chief justice, 1861; chief justice and judge of the vice-admiralty court, 1862.
HARDING, JAMES.—Appointed harbour-master, West Australia, 1851.

HARDING, W.—Appointed Clerk to first puisne judge at the Cape in 1829; registrar of circuit courts, 1830; secretary to the land board, 1833; in 1836, appointed to prepare a précis from the records in the supreme court, of all criminal trials since the year 1736, to give an insight into the Roman Dutch Law as it existed; in 1837-38 collected and published the laws in force in the Cape Colony; in 1838, appointed clerk of the peace, Swellendam; 1845, crown prosecutor at Natal; 1846, resident magistrate in Natal, and joint auditor of the public accounts; 1847, chief commandant, Burgher forces in Natal; 1848, established and commanded, as lieut.-colonel, the volunteer yeomanry cavalry corps; same year, sent on special mission to emigrant Boers; 1850, on special duty to the Amaponda chief, Faku; 1853-4, acting colonial secretary; 1856, recorder of Natal; 1858, chief justice of that colony and member of the executive council, and judge vice admiralty court.
HARDINGE, STEPHEN T.—Postmaster and secretary to post-office, Tasmania; appointed Jan. 1, 1858.
HARGRAVE, JOHN FLETCHER.—Q.C. and solicitor-general of New South Wales, M.L.C., representing the Cowper government in the legis-
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
lative council since, May 27, 1862; attorney general N.S.W., 1864; 3rd puisne judge, 1865.

HARPER, CHARLES.—Was 1st clerk of board of supply, Malta, from June, 1819, to March, 1822; deputy registrar of contract, 1822 to 1824; editor of the government gazette, Aug. 1826 to June, 1830; and appointed magistrate of judicial police, July, 1839.

HARRIS, 3rd BARON (Utd. Kgdm. Creat. 1815).—GEORGE FRANCIS ROBERT HARRIS.—Educated at Christ Church, Oxford; was 4th class in classics, 1831; was appointed lieut.-governor of Trinidad, May, 1846; governor and commander-in-chief there, Nov. 1846; governor of Madras, Feb. 1854; major East Kent mounted rifles, 1855.
HARRIS, SAMUEL RAWLINSON.—District-commissary of taxation, British Guiana.
HARRISON, BENJAMIN.—Appointed an engineer in harbour-master's department at Mauritius, May 1847.
HARRISON, WILLIAM.—Appointed engineer in the harbour-master's department at Mauritius Nov. 1831.

HART, DANIEL.—Assistant clerk, commissariat department, Trinidad, 1825; ensign in the Royal Trinidad battalion militia forces, 4th Oct. 1831; adjoint-commandant of the districts of Santa Cruz, 1832; commandant of the districts of Las Cuevas and Marascas, 1833; lieutenant of St. Joseph's light infantry regiment of militia, 20th Nov. 1833; special magistrate for the abolition of slavery, 22nd July, 1834; provost-marshal general militia forces, 14th March, 1835; captain in the St. Joseph's light infantry regiment of militia, 31st May, 1837; sent by the governor of the island (Sir Geo. Hill) special commissioner to Venezuela, to claim indemnification for a drogher belonging to the island that had been captured by a Venezuelan cruiser, as also to enforce the release of two British subjects, 1837; commissioner of roads and bridges, 1842; governor of the Royal Gaol, 10th June, 1847; sent to Antigua to inspect and report on, prisons of that island, 10th April, 1857; sent to Barbados, to confer with the governor-general of that island under a dispatch from the secretary of state for the colonies (H. Labouchere, esq.), to Governor Hincks, in regard to the transfer of convicts of Barbados to Trinidad, 25th Oct. 1857; superintendent of prisons, 1st Feb. 1858; acted as inspector of police and inspector of weights and measures, from 22nd Oct. 1858 to 5th April, 1859; sanitary inspector, 1862; again acted as inspector of police, from the 8th Feb. 1862 to the 22nd Aug. 1863, when he resumed his duties as superintendent of prisons and sanitary inspector; sanitary inspector under local board of health, Nov., 1865; is author of the "History of Trinidad."

HART, D. MCDONNELL.—Appointed assistant clerk of the peace for the Western County of St. George, Trinidad, 10th March, 1866; was previously supernumerary in the office of keeper of maps and surveys, and in the immigration office.

HART, NATHANIEL.—Entered the public service at Trinidad, April 1, 1813, as assistant clerk in the ordnance department; was appointed acting clerk in 1818, and continued in that appointment until the reduction of the department in June, 1820, when he was appointed by commissary-general Turquand, as the clerk in the commissariat department; he was ordered from Trinidad to St. Lucia, where he served from July 1820, till June, 1822; in Jan. 1821, while at St. Lucia, he was appointed by General Mainwaring, the then governor, to be registrar of the vice-admiralty court, which office he was obliged to relinquish

in June, 1822, when he was ordered back to Trinidad. He again served at Trinidad from June 1822 till Sept. 1825, when he was ordered to Barbados by Commissary-General Routh; he served in Barbados from Sept. 1825 to June 1826, when he was ordered to Tobago; he served at Tobago till Feb. 1827, when he was again ordered back to Trinidad, and served in that island from Feb. 1827 till Oct. 1828, when he was appointed by Colonel Farquharson the then acting governor, to be governor for the gaol and attorney for prisoners; he continued in this office till 1835, when he was appointed by Lord Glenelg, secretary of state for the colonies, to be provost-marshal of St. Christopher, in which office he continued till Jan. 1, 1850, when he was appointed by governor Mackintosh to be treasurer of St. Christopher (since confirmed by Her Majesty), which office he now holds; in 1855 he was appointed controller of customs, registrar of shipping, and shipping-master; in 1856, inspector of prisons; and in 1859, a member of Her Majesty's legislative council, by Sir Hercules Robinson, governor of St. Christopher, in recognition of his long services to the public.
HARVEY, SETH.—Solicitor-general of Bermuda; appointed 1856; is a member of the house of assembly.

HARVEY, THOS. C.—M.A., Univ. Pens., 1837; articled pupil of W. Gravatt, C. E., London, 1837; civil engineer and surveyor on the Great Western Railway, 1840; on the improvement and navigation of the river Avon, 1841; also on the Sheffield and Doncaster railways, 1841; civil engineer on the Mons and Menage railway, Belgium, 1846; civil engineer at Honduras, 1853; assistant surveyor-general, Bahamas, 1854; civil engineer and surveyor, 1839; inspector of imperial lighthouses, 1857 and 1861; member of executive council, 1860.
HATHORN, J.—Appointed auditor of Natal, Oct. 1851; master of supreme court, 1863.

HAVILAND, THOMAS H.—Clerk in cheque office of Navy Victualling Yard, Chatham, 1813; provost-marshal, Prince Edward Island, by warrant from Prince Regent, 1815; naval officer, 1818; member of executive and legislative councils, 1823; assistant judge of supreme court, 1824; colonial treasurer, 1830; marshal and serjeant at mace vice-admiralty court, 1835; president of legislative council, 1839; colonial secretary and clerk of executive and legislative councils, 1839; resigned these offices on introduction of responsible government, 1851; elected mayor of Charlottetown, 1857, and has been annually re-elected to that office.

HAW, C.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate at Caledon division of the Cape of Good Hope; appointed July, 1859; was clerk of the peace in the division of Albany, 1836 to 1842; clerk to the civil commissioner, Somerset, 1842 to 1850; clerk of the peace for the Victoria district, Sept. 1850 to 1856; civil commissioner for Cradock, Feb. 1856 to date of present appointment.
HAWKER, GEORGE C., M.A., Trinity College, Cambridge.—Speaker of the house of assembly, South Australia, 1860; re-elected, ditto, 1863.

HAWKINS, A. CESAR.—Entered the army as ensign in the 1st royals, Feb. 1836, and retired after 12 years service; appointed resident magistrate of the Weenen, in colony of Natal, Sept. 1852, and removed to resident magistracy of the Upper Umcomas division of that colony, Feb. 1855; was a major in a yeomanry cavalry regiment of volunteers called the Natal carbiniers.
HAWTAYNE, G. H.—Was private secretary
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

to Mr. E. J. Eyre, when lieut.-governor of St. Vincent, in 1854, and was appointed stipendiary and police magistrate of that colony in March, 1857; member of assembly; is the captain commanding the Queen's Volunteer corps, which was raised by him in 1863.

HAY, SIR E. HAY DRUMMOND HAY, KNT. BACH. (Creat. 1859.)—Entered the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, 1832; acted for some time as private secretary to Mr Hay, under secretary of state; appointed president administering the government of the Virgin Islands, Feb. 1839; lieut.-governor of St. Kitt's, Sept. 1850; lieut.-governor of the Leeward Islands, Antigua, Montserrat, Barbados, St. Christopher, Nevis, Anguilla, the Virgin Islands, and Dominica, 1852; and governor and vice-admiral of St. Helena, March, 1855 to July, 1863.
HAYWARD, JOHN.—Solicitor-general, Newfoundland; appointed 1858.
HAZELAND, F. J.—Crown solicitor, Hong Kong; appointed 1861.

HEAD, 8th BART. (Eng.; Creat. 1676); Privy Councillor, 1857.— RIGHT HON. SIR EDMUND WALKER HEAD.—Succeeded his father in 1838; educated at Oriel College, Oxford, where he was 1st class in classics, 1827; became a fellow of Merton, and graduated M.A. 1830; was university examiner in 1834; appointed one of the poor-law commissioners in Dec. 1841, having previously been an assistant commissioner; was lieut.-governor of New Brunswick from Oct. 1847 to Sept. 1854, when he was appointed governor-general of Canada; which government he held until Oct. 1861. Is author of "The Handbook of Spanish Painting." Was appointed in 1863, to be one of Her Majesty's civil service commissioners in England; is Governor of the Hudson's Bay Company.
HECKROODT, J. H.—Has been in the customs' department at the Cape since 1842; was appointed chief clerk in that department, Oct. 1853.
HELMICH, A.—Postmaster-general, Western Australia; appointed 1847. Was clerk to the auditor-general of the colony from 1843 to 1847.
HEMMING, A. W. L.—Appointed, after competitive examination, a clerk in the office of secretary of state for the colonies, Feb. 1860; promoted Oct. 1864, 1st junior class.

HEMMING, JOHN.—Entered the civil service of the Cape of Good Hope, after competitive examination, on the 1st February, 1857; as clerk to the resident magistrate at Oudshoorn, was transferred to Bathurst, in October, 1858; and promoted to a 2nd class clerkship in January, 1860; and removed to Queenstown as 1st clerk to the civil commission, and resident magistrate.

HENDERSON, A.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate at Murraysburg division, Cape Colony; appointed 1859. Was magistrate's clerk for Victoria district, July 1848 to 1854; clerk to the civil commissioner at Albert, Aug. 1854; clerk to civil commissioner, Victoria, East, April 1856; and acting civil commissioner, 1857.

HENDERSON, E. Y. WALCOTT.—Entered the royal engineers, 1838; promoted to be lieut. 1841; captain, 1847; and major, Oct. 1858; Lieut.-Col. 1861; was appointed comptroller of convicts in Western Australia, from 1850 to 1862; appointed July, 1863, to succeed Sir Joshua Jebb as superintendent of convict prisons in England.
HENDERSON, J. E.—Chief clerk in comptroller-general's department, Western Australia; appointed Sept. 1852.
HENDRY, W. A.—Deputy commissioner of crown lands, Nova Scotia.
HENRY, W. A.—Soliciter-general of Nova Scotia, and member of the executive council and house of assembly.
HENSLows, FRANCIS H.—Clerk of the house of assembly of Tasmania; appointed 1856.
HENTY, WILLIAM.—Colonial secretary of Tasmania; appointed April, 1857.
HERBERT, W. H.—Appointed assistant government printer, Ceylon, 1854; acting government printer, 1864.

HERBERT, ROBERT G. W.—Educated at Eton, and at Balliol College, Oxford, of which he was elected Scholar in 1849; Hertford Scholar, 1851; Ireland Scholar, 1852; Latin Verse Prize, 1852; Eldon Scholar, 1854; elected Fellow of All Souls, 1854; appointed private secretary to the chancellor of the exchequer (the Right Hon. W. E. Gladstone), 1855; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1858; appointed in 1859 to be the first colonial secretary of Queensland; continues to hold that appointment under responsible government.

HERCHENRODER, F.—Was registrar of the court of first instance at Mauritius, Feb. 1841; appointed chief clerk in the registrar of supreme court office, Jan. 1852; registrar of supreme court, August, 1862.
HERCHENRODER, L.—Appointed first clerk in the registration and mortgage department, Mauritius, April, 1862.
HESLOP, A.—Attorney-general, Jamaica; appointed 1855. Called to the bar, Inner Temple, May, 1844; appointed one of the revising barristers of Jamaica, 1848.
HEWET, JOHN.—Appointed an engineer in the harbour-master's department at Mauritius, Sept. 1851.
HICKMAN, W.—Private secretary to the Marquis of Normandy, governor of Nova Scotia.

HIGGINS, W.M. F.—Entered the colonial office as a junior clerk in Aug. 1838; served as private secretary to the following under-secretaries of state in succession—Mr. Hawes, Mr. Peel, Earl of Desart, Mr. Ball, Mr. Fortescue; was appointed private secretary to Lord Stanley in Feb. 1858, and to Sir Edward Bulwer Lytton in June of the same year; left the colonial office in November, 1858, upon being nominated by the lord chancellor one of the registrars of the court of bankruptcy; appointed master of the court, Nov. 1863.

HIGGINSON, CHAS. HENRY.—Appointed private secretary to the governor-in-chief of the Leeward Islands, Nov. 1846; private secretary to Sir James Higginson, when governor of Mauritius, 1851 to 1857; and appointed officer of the civil status in Mauritius, Feb. 1857.

HIGGINSON, K.C.B. (Civil.; Creat. 1857.)— SIR JAMES MACAULAY HIGGINSON.—Was for some years private secretary to Lord Metcalfe, in Jamaica and Canada; was governor of Antigua and the Leeward Islands, also governor of Mauritius, 1850 to 1857.
HILES, THOMAS.—Appointed first clerk in the audit office, Natal, March 1864.

HILL, JOHN M.—Was appointed magistrate of Malmesbury division, Cape of Good Hope, 1838; resident magistrate of Pioketberg, 1848; civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Port Elizabeth, 1854; and civil commissioner and resident magistrate of the Cape division of the colony, 1857.
HILL, RICHARD.—Stipendiary magistrate and assistant secretary in stipendiary magistrates' department, Jamaica; appointed 1836.
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

HILL, SAMUEL HARTLEY.—Entered the colonial secretary's office, Grenada, Feb., 1852; appointed chief clerk provisionally, Dec., 1854, and permanently, June, 1857; secretary to the Board of Education, March, 1864; held the appointments of police magistrate of the western district and coroner of St. Patrick, from March to August, 1865.

HILL, COL. STEPHEN JOHN, C.B.—Entered the army, Nov. 10, 1825; was promoted lieut. Feb. 13, 1828; a captain, April 15, 1842; commanded an expedition 80 miles up the Gambia in 1849; stormed and destroyed the fortified town of Kednung, May 7, and was present at the action and defeat of the enemy on the plains of Queenella; he also commanded a detachment of the 2nd and 3rd West India regiments in the combined attack of a British and French naval and land force under Commodore Fanshawe, in the attack and total defeat of pirates, at the island of Basis, Jeba River, Western Africa, Dec. 12, 1849; received the thanks of the lords of the admiralty and was promoted to the brevet rank of major, Feb. 15, 1850; obtained his majority, Sept. 9, 1851; was appointed governor and commander-in-chief in and over Her Majesty's forces and settlements, and their dependencies, on the Gold Coast, April 1, 1851; was promoted to a lieut.-colonelcy, Feb. 3, 1854; was appointed captain-general and governor-in-chief in and over the colony of Sierra Leone and its dependencies, Nov. 6, 1854; and consul-general in the Sherbro country, on the west coast of Africa, Nov. 11, 1854; which appointment he held till Sept. 1862, when he was appointed governor-in-chief of Antigua, and the Leeward Islands, Nov. 1862; became a colonel, April 6, 1857; exchanged into, and sold out of the 3rd light dragoons (now hussars) in 1860; his commission of colonel was, Oct. 26, 1858, antedated to Nov. 28, 1854, in accordance with the 14th clause of the royal warrant of Oct. 14, 1858; was acting judge in the mixed commission court at Sierra Leone from Aug. 8, 1858 till Jan. 12, 1859; was appointed a C.B. May 18, 1860; appointed sheriff of the colony in 1863.
HILLMAN, ALFRED.—Record keeper, survey department, Western Australia, appointed 1862, to which year from 1830 he served as an assistant surveyor.
HILY, DU BREUIL.—Appointed assistant government surveyor at the Mauritius, April, 1856.

HINCKS, FRANCIS, C.B. (Creat. 1862.)—Was for several years a prominent member of the Canadian ministry, in which he held the office of inspector-general of accounts; appointed governor-in-chief of Barbados, and the Windward Islands, Oct. 1855; and governor and commander-in-chief, British Guiana, 1861.
HITCHCOCK, W.—Appointed assistant-refiner in the assay-office of British Columbia, Sept. 1857; and since the reduction of the staff in Dec. 1863, has acted as chief melter.

HITCHIN, EDW. WM.—Appointed clerk in the registrar-general's department, South Australia, 1850; clerk in colonial secretary's department, 1851; second assistant colonial secretary. 1856; secretary to commissioner of crown lands and immigration, 1857; secretary to commissioner of public works, 1859; under treasurer, 1860.

HITZLER, JAMES S.—First sworn clerk and notary-public, registrar's office, Demerara and Essequibo; appointed junior sworn clerk and notary-public, April 13, 1848; acted as registrar, Aug. to Nov. 1860, and from June, 1862, to July, 1863; justice of the peace, and commissioner of affidavits; fees. Appointed registrar, 1864.

HOBSON, ANTHONY P.—Inspector-general of police of St. Vincent; appointed 1859; was appointed acting provost-marshal in 1857; clerk of the executive council and committee, 1858; A.D.C. to lieutenant-governor.
HOBSON, CHAS.—Stipendiary magistrate for the eastern district, county of St. Patrick, Trinidad; and commissioner of petty civil court, San Fernando; appointed 1856.
HOBSON, J. H.—Stipendiary magistrate, eastern district, county of St. George, Trinidad; appointed 1850.

HODGES, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1858).—SIR WILLIAM HODGES was called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1833; practised for some years as a special pleader and went the western circuit; was recorder of Poole; appointed chief justice of the Cape of Good Hope, May 1858. Is also judge of the vice-admiralty court, and president of the legislative council of that colony; was knighted on his appointment as chief justice.

HODGSON, ROBERT.—Admitted to the bar of Nova Scotia, 1819; to the bar of Prince Edward Island, 1819; appointed surrogate and judge of probate, Prince Edward Island, 1828; attorney-general and advocate-general, 1828; appointed to executive and legislative councils, 1829; president of legislative council, 1840; acting chief justice, 1841; resigned office of attorney-general, &c., on introduction of responsible government, 1851; appointed chief justice, 1852; confirmed in the appointment, 1853; judge of court of vice-admiralty, 1853; administered the government during the absence of Lieut. Governor Dundas, 1865.
HODGSON, MAJOR-GENERAL STUDHOLME.—Appointed in 1865 to the command of H.M. forces in Ceylon; has seen 25 years' service, passed in India, Burmah, West Indies, and the Mediterranean.
HOFMEYR, J. H., L.L.D.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Bredasdorp division of Cape Colony; appointed 1855.
HOFMEYR, J. H., junior.—Chief clerk master's office, supreme court, Cape of Good Hope; was previously in the surveyor-general's department from 1852.
HOGAN, W.—Appointed superintendent of land police in Western Australia, 1861.
HOGSETT, GEORGE J.—Attorney-general, Newfoundland, from 1858 to 1861.
HOLDER, JOHN A.—Coroner, Tobago, 1859; is a member of the privy council.
HOLLAND, JOHN.—Was appointed clerk in surveyor-general's department at Mauritius, Aug. 1842; transferred to audit office, Feb. 1845; appointed senior examiner in that department, July, 1859.

HOLLIGAN, JAMES RICHARD.—Educated at Winchester; graduated at Trinity College, Cambridge; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, April, 1847; acted as attorney-general of Grenada in 1852; appointed auditor-general of Barbados, Dec. 1855; and acting colonial secretary during the absence of Mr. Walker, at St. Vincent, in 1857; was for several years a member of the house of assembly of Barbados; and was appointed colonial secretary of that island, March, 1862; administered the government of St. Kitts, from the 28th Nov. 1864, to 17th June, 1865; and reassumed the temporary administration of the same government, 28 April, 1866.
HOLLOWELL, J. A.—Lic. med. and surg., Bengal College; appointed medical assistant to Ceylon government, third class, 1843; second class,
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
1845; first class, 1854; assistant colonial surgeon, 1858.

HOLMES, G. W.—Private secretary to Mr. Seymour, Honduras; appointed July, 1857; is a captain in the royal artillery. Has received at different times commissions as police magistrate of Belize, acting colonial secretary, and held the dormant appointment of administrator of the government of Honduras; private secretary to the Governor of British Columbia, 1864; and acting commissioner of lands and works.
HOLMES, Thos. R.—Clerk in audit department, British Columbia; appointed March, 1861. Was appointed commissariat clerk, Aug. 1859.

HOLMES, SIR WILLIAM, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1855.)—Is provost-marshal of British Guiana; appointed 1847; is adjutant-general of the militia; was private secretary to Governor Sir H. Light, during his administration of the government of that colony, which he represented at the Paris Exhibition of 1855; was knighted and made a chevalier of the legion of honour 1855; represented British Guiana at the International Exhibition, 1862. Is mayor of Georgetown for the second time.
HOOK, C. M.—Appointed 4th writer, colonial secretary's office, Sierra Leone, 1862.
HORNE, WM. A.—Police magistrate, Grenada; appointed 1853. Is clerk of patents and receiver of church and land revenues, which appointments he has held since 1833; is a member of council.

HORSFORD, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1841); C.B. (Civil) 1852.—SIR ROBERT MARSH HORSFORD, eldest son of the Hon. Paul Horsford, late chief justice of Antigua (who died 1850).—Was educated at Winchester and at Exeter College, Oxford, where he graduated B.A. 1820; called to the bar at the Middle Temple, 1822; was solicitor-general of Antigua from 1825 to 1846; attorney-general from 1846 to Nov. 1847, when he was appointed chief justice; retired from the bench, Aug. 1856; received the honour of knighthood for his services in the West Indies.

HOSEASON, CAPTAIN WILLIAM.—A post captain in the royal navy, on half-pay; appointed superintendent of ports at Malta, Dec. 1856. Is shipping-master under the Merchant Shipping Act of 1854, and ex-officio member of council.

HOULTON, SIR EDWARD VICTOR LEWIS, M.A., K.C.M.G. (Creat. 1859.)—Educated at Oriel and St. John's Colleges, Oxford, of which latter college he was elected fellow; graduated B.A. in 1845, M.A. in 1847; was private secretary to Sir Wm. Molesworth, when president of the board of works in 1853, and when secretary of state for the colonies in 1855; appointed chief secretary to the government of Malta, Sept. 1855. Is ex-officio member of the council of government.
HOUSTON, LEWIS.—Is administrator-general of Berbice; appointed 1845. Was sworn clerk and notary at Berbice, 1831.
HOWELL, JOHN SIMPSON.—Was employed in the colonial bank at Barbados, and appointed treasurer of that colony, 1860.

HOWSE, ALFRED RICHARD.—Appointed 26th Oct. 1863, clerk in charge of stores, &c., land and works department of British Columbia; a non-commissioned officer in the corps of royal engineers; served as surveyor and clerk in the land office, since May, 1859.
HOYLES, H. W.—Attorney-general and premier of Newfoundland since the change of government in 1861; appointed chief justice, 1865.
HUDSON, H.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Somerset division, Cape of Good
Hope; appointed May, 1847. Was clerk to the lieut.-governor's secretary, 1838.

HUFFAM, FRED. SOWLEY.—Clerk of the supreme court of Hong Kong, appointed 1859; and registrar of companies, appointed 1865. Entered the colonial service, Hong Kong, as 3rd clerk in the colonial secretary's office, in 1855; appointed judges' clerk in 1856; acting deputy registrar, 1860, 1861, 1862; acting registrar, from Nov. 1861, to May, 1862.

HUGGINS, H. C.—Called to the bar at the Inner Temple, Jan. 3, 1858; appointed Queen's counsel at Nevis, and solicitor-general of that colony, 1860; and attorney-general of British Honduras, 1863; and was appointed in 1863 stipendiary magistrate at Demerara.

HUGGINS, HORATIO JAMES—Queen's advocate of Sierra Leone from 9 May, 1863, and chairman of commissioners "for the settlement of claims to land" from 1 Sept. 1863. Is member of the executive and legislative councils. Acted as chief justice and judge of the courts of vice-admiralty, insolvency, and divorce and matrimonial causes, from 20 May, 1865, to 10 Mar. 1866, and again, from 12 Sept. 1866; became judge ad interim of the mixed courts of justice under the slave trade treaties, on the death of Mr. Skelton, on 21 May, 1865, until the 19 March, 1866; was sent as chief commissioner, with Major Ireland, of the 3rd West India regiment, to the Moriah Country, in June, 1866, to effect the release of British subjects siezed and imprisoned during the war then raging in that territory between King Boharry and his nephew, Malaghi Baily, and to prevent further injury to life and property; and again as sole commissioner on a second commission to the same country in July, 1865, receiving on both occasions the thanks of the government for the readiness with which he undertook the duties and the "ability he displayed." He also acted as attorney-general in the island of St. Vincent in 1857 and 1858, when a note was made at the colonial office of the strong testimony borne to his able, painstaking, and laborious services. He was educated at a military college in France, and called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn in Hilary Term, 1838.
HUGHES, PERCY.—Appointed customs and tide surveyor, and superintendent of quarantine, Gambia, May 1863.
HUGON, THOMAS JOSEPH.—Was protector of immigration at the Mauritius from 1847 to 1860. Retired on pension, 1860; was formerly in the East India company's service.

HULL, HUGH MUNRO.—Appointed clerk to the governor of Tasmania, 1834; senior clerk, colonial secretary's office, 1841; police magistrate and chairman of quarter sessions, 1856; clerk assistant to parliament, 1857; coroner for Tasmania, 1856.
HULM, V. B.—Appointed Clerk in surveyor-general's department at Mauritius, Feb. 1844; promoted to be chief clerk. Feb. 1856.

HUME, W. W.—Appointed acting assistant to the government agent for the western province, Ceylon, 1848; deputy fiscal, and acting assistant agent for the central province, Oct. 1849; acting assistant government agent, Colombo, 1853; commissioner of requests, Jaffna, in that colony, 1854; temple land commissioner, 1859; senior temple land commissioner, 1860; appointed assistant government agent and commissioner of requests, Matelle, 1865.
HUMPHREYS, N.—Clerk of the house of assembly, Antigua; appointed 1810.
HUMPHRYS, WM.—From 20th Aug. 1835, to March 1837, in H.M. customs at Dominica, when
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

he was appointed secretary to the commissioners of compensation; July 1837 to June 1838, private secretary to Governor Light, of Dominica; July 1838 to Jan. 1841, private secretary to Governor Light, British Guiana, and also assistant government secretary; 1843, stipendiary magistrate, and immigration agent general; March 1853, stipendiary magistrate; sheriff of Essequibo, March 1863.

HUNT, CAPTAIN JOHN CALVIN.—Half-pay; joined Austrian army, 1844; served in 5th cuirassiers regiment; served at siege of Vienna, 1848, and campaign in Hungary; was present at all actions during the campaign; captain, 1852; was with the army of observation on the Vistula and Bukovina, in Poland, 1854; received extra certificates and thanks from the Emperor of Austria for good service and bravery; in 1855 proceeded to England, and joined the cavalry of British German Legion; in Nov. 1856 proceeded with German Legion to the Cape of Good Hope, where he was employed until disbanded in 1860; appointed acting deputy-assistant commissary-general at East London, British Kaffraria, 1860; Sept. 1861, appointed acting special magistrate to the Ganobie district, British Kaffraria.
HUNTINGTON, —Inspector of roads and bridges, Mauritius, 1866.

HURON (CANADA), 1st BISHOP OF. Founded 1857.—RIGHT REV. BENJAMIN CRONYN, D.D.—Educated at Cambridge, and Trinity College, Dublin; was rector of London, Canada West, from 1832 till his consecration in 1857. This diocese, formerly a portion of the see of Toronto, was erected into a bishopric when the diocese of Toronto was reconstituted in 1857. Population, 472,850 souls.
