NICHOLAS, REV. S.—Portuguese colonial chaplain, Colombo, Ceylon, 1864.
NICOL, GEORGE WM.—Was acting fourth writer in colonial secretary's office, Sierra Leone, 1829; held various offices in the colonial and in the mixed commissions courts until Feb. 1837; in Dec. of

that year was admitted to practice as a proctor in those courts; was an ensign in the Sierra Leone militia, 1829, a lieutenant, 1832; served in the military and naval expedition against the rebels at Cobolo on the Ribbie River; was appointed manager and magistrate of the second eastern district of the colony, 1838; fourth writer in the colonial secretary's office, and registrar of the court of chancery, 1841; registrar of the vice-admiralty court, 1846; and attained the rank of lieutenant-colonel of militia in that year; in 1850, was appointed auditor, and commissioner for the settlement of claims of land in 1859, and chairman of that board in 1862; colonial secretary and accountant and ex-officio member of the executive and legislative council, Aug. 1859.

NIND, PHILIP H.—Educated at Eton and Oxford, graduated B.A., 1856, M.A., 1857; entered colonial service as extra clerk in the office of the colonial secretary of Vancouver's Island, October, 1859; appointed assistant gold commissioner and stipendiary magistrate for Cariboo, British Columbia, July, 1860; superintendent of gold escort, June, 1863; member of legislative council, Dec. 1863; magistrate at Douglas, March, 1864.
NOAD, W. R.—Was acting surveyor-general, Newfoundland, 1847; appointed assistant surveyor. Ceylon, Oct. 1852; acting surveyor-general, 1865.

NORMANBY (RIGHT HON. GEORGE AUGUSTUS CONSTANTINE PHIPPS), MARQUIS OF,—succeeded to the title in 1863.—Privy councillor, appointed 1851; appointed ensign and lieutenant, Scots fusileer guards, 1838; deputy-lieutenant of Yorkshire, 1844; major, North York militia, 1841; resigned 1853; was treasurer of the Queen's household from Jan. 1853 to Feb. 1858, having been comptroller from July, 1851 to Feb. 1852; was added to the privy council on becoming controller; was M.P. for Scarborough from 1847 to 1851, when he was defeated after accepting office; regained his seat in July, 1852; appointed lieut.-governor of Nova Scotia, Jan. 1858; and resigned that appointment in Sept., 1863.

NORRIS, HENRY C.—Appointed clerk in the office of secretary of state for the colonies, April, 1841; promoted assistant-clerk, Oct. 1859; was secretary to Lord Carnarvon from Feb. 1858 to May, 1859; held the same appointment with Mr. C. Fortescue from June till Oct. 1859.

NORTON, BERNARD GUSTAVUS.—B.A. of Queen's University, Ireland; of the Inner Temple, London; called to the bar, 1855; appointed solicitor-general, British Guiana, 6 March, 1863, as also judge advocate of the militia; now acting 2nd puisne judge, Berbice; admitted to practice at the bar of the supreme court of British Guiana, Dec. 1860; appointed 2nd puisne judge, 1865.

NOVA SCOTIA, 4th BISHOP of. Founded 1787.—RIGHT REV. HIBBERT BINNEY, D.D.—Educated at King's College, London; was afterwards successively scholar and fellow of Worcester College, Oxford, where he graduated 1st class mathematics, and 2nd class classics, 1842, M.A. 1844; appointed tutor of that college in 1846, and bursar in 1848; ordained a deacon, 1842, a priest, 1843, and consecrated 4th Bishop of Nova Scotia, 1851; is an honorary fellow of King's College, London; patron of the archdeaconries of Nova Scotia and New Brunswick, and visitor of King's College, Nova Scotia. This was the first colonial see founded by Great Britain; the diocese includes Nova Scotia, Cape Breton, and Prince Edward's Island.
NOYES, WILLIAM T.—Police magistrate, Port Sorell, Tasmania; appointed July 1, 1857.
NUNES, W. A.—Was appointed in Nov 1835,
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

amanuensis to Sir James Stephen, when under-secretary of state for the colonies; and in Nov. 1839 was made superintendent of the copy department of the colonial office, with which was combined the appointment of registrar in Jan. 1853.
OFFICER, ROBERT, M.A.—Entered the service as colonial surgeon, 1826; speaker of the house of assembly of Tasmania.

OGILVY, DAVID STEWART.—Was ensign in Madras native infantry, 1851; appointed lieutenant land transport corps, 1854; captain, 1854; appointed to the Madras sappers and miners, 1857; assistant field engineer with Sir Colin Campbell's army, Lucknow, which appointment he quitted in March, 1858, proceeding to Mauritius on sick leave in consequence of a wound received in the assault on the king's palace at Lucknow. Has received the Crimean medal and Sebastopol clasp, Turkish medal and Indian medal and clasps; appointed stipendiary magistrate at Mauritius, Sept. 1858; was for some time acting protector of immigrants.

O'BRIEN, JAMES W.—Appointed clerk and sorter in the imperial post-office, Trinidad, in 1842; held that appointment till 1851; in charge of entire department from 1851 to 1858; and was also employed during that period as clerk in the colonial post-office; acted as Her Majesty's packet agent in 1858; in Jan. 1860, owing to the amalgamation of the imperial and colonial post-offices, was appointed chief clerk of the general post-office; promoted to chief clerk in auditor-general's department 17th April, 1862; was acting postmaster-general from July to Sept. 1862; acting auditor-general from 4th October to 31st December, 1865.

O'DONNELL, J.—Employed on the ordnance survey of England and Scotland, from October, 1847, to October, 1857; appointed to the surveyor-general's department in Ceylon, January, 1855; appointed assistant-surveyor, Ceylon, 1864.

O'GRADY, H. E.—Acting assistant government agent at Jaffna, Ceylon, 1840; assistant government agent at Kandy, 1844; district judge of Batticaloa, 1845; acting district judge, Caltura, 1850; acting assistant colonial secretary and clerk of the councils, 1851; district judge, &c., Trincomalee, 1852; government agent, north-western province, 1855; acting government agent and fiscal for the southern provinces, 1856; resumed duties in north-west province, 1858; acting government agent, east province, 1864; appointment confirmed 1865.

O'HALLORAN, WILLIAM LITTLEJOHN.—Entered army as ensign 14th foot, in Dec. 1823; obtained lieutenancy in action, 1826; company by purchase in 38th regiment, April, 1837; served at siege and storming of Bhurtapore in 1825-6; on staff, Sangor division, 1828-9; employed on recruiting service in Ireland in 1832-3; retired from service, April, 1840; appointed a member of the audit board in South Australia, 1843; private secretary to Governor Grey, and clerk of the councils, 1843; auditor-general, 1851.

OKEY, CHARLES H.—Member of council, Antigua, barrister of the Inner Temple, knight of the legion of honour, author of works on international relations, educated at merchant tailors' school, also at Heidelberg University, acted as private secretary to Lord Stuart de Rothesay, ambassador to the court of France, and as counsel to Her Majesty's embassy under four successive ambassadors in Paris; acted as police magistrate, Antigua, from July 1862 to May 1863; appointed puisne justice of Antigua in August, 1863.
OLIPHANT, LAURENCE.—Has been called to

the English bar; was appointed private secretary to the chief justice of Ceylon, 1848; accompanied Lord Elgin's special mission to Washington in May, 1854, as private secretary; was shortly afterwards appointed chief secretary and superintendent-general of Indian affairs in Canada; and accompanied Lord Elgin's special mission to China in April, 1857, as private secretary, and returned to England in May, 1859; was appointed a captain in the West Middlesex rifle volunteer corps, Feb. 28, 1860.
OLIVIER, FELIX.—Appointed clerk in the registration and mortgage office at Mauritius, Nov. 1836, and taxing clerk, July, 1857.
O'MAHONY, J.—Appointed 3rd tide-waiter, customs' department, Natal, 1860; second landing-waiter in that department, 1863.

ONDAATJE, REV. S. D. J.—Appointed colonial chaplain of Jaffna, Ceylon, Dec. 1840; resigned that appointment, September, 1842; appointed colonial chaplain of St. Thomas' Church, Colombo, in that colony, Dec. 1861.

ONDAATJE, W. C.—Lic. Med. and Surg., Bengal College; appointed medical sub-assistant to the government of Ceylon; 3rd class, 1846; 2nd class, 1851; 1st class, 1856; and assistant colonial surgeon, 1858.

ONTARIO, 1st BISHOP of, 1861.—Dr. J. T. Lewis.—The diocese of Ontario comprises that part of West Canada which is bounded on the north by the Ottawa river, on the south by the Lake of Ontario; eastward from and including Prince Edward island; on the south-east by the St. Lawrence river and the district of Vandreuil. It is a subdivision of the old diocese of Toronto. Its population is 370,000.
ORANGE RIVER, FREE STATE, 1st Bishop of.—Dr. E. TWELLS.—Consecrated 1863.

ORD, COLONEL HARRY ST. GEORGE, C.B., 1865.—Entered the royal engineers, 1837; promoted to be lieut., 1839; captain, 1846; brevet-major, 1854; lieut.-colonel, 1859; served principally in the West Indies and coast of Africa; served as brigade-major in the combined French and English expedition to the Baltic in 1854; and was at the siege and capture of Bomarsund, for which he received brevet rank; was employed under the colonial office in 1855-6 as commissioner to the Gold Coast; and in 1856-57 and 1860 at the courts of Paris and the Hague, to assist the ambassador and minister in carrying out negotiations with respect to the French and Netherlands possessions on the coast of Africa; appointed lieut.-governor of Dominica, Aug. 1857; and governor and commander-in-chief of Bermuda, Feb. 1861; sent on a special mission to the settlements on the West Coast of Africa, Oct. 1864; governor of Straits Settlements, 1867.

O'REILLY, PETER.—Formerly a lieutenant in the revenue police in Ireland; appointed assistant gold commissioner and stipendiary magistrate of British Columbia, April, 1859; high sheriff, 1859; legislative councillor, 1863; and chief commissioner, Jan. 1864.
ORGIOS, PAULIN, M.D.—Medical officer, parish of St. Andrew's, 1858.

ORMSBY, JOHN.—Was a writer in the secretary's office at Mauritius, 1824; transferred to audit office, April, 1826; appointed chief clerk in customs' department, Jan. 1828; sub-agent for protection of slaves at Seychelles, May, 1830; chief clerk, treasury department, Aug. 1832; assistant treasurer, 1843; chief clerk, immigration office, 1848, and dépôt stipendiary magistrate, March, 1859.
ORPEN, A. R.—Sub-collector of customs, Hon-
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
deklip Bay, Cape of Good Hope, 1862; has served in the department since 1858.

ORPEN, HENRY M. HERBERT.—Served as captain commanding Colesbey native levies, from Jan. 1851 to Aug. 1853, during the Kaffir war of those years; also as district adjutant of North Victoria, from April, 1852, to March, 1853; held several other military appointments during the same period; received a Kaffir war medal for services; appointed landing-waiter and searcher in the customs' department at Port Elizabeth, Cape Colony, Dec. 1853; measuring surveyor under merchant shipping act, Jan. 1856; senior landing-waiter and searcher, Dec. 1856; landing-surveyor, August, 1857.
OSBORN, M.—Clerk to resident magistrate at Ladigmith, Natal; appointed Sept. 1865, resident magistrate of Newcastle in that colony.
O'SULLIVAN, DANIEL.—Stipendiary magistrate, Dominica; appointed 1835. Is senior assistant justice of the court of common pleas, and a member of executive and legislative councils of that island.
OTAGO, 1st BISHOP OF, 1865-6.—Right Rev. Henry Lascelles Jenner, D.D. of Trinity Hall, Cambridge, lately vicar of Preston, near Sandwich; consecrated 1861.
OUDKERK, GERARD.—Colonial book-keeper, British Guiana. Appointed 1859; formerly book-keeper to the police force from 1851, and penal settlement, 1848.

OUDTSHOORN, G. V. R. V.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Swellendam division of the Cape of Good Hope; appointed Aug. 1859; was clerk to the magistrate at Wynberg from 1841 to 1849; clerk to civil commissioner of Swellendam, Feb. 1849.
OVERBEEK, J. C.—Chief clerk, finance branch, colonial office, at the Cape of Good Hope; appointed, 1862; was in the orphan chamber from 1822 to 1826; in the government bank from 1826 to 1842.
PACK, H.—Appointed assistant surveyor, Ceylon, Nov. 1860.

PACKER, CHARLES.—Graduated B.A. Trinity College, Cambridge, 1839; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1841; appointed solicitor-general, Barbados, Jan. 1847; escheator-general, 1859; elected member of the general assembly for the parish of St. Andrew's, 1846; and has continued to represent the same constituency to the present time; elected speaker of house of assembly, 1861.

PADMORE, W.—Clerk in customs, Jamaica, 1839; clerk and warehouse keeper, St. Christopher, 1842; acted as collector of customs, 1845 and 1848; clerk and assistant comptroller of customs and navigation laws, Barbados, 1853; acting comptroller, St. Christopher, 1854; pensioned Jan. 1855, on abolition of customs in West Indies; Notary public, St. Christopher, 1858; superintendent of immigrants, 1858; private secretary, clerk to executive council, clerk to administrative committee, 17th June, 1865.
PAKENHAM, T. C.—Was appointed district clerk at Port Louis, Mauritius, Feb. 1857; clerk to the chief judge, June, 1857; and appointed curator of vacant estates, Jan. 1859; consul at Madagascar.

PAKINGTON, 1st BART. (United Kingdom, Creat. 1846.) Privy Councillor, 1852.—RIGHT HON. SIR JOHN SOMERSET PAKINGTON.—Was educated at Eton and at Oriel College, Oxford; was secretary of state for the colonies from Feb. to Dec. 1852; was first lord of the admiralty, Feb. 1858 to June, 1859; became a deputy-lieut. of Worcestershire in 1832;
major, Worcestershire yeomanry cavalry, 1856; was chairman of the quarter sessions of that county from 1834 to 1854, and has been M.P. for Droitwich since 1837.

PALMER, GEORGE.—Acting auditor of public accounts, Grenada, and colonial revenue officer; appointed 1854. Entered the Imperial customs in 1837; collector of customs, Grenada, 1842, and again in 1848; on the reduction of the customs staff in 1849, was made clerk and searcher, and held that appointment until its abolition in 1863; colonial revenue office, 1854; and acting auditor of accounts, 1857; appointed police magistrate, Jan. 1865; and coroner of St. Patrick, August, 1865.
PALMER, JOHN, M.A. of Trinity College, Dublin.—Colonial treasurer, Dominica; appointed 1852. Is comptroller of customs and navigation laws, and a member of the house of assembly.

PANTIN, H. J.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin; called to the bar, Middle Temple, on 30 Jan. 1846. In July, 1848, appointed acting stipendiary magistrate for Naparima police district, Trinidad. In October following, transferred to southern police district, where he continued to act till May, 1849. In Oct. 1849, during riots, despatched with commission of special magistrate in charge of boat's crew of seamen from H.M.S. 'Scorpion,' under command of Lieutenant Morney, R.N., to inquire into outrages committed by a mob supposed to be connected with rioters in Port of Spain. Performed this duty, and received a letter of approval from the governor, Lord Harris. In Dec. 1849, appointed commissioner to visit and report on African immigrants located on estates. In April, 1830, appointed acting stipendiary magistrate for Naparima police district; acted as such till October of same year, when he was confirmed, and removed to the county of Caroni.
PARK, H. C.—Was employed for four years on the ordnance survey; appointed to the surveyor-general's department, Ceylon, Feb. 1858.

PARKER, C. S.—Educated at Eton and University College, Oxford; first-class in classics, M.A., fellow of University College; public examiner, 1859, 1860, and 1863; examiner in modern languages, 1862-3; curator of the university galleries; appointed private secretary to Mr. Cardwell, May, 1864, to July, 1866.

PARKER, THOMAS.—Auditor and comptroller of public accounts, St. Lucia; appointed 1853. Is a member of the executive and legislative councils of that island; was formerly in colonial service at Barbados, for which he receives a pension.

PARKER, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1858.) SIR HENRY WATSON PARKER.—Was private secretary to Sir George Gipps when governor of New South Wales; was for many years a member and chairman of committee of the legislative council; was first minister and principal secretary and a member of the executive council of New South Wales. Residence, Richmond, S.W.
PARTELOW, J.—Auditor-general, New Brunswick.

PARSONS, J.—Held office of deputy fiscal at Kandy, in Ceylon, 1846; commissioner of requests, &c. Matura, 1851; assistant to the government agent, Kandy, 1851; commissioner of requests, Putlam, 1853; acting district judge, Tangalle, 1855; assistant government agent, &c. &c. Hambantotte, 1856; acting principal collector of customs, 1866.
PASSMORE, THOS. H.—Medical officer for
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
the parishes of St. John and St. Mark; appointed May, 1862.
PASSOW, F. W.—Examiner of accounts to the government of Nova Scotia.

PATERSON, G. W.—Appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, February, 1859. Commissioner of requests and police magistrate, Point Pedro, 1862; acting assistant, government agent, Trincomalee, 1863; acting police magistrate, Galle, 1864; assistant to the government agent at Colombo, January, 1865.
PATTERSON, P.—Civil engineer, Natal, 1860. Was previously in the service of the colony of Grenada.

PAUNCEFOTE, JULIAN.—Called to the bar, Inner Temple, May, 1852; appointed private secretary to the late Sir Wm. Molesworth, when secretary of state for the colonies, in 1855. Has practised at the Hong Kong bar for several years; acting attorney-general in May, 1865; appointed attorney general of Hong Kong, 22 July, 1866. Is ex-officio member of the executive legislative councils of that colony.
PEARSE, BENJAMIN W.—Assistant surveyor-general, Vancouver's Island, appointed 1861; previously employed in same capacity by Hudson Bay Company during their tenure of the Island.

PEEL, Privy Counsellor. Appointed 1857.—RIGHT HON. FREDERICK PEEL, 2nd son of the late Sir Robert Peel, 2nd bart.—Educated at Harrow and at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he was 1st class in classics, 1845; was called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1849; was under-secretary for the colonies from Nov. 1851 to March, 1852, and from Dec. 1852 to Feb. 1855; under-secretary for war from 1855 to 1857; is a deputy-lieut. of Warwickshire; was M.P. for Leominster from Feb. 1849 to July, 1852, and for Bury, Lancashire, from July, 1852, to April, 1857; re-elected for Bury, May, 1859, and appointed secretary to the treasury, 1860.
PEMBERTON, AUGUSTUS.—Commissioner of police and stipendiary magistrate at Victoria, Vancouver's Island; appointed 1858.
PEMBERTON, J.D.—Employed in the capacity of colonial surveyor of Vancouver's island by the Hudson Bay Company from 1851 to 1859; appointed surveyor-general of Vancouver's Islands, Aug. 1860.
PEMBERTON, SHOLTO THOS.—Chief justice of Dominica, appointed 1861. Was previously solicitor-general of the island of Nevis, 1850; is from the colonial bar.
PENNELL, G. B.—Appointed April, 1864; colonial engineer, civil engineer, and architect, Jamaica.

PENNELL, E. B.—Appointed, after a competitive examination, a clerk in the office of secretary of state for the colonies, April, 1859; promoted to the 3rd class, April, 1863; appointed 25th Nov. 1865, private secretary to Mr. Forster, under secretary of state for the colonies, until 6 July, 1866; also to Mr. Adderley, from that date to 1 Oct., and to Sir F. Rogers from 1 Oct. 1866.

PENNELL, R. C.—Colonial secretary and auditor, St. Helena; appointed 1844. Vice-consul at Rio de Janeiro in May, 1830; acting commissary judge and consul during part of 1832-3; special justice in Jamaica in 1834; and auditor at St. Helena, on the 24th Dec. 1836.

PENNINGTON, EDWARD.—Appointed a clerk, in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, 1838; was transferred to the war department, on the separation of the two offices in 1854, as an assistant senior, and promoted to the 1st class in
1856; was assistant to the chief clerk from 1843 to 1854.
PERCH, THOS.—Police magistrate of Bridgetown, Barbados, and a master in chancery.
PERKIS, A. E.—Consular clerk, Hong Kong; appointed 1862.

PERLEY, MOSES HENRY.—Was appointed one of the commissioners for Indian affairs in the province of New Brunswick in 1838; emigration officer for New Brunswick, June 17, 1843, and May 25, 1855; commissioner for the North American fisheries, under the reciprocity treaty with the United States of June 5, 1854.

PERTH (WESTERN AUSTRALIA), 1st BISHOP OF. Founded 1856.—RIGHT REV. MATTHEW BLAGDEN HALE, D.D.—Educated at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A. 1835, M.A. 1838; was appointed archdeacon of Adelaide, 1847; canon there, 1849; and consecrated 1st Bishop of this see, 1857. The diocese includes the whole of Western Australia.
PEYTON, PHILIP.—Employed by Messrs. Watt, of Birmingham, for 4 years as bronze strip caster; appointed foreman of the melting house in Hong Kong mint, Feb. 1865.
PHELPS, W.—Appointed assistant surveyor of Western Australia, 1855.
PHELPS, WILLIAM R.—Appointed chief justice of St. Helena, 1863. Is also judge of the Vice-Admiralty court, and member of the council of that island.
PHILLIPS, H. L.—Second puisne judge, Natal; appointed 1863. Acting first puisne judge 1864.
PHILLIPS, MAURICE G.—Assistant clerk gold commissioners' office, Cariboo, British Columbia, formerly Inland revenue office, Somerset House.
PHILPOTT, EDWARD.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Alexandria division, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1857. Was clerk to the magistrate at Port Elizabeth, 1837 to 1857.
PHIPSON, T.—Appointed sheriff of Natal, 1852; and clerk to the registrar of deeds in that colony, 1861.
PLAT, HENRY.—Was assistant clerk at district court, Flacq Mauritius, Feb. 1853; joint clerk, Nov. 1856; appointed 1st stipendiary clerk at Plaines Williams, Sept. 1858.

PICQUENARD, DONALD, C. JOSEPH.—Appointed extra clerk, registration office, March 1, 1853; clerk, post office, April 1, 1853; clerk, treasury, Jan. 1, 1854; junior clerk, treasury, July 1, 1859; curator of vacant estates, Mauritius, July 1, 1862.
PIERCE, WILLIAM EDWARD.—Secretary and clerk of cheque at the public hospital, British Guiana; also secretary to the committee of pilotage; was a sworn accountant.
PIERS, C.—Superintendent-general of convicts, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1856. Was resident magistrate, Tulbagh, from 1848.

PIKE, ADOLPHUS.—Entered the civil service of Sierra Leone as acting first writer, colonial secretary's office, 1843, having previously served as a temporary clerk in the commissariat department at that station, from Jan. 1853; was appointed agent of emigration for the island of Trinidad, 1844; and agent-general of emigration for the West Indies, 1846; receiver-general of unrepresented estates, 1848; acting harbour-master in 1855; acting collector of customs, 1856; postmaster, 1857; assessor of house and land tax, 1858; acting colonial secretary, 1858; is 1st writer in the colonial secretary's office, colonial auditor of accounts, a magistrate of the colony, and marshal of the mixed commission courts.
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
PIKE, THOMAS —Appointed harbour-master, Sierra Leone, Aug. 1857; is ex-officio superintendent of quarantine, superintendent of lighthouses, and superintendent of the water police.
PILGRIM, F. F.—Police magistrate, St. John's Parish, Barbadoes.
PINDER, C. F.—Registrar of the court of bankruptcy, clerk of the court of common pleas, and prothonotary, Bahamas; appointed 1842.
PINDER, JOHN. — Was inspector-general of police at Bahamas, 1840; harbour-master at Nassau, 1852; and provost-marshal, 1856. Is a member of council.

PINE, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1856.)—SIR BENJAMIN CHILLEY CAMPBELL PINE. — Educated at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A. 1834, M.A. 1840; called to the bar, 1841; appointed Queen's advocate at Sierra Leone, 1841; acting governor there, 1848; accompanied capt. Monypenny, R.N., to the Sherbro river, and destroyed a strong stockaded fort, from which outrages had been committed on British trading vessels. In 1849, again proceeded to the Sherbro, in conjunction with Captain Dunlop, R.N., and a detachment of troops, and put an end to the civil war, which had ravaged the country for years; lieut.-governor of Natal, 1849; 1851 received the thanks of her majesty's government for the measures he took to procure peace in the colony during the Kaffir war; in 1855, led a force of mounted volunteers against the Amabaca tribe, numbering 2,000 men, and completely enforced their submission; governor and commander-in-chief of the Gold Coast settlements, 1856; lieut.-governor of St. Christopher's, May, 1859. Is author of some articles in the Encyclopædia Britannica on the "African Colonies;" acting gov. Antigua, 1866.

PINE, RICHARD.—Appointed governor of the Gold Coast, 1862; was Queen's advocate, police magistrate, clerk of the councils, and superintendent of police at the Gambia, from 1855 to date of present appointment; prior to and during which period he held many appointments, and was frequently employed on important missions to native kings and chiefs.
PINES, HENRY G.—Immigration agent, Nova Scotia, and member of the legislative council.
PINN, A.—Appointed professor of English at the royal college of Mauritius, Feb. 1862.

PINNEY, F. B.—Collector of customs and principal comptroller of customs and navigation laws, Cape of Good Hope; is a member of the executive council; also, chairman of the harbour board; entered the home service as clerk in the legacy duty office in 1830; appointed to secretary's department of stamps and taxes in 1834; sub-collector of customs, Simon's Town, Cape of Good Hope, in 1838, and, in conjunction with that appointment, resident magistrate in 1848; acting sub-collector and comptroller of customs at Port Elizabeth, in 1857; confirmed in that appointment on the promotion of his predecessor, in 1861, and appointed to his present office in 1865.
PLAGUEMANN, O. F.—Superintendent of police, St. Christopher, March, 1858, till June, 1859; superintendent of public works, June, 1859, till the present time.
PLATT, H. A.—Private secretary to Governor Seymour at Honduras; appointed 1859; as a lieut. in the 69th regiment.

PLOW, HENRY F.—Appointed private secretary to lieut.-colonel Ord, governor of Bermuda, 1861; also acted in the same capacity at Dominica from 1857 to 1861; accompanied Colonel Ord on his mission to the West Coast of Africa, 1864-5; and
private secretary to Colonel Ord in the Straits Settlements, 1867.
PLOWDEN, ALFRED CHICHELE, B.A.—Of Brasenose College, Oxford. Appointed private secretary to Sir John P. Grant, K.C.B., governor of Jamaica, 7th August, 1866.

PLUES, S. S.—In the colonial service in Jamaica from 1843 to 1854, when he was appointed police magistrate of Honduras, which post he held till June, 1861; clerk of the courts and keeper of public records, June 1861 to August 1864; appointed attorney-general of Honduras, August 1864.
PLUMMER, H. P.—Stipendiary magistrate, British Guiana.

POLE, H.—Appointed commissioner of requests and police magistrate of Mulletivoe, Ceylon, 1845; commissioner of requests, &c., at Galle, 1847; at Jaffna, in 1848; district judge, &c., of Matura, 1854; acting assistant government agent, &c.; Manaar, 1859; resumed duties at Matura, 1861, acting district judge at Jaffna, 1863; resumed duties at Matura, 1865.

POLE, R. C.—Employed from July, 1859, till August, 1861, in the surveyor-general's department, Ceylon; acting landing and tide surveyor at Galle, April, 1862; acting assistant government agent, Jaffna, June, 1863; confirmed 1865.
POLLARD, WILLIAM BRANCH—Appointed auditor-general, British Guiana, 1860; was financial accountant from 1853 to 1860, when that office was abolished.
POLLARD, WILLIAM BRANCH, Jun. — Colonial civil engineer, British Guiana.
POOLEY, CHARLES E.—Appointed clerk to the attorney-general of British Columbia, and a deputy registrar for the colony, January, 1863.

PORTER, G. H. A.—Appointed colonial secretary of the Virgin Islands, 9th July 1844; registrar of deeds, August, 1846; notary public, 1851; registrar in Admiralty, March, 1847; clerk to court of Petty Sessions, February, 186; private secretary to Presidents Price, Longden, and Sir A. Rumbold, Bart., from 1859 to 1865; member of executive council, 1849 to 1852, when he resigned, but was re-appointed, August, 1865; is major of the Virgin Islands artillery.
PORTER, JOSEPH.—Chief clerk in colonial secretary's office, Vancouver's Island; appointed 1859.
PORTER, W.—Attorney-general of the Cape of Good Hope; appointed Sept. 1839. Is a member of the executive council, and has a seat in both houses of parliament; retired on a pension, 1865.

POTGIETER, CONRAD FREDERICK. — Assistant commissary general to the forces; served at the Cape of Good Hope in the commissariat, from 1835 to 1853, taking part in the Kaffir wars of 1835-46 and 1851-2, served in Turkey during the war with Russia, from 1854 to 1856; served in the civil service of British Kaffraria, in the treasury, from Feb. 1858 to June; and again as acting treasurer, from 1st Jan. 1860 to present time.

POUPARD, RAYMOND, P. A.—Was clerk in district magistrate's office at Mauritius, April, 1856; transferred to audit office, Oct. 1857; and appointed a junior examiner in that department, Jan. 1860; resigned.
POWELL, LEWIS, M.D.—Appointed to the civil hospital at Mauritius, Oct. 1840; superintendent of lunatic asylum, June, 1853.

POWER, E. R. B.—Was gazetted to the office of extra assistant to the colonial secretary, Ceylon, on the 1st June, 1833; to be extra assistant, and to act as private secretary to the right hon. the governor, Sir R. Wilmot Horton, Bart. on the 6th
Digitized by Google
R
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

Dec. 1833; to be private secretary to the governor on the 26th Dec. 1833; on the 16th Feb. 1835 proceeded to England by order of the governor, in charge of despatches for the secretary of state; returned to the colony, and resumed his duties as private secretary to the governor, on the 28th Oct. 1836, and continued to hold that appointment until the departure of Governor Sir R. Wilmot Horton from Ceylon in Nov. 1837; was appointed assistant government agent at Kurnegalle on the 10th May, 1837; and assistant government agent and district judge at Four Korles, on the 1st Oct. 1837; assistant government agent, &c. at Kaigalle, 1845; acting district judge, Badulla, 1849; principal assistant colonial secretary, and clerk to the councils. 1851; government agent, &c. central province, 1854; and a member of the legislative council; retired from the Ceylon civil service on a pension, May 1860; at the request of the committee appointed by the governor to further the objects of the International Exhibition, 1862, Mr. Power acted as the representative of Ceylon in London on that occasion.

POWER, JULIUS CHAS. — Entered the service in Oct. 1845 as accountant, surveyor-general's department, Hong Kong; appointed clerk of deed registry, also in the same department, 1847; appraiser of the supreme court in its ecclesiastical jurisdiction in Jan. 1846; was joint assessor and collector of police and lighting rates for the years 1856 and 1857; acted as colonial treasurer from April, 1860, to June, 1861; member of and honorary secretary to the board of education in June, 1861; acted as assistant magistrate of police, coroner, and sheriff, from Oct. 1861, to July, 1862; and as second magistrate of police and sheriff till end of 1862; reappointed sheriff in Jan. 1863, and held the office till Jan. 1864.

POWER, T. C. — Appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, 1845; assistant agent at Galle, 1845; district judge, &c., Ratnapoora, 1846; assistant government agent, Kaigalle, 1849; assistant government agent, Kurnegalle, 1853; acting assistant agent, acting district judge, &c., Ratnapoora, 1859, in which appointment he was confirmed, 1862; district judge, &c., Badulla, 1863.
POWNALL, WM. R. — Appointed a clerk in emigration commissioners' office, London, Dec. 1848.
PRESTAGE, WARD. — Overseer of works, Hong Kong, appointed July, 1863.
PRICE, JOHN. — Superintendent of Corradino prison, Malta.

PRICE, J. T. — Appointed writer, and attached to the office of the government agent, Jaffna, Ceylon, July, 1860; passed in Tamil, 1862; acting assistant government agent, Mulletivoe, 1863; police magistrate at Kayto, 1864.

PRICHARD, C. J. — Late lieutenant in her Majesty's 38th regt. of foot; appointed warden of the Gaol at New Westminster, in the colony of British Columbia, Nov. 19, 1860. Is a justice of the peace for the colony, and holds an acting commission as judge of the county court.

PRIMET, C. BERESFORD. — Appointed clerk of customs, bonded warehouse-keeper, &c. at the Gambia, 1855; served under Colonel G. D'Arcy as Major in the royal Gambia militia, during the Badiboo war, and was present at the taking of Kinty Cunda and Sabba, and at the storming of the latter town on the 21st Feb. 1861; appointed assistant coroner, 1861; acting collector of customs, acting registrar, acting clerk of the crown, &c., justice of the peace, and commissioner of the court of requests, in 1862; and most favourably
mentioned by the governor in despatch to secretary of state, 16th June, 1862, for zeal and energy displayed in bringing the Barra civil war to a conclusion. Acting auditor at the Gambia, 1864.
PRINCE, THOMAS. — Entered the immigration department at Mauritius, July, 1854; appointed police inspector, March, 1857, and superintendent, October, 1860.
PRITCHARD, R. M. — Collector and comptroller of customs, St. Helena; appointed 1852. Is also registrar of shipping; was previously clerk in the same department; entered the service, 1839.
PRITCHETT, JOHN. — Superintendent of coining presses in Messrs. Watts foundry at Birmingham, Feb. 1865; foreman of the press-room, Hong Kong mint.

PYNE, WM. ROWLAND. — Appointed government clerk of the Falkland Islands, by Lord Taunton, Feb. 1858; clerk to executive and legislative councils and in charge of government stores, May 1859; was acting colonial secretary from January, 1862, to September of that year, when he was confirmed in the office of colonial secretary, by the Duke of Newcastle, K.G.; from January, 1862, to August, 1866, in charge of the colonial treasury; appointed magistrate, December, 1862, and a member of the executive council, January, 1863; is, by colonial ordinance, shipping master, officer of customs, and registrar of seamen; also, postmaster-general of the Falkland Islands; has, at different times, held the offices of coroner and register of lands, births, deaths, and marriages; is likewise colonial emigration and immigration agent; also, by custom, attorney general and crown prosecutor in the colonial law courts.

QUEBEC, 4th BISHOP OF. — DR. J. W. WILLIAMS, 1863; the diocese was first constituted by patent, 28 June, 1793, and comprised Upper and Lower Canada and their dependencies. It now includes districts of Quebec, Three Rivers, St. Francis and Gaspé, and contains 43 parishes.

QUIN, WM. — Formerly a sergeant in the royal artillery, and accompanied Colonel Chesney, R.A., in the last Euphrates expedition; after which he received an appointment under the imperial customs in the West Indies, 1840, and was made sub-treasurer of St. Christopher; appointed accountant in the treasury at Antigua, 1857; appointed captain superintendent of police at Hong Kong, 1862.
QUIN, R. — Appointed assistant surveyor, Western Australia, 1863.

RAINIER, J. — Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of the Malmesbury division of Cape Colony; appointed 1856; is also superintendent of roads and chairman of divisional council; was magistrate for Riversdale, 1851 to 1856.
RAWLINS, EDWARD G. — Treasury clerk at St. Kitt's; appointed Aug. 1, 1850; was acting sub-treasurer from April 1861 to Sept. 1862.

RAWSON, RAWSON WILLIAM, C.B. — Appointed a clerk in the board of trade, January, 1829; served as private secretary to the vice-president, Mr. C. Poulett Thomson, 1830-34; to the president, Mr. Alex. Baring, 1834; and to the vice-president, Mr. W. E. Gladstone, 1841-42; acted as secretary to the pilotage commission, 1834-36; appointed civil secretary to the governor-general of Canada, Aug. 1842; transferred, upon the abolition of that office, to the treasurership of Mauritius, Jan. 1844; appointed colonial secretary of the Cape of Good Hope, May, 1854; admitted a companion of the civil order of the Bath, Oct. 1858. Appointed governor of the Bahamas, Nov. 1864.
READ, EDWARD. — District-commissary of taxation, British Guiana.
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
REDHEAD, A.—Stipendiary magistrate for Toco.
REDL, CHARLES ARTHUR.—Rector of the royal college, Mauritius; appointed January, 1859.

REES, GEORGE HERMAN.—2nd class clerk, despatch and correspondence branch, colonial secretary's office, Jamaica; entered the service on the 20th April, 1865, as clerk in the inspector of prison's office; transferred on 22nd December of the same year, to the private secretary's office, as chief clerk; and made 2nd class clerk in the colonial secretary's office, on the 18th of September, 1866.

REGNARD, F. J.—Appointed assistant civil commissary Flacq district, Mauritius, 1837; police officer and inspector of distilleries, 1838; stipendiary magistrate, Flacq, 1845; stipendiary magistrate at Pamplemousses, May, 1861.

REID, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1840.)—SIR JAMES JOHN REID.—Is a member of the supreme council of justice of the United States of the Ionian Islands; was formerly an advocate at the Scottish bar, to which he was admitted in 1827.
REID, R.—Writer, Ceylon civil service, 1866.

REILY, JOHN.—Was appointed a member of the legislative council of St. Vincent, 1844; resigned his seat at that board, 1858; was appointed police and stipendiary magistrate in that island, 1845; re-appointed to legislative council, and member of the administrative committee, 1863.
RENNALLS, SAMUEL.—Assistant clerk to the legislative council, Jamaica; custos rotulorum of the parish of St. Thomas in the Vale.

RENNIE, W. H.—Entered the colonial service in April, 1851, acting surveyor in the Falkland Islands; was sent to inspect and report upon the guano deposits in 1852; appointed government clerk in 1853; acting colonial secretary, 1856; and auditor-general, Hong Kong, 1858. Is a member of the legislative council.
RENOUF, CHARLES.—Clerk in colonial secretary's office at Mauritius, 1837; appointed clerk to the district magistracy at Flacq, 1858; stipendiary magistrate at Pamplemousses, April, 1862.
REYNE, H. E.—District officer, Ambegamua, Ceylon.
REYNOLDS, T. J.—Assistant surveyor, Ceylon, 1856.
REYNOLDS, T. J.—Employed on the unfixed establishment of the surveyor-general's department, Ceylon, from 1st May, 1856, till 28th February, 1866; appointed assistant surveyor on 1st March, 1866.
RICHARDSON, H.—A member of the privy and legislative councils, of Tobago.
RICHARDSON, OLIVER K.—Appointed second clerk, colonial secretary's office, South Australia, 1841; chief clerk, 1842; assistant colonial secretary, 1853; under secretary, 1856.

RICKWOOD, A. G.—Appointed inspector of distilleries at Mauritius, Feb. 1856; taxing clerk, January, 1857; clerk in colonial secretary's office, February, 1857; superintendent of distilleries, September, 1862.
RITTER, GUSTAVE.—Clerk to the judge of the supreme court of Mauritius.

RIVERS, J. C.—Clerk to the executive council, and aide-de-camp to the governor, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1861; was in the colonial office from 1844 to 1846; in the road department from 1846 to 1848; was assistant civil engineer from 1848 to 1855; civil commissioner and resident magistrate, Namaqualand, from 1855 to 1859.
ROBERTS, HENRY.—Called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1836; appointed by Lord John Russell, Chairman, &c. of quarter sessions, and

chief judge of common pleas in Jamaica, 1840; also commissioner of education in that colony in 1845; resigned Jan. 1853; appointed private secretary to the Duke of Newcastle, Jan. 1853; under secretary for war, Dec. 1854; resigned March, 1855; appointed commissioner of inland revenue, Oct. 1855, which appointment he now holds.

ROBERTSON, D.—Member of the Royal College of Surgeons, Edinburgh; appointed surgeon of the liberated African department, M'Carthy's Island, Gambia, May 24, 1834; colonial surgeon, April 1, 1838; and colonial secretary, May 6, 1848; has served one year as Queen's advocate and judge of the court of common pleas; several years as assistant judge; three different times as acting chief justice; and nearly 34 months as acting governor; volunteered his services as surgeon of H. M.'s brig 'Curlew,' when the officers and crew were suffering from yellow fever, the surgeon and all the officers, with the exception of the commander and one midshipman and a third of the crew, having died in the harbour of Bathurst of that fatal disease. Acting governor 1864.

ROBERTSON, J. ANDERSON.—Was clerk in the colonial secretary's office at Mauritius, May, 1837; chief inspector of licences, November, 1844; coroner in admiralty court, 1846; superintendent of immigration taxes, 1847; appointed magistrate in the island, Jan. 1852; junior district magistrate, July, 1866.
ROBERTSON, R. G. MCDONNELL.—Appointed first writer, Feb. 8, 1862; and lieut. in the Royal Gambia Militia Artillery, April 11, 1862.
ROBINSON, BEVERLEY.—Receiver-general and provincial treasurer, New Brunswick.
ROBINSON, BRYAN.—Assistant judge, Newfoundland; appointed 1858. Has been attached to the judicial establishment of the colony since 1833.

ROBINSON, SIR HERCULES, G.R.—Knt. Bachel. 1859.—Served for some time in the 87th fusiliers; on his retirement from the army was actively engaged during the Irish famine, 1846-9, under the commissioners of public works and poor law board in Ireland; was appointed in 1852 Chief Commissioner to inquire into the fairs and markets of Ireland; was appointed President of Montserrat, 1854; lieutenant-governor of St. Christopher, 1854; with which he held the dormant commission of governor-in-chief of the Leeward Islands; appointed governor, commander-in-chief, and vice-admiral of Hong Kong, June, 1859. Was a member of the commission appointed in the autumn of 1863, to inquire into the financial condition of the straits settlements; appointed governor of Ceylon, Jan. 1865.

ROBINSON, 1st BART. (Utd. Kgdm. Creat. 1854); C.B. (Civil) 1850.—HON. SIR JOHN BEVERLEY ROBINSON.—Appointed chief justice of Upper Canada, 1829; is chancellor of Trinity College, Toronto; received the hon. degree of D.C.L. at Oxford, 1855.
ROBINSON, M. R.—Was deputy surveyor-general at the Cape, 1846 to 1859; appointed deputy colonial engineer and commissioner of roads in that colony, Aug. 1859.

ROBINSON, WILLIAM C. F.—Was private secretary to Sir Hercules Robinson in the government of St. Kitt's and Hong Kong, from 1855 to 1860; president of Montserrat, 1852; administered the government of Dominica from the 5th Jan. to 18th Oct. 1865; governor and commander-in-chief of the Falkland Islands, 23rd May, 1866.
ROBINSON, WILLIAM.—Appointed clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies,
Digitized by Google
R 2
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

Nov. 3, 1854; promoted to 3rd class, June, 1858; was private secretary to Mr. Herman Merivale, from June 1858 to May, 1860; to Sir Frederic Rogers, Bart., from May 1860 to March, 1862; when he was promoted to be an assistant senior. Private secretary to the Right Hon. Edward Cardwell, from April 4 to June 17, 1864.
ROE, JOHN S. — Is a lieut. in the royal navy on half-pay; appointed surveyor-general of Western Australia, 1828. Is a member of the executive council.

ROGERS, 8th BART. (Eng. Creat. 1698-9.) — SIR FREDERIC ROGERS. — Educated at Eton, and at Oriel College, Oxford, where he attained a double first class in 1832; a Craven university scholarship, Oriel fellowship, Vinerian scholarship and fellowship; graduated M.A. 1835; B.C.L. 1838; called to the bar of the Inner Temple, 18; was appointed registrar of joint stock companies, 1843; and one of the commissioners on the colonial land and emigration board, 1846; assistant commissioner for the sale of encumbered estates in the West Indies, 1857; and permanent under-secretary of state for the colonies, 1860.
ROGERS, HENRY. — Junior assistant surgeon civil Hospital, Mauritius, May, 1862; senior assistant, February, 1865.

ROLLESTON, CHRISTOPHER. — Entered the public service in New South Wales on Jan. 1, 1846, as commissioner of crown lands for the district of Darling Downs; obtained leave of absence to visit England in Jan. 1853; returned to the colony at the end of 1854; appointed private secretary to Sir William Thomas Denison, governor-general in Jan. 1855; and registrar-general of the colony of New South Wales on Jan. 1, 1856; which office he now holds.
RONDEAUX, J. — Appointed clerk to the second puisne judge of the supreme court of Mauritius, May, 1863.
ROOSMALECOQ, A. H. — Appointed assistant commissioner of roads, Ceylon, 1845; assistant government agent, Galle, 1846; district judge of Tangalle, 1853; district judge, Trincomalee, 1862.
ROPER, W. T. — Rector of St. George's, Dominica.
ROSS, EDMUND CHARLES. — Member of the administrative committee of St. Vincent; was appointed inspector-general of schools in that island, 1858; was called to the bar at the Middle Temple, 1838.

ROSS, H. J. — Of Middle Temple, barrister-at-law, home circuit, Surrey sessions; attorney-general, Grenada, 1856; chief justice of St. Kitt's and Nevis; appointed 1857; also judge of the complaint court and visiting justice of the gaol.

ROSS, WM. A., F.R.G.S. — Appointed colonial secretary at the Gold Coast, July, 1860; acting chief justice of supreme court, 1861; acting judge of vice-admiralty court, 1862; and acting governor, April, 1862. Is author of "Sweden and Denmark;" "Letters of an African on the Slave Trade, and on Human Sacrifices in Dahomy," &c. &c. Resigned on account of ill health, April, 1864.
ROUILLAND, JOHN. — District magistrate, Mauritius, February, 1865.

ROUTH, RANDOLPH. — Was deputy assistant commissary-general in Canada, 1834; assistant commissary-general in Ireland, 1846; assistant commissary-general at the Cape of Good Hope, 1851; was acting colonial treasurer of British Kaffraria, 1854, and appointed deputy commissary-general, Mauritius, March 1860.
ROWAN, K.C.B. (Creat. 1856.) — SIR WILLIAM

ROWAN. — Entered the army as ensign, 52nd foot, in 1803; served 25 years with that regiment in Sicily, Portugal, Spain, France, Belgium (at Waterloo), and North America; was civil and military secretary to Lord Seaton in Canada from 1832 to 1839; attained the rank of major-general in 1846; commanded the forces in Canada with the local rank of lieut.-general from 1849 to 1855; and for a portion of that period administered the civil government of the province; made lieut.-general and col. of the 19th foot, 1854.

RUMBOLD, 5th BART. (GREAT BRITAIN. Creat. 1779.) SIR CARLO ARTHUR HENRY. — Entered the army in 1837; served with the 51st K.O.L.I. from 1837 to 1840, in New South Wales and Tasmania; with the 21st fusiliers from 1840 to 1842, in Bengal, Mauritius, and the Cape of Good Hope; purchased a company in 1st W. I. regiment in 1844; served in the West Indies until 1845; exchanged to 70th regiment and sold out in 1846; was appointed stipendiary magistrate at Jamaica, July, 1848; held that office until 1855; served with the Osmanli cavalry during the late Russian war, and was brigade-major to major-general Charles Havelock; holds the rank of colonel in the imperial Ottoman army, and has received her Majesty's royal licence to accept and wear the imperial order of the Medjidié; was appointed president of Nevis, 4th March, 1857, till March, 1863; president of the Virgin Islands, Oct. 1865.

RUMSEY, R. MURRAY. — Appointed, 1835, by Lord Glenelg, as stipendiary magistrate for St. Christopher; sent Jan. 1837, at the instance of Lord Glenelg, to the Virgin Islands, to associate himself with special justice Gordon, respecting the removal of some prædial apprentices from Thatch Island; noticed with approbation in a despatch of Lord Glenelg's, date Oct. 31, 1838, to Sir W. Colebrook, governor-in-chief, Antigua; offered by Lord John Russell the provost-marshalship of Dominica, 1841; mentioned in a despatch dated Aug. 19, 1841, with Mr. R. B. Cleghorn, as "the two stipendiary magistrates in this government whose reports have displayed in his Lordship's (Lord John Russell's) estimation the greatest judgment and ability." Appointed by Lord Stanley to the colonial secretaryship of Dominica, in 1842; and appointed by the same secretary for the colonies, colonial secretary of St. Christopher, 1844; also May 21, 1844, appointed by the governor-in-chief, registrar of deeds with the approbation of the council and assembly (Act 69, 1727).

RUPERT'S LAND. 1st BISHOP OF. Appointed 1849. — RIGHT REV. DAVID ANDERSON, D.D. — Was formerly scholar of Exeter College, Oxford, where he graduated B.A. 1836, M.A. 1839, and D.D. 1849; was ordained in 1837 and consecrated in 1849; was previously vice-principal of St. Bees' clerical college, and perpetual curate of All Saints' church, Derby. This diocese, comprehending the whole of the Hudson's Bay Company's settlements (or Prince Rupert's Land), was erected into a see in 1849, and endowed by a legacy from the late W. Leith, Esq. Retired 1864.

RUPERT'S LAND. 2nd BISHOP OF. Appointed 1865. — RIGHT REV. ROBERT MACHRAY, D.D. — Late dean and fellow of Sidney Sussex College, Cambridge, and vicar of Madingley; graduated in 1855, when he was 34th Wrangler. The diocese of Rupert's Land is 37,000 square miles in extent, and has a population of 200,000 souls.
RUSHWORTH, EDW. EVERARD. — D.C.L. and late fellow of St. John's College, Oxford; appointed a clerk in the inland revenue office,
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

Somerset House, 1842; was private secretary to Sir Gaspard Le Marchant, in the governments of Newfoundland and Nova Scotia, from 1847 to 1855; president administering the government of Montserrat from 1855 to 1861; appointed auditor-general of Mauritius, 1861. Was acting director of the railway department. Is a member ex-officio of the council and commissioner of admiralty court in that island; appointed March, 1866, government secretary, British Guinea; administrator, government of Trinidad, April, 1866; financial secretary, Jamaica, Oct. 1866.

RUSSELL, EARL (Creat. first Earl, Aug. 1861.) JOHN RUSSELL.—Was M.P. for Tavistock from 1813 till Mar. 1817, and also from July, 1818, till Mar. 1819; for Huntingdonshire from 1820 till 1826; and for Bandon Bridge from Jan. 1827 till 1830; was made a privy councillor, Nov. 22, 1830; was paymaster of the forces from Dec. 16, 1830, till Dec. 23, 1834; was M.P. for Tavistock from Dec. 1830 till Dec. 1832, when he was returned for South Devon, which county he represented till May, 1835; was appointed one of the ecclesiastical commissioners for England, Aug. 15, 1836; was secretary of state for the home department from April, 18, 1835, till Aug. 30, 1839; was M.P. for Stroud from May 21, 1835, till June, 1841; was secretary of state for the colonies from Aug. 30, 1839, to Sept. 3, 1841; was M.P. for the city of London, from July, 1841, to 1861; was first lord of the treasury from July 6, 1846, till Feb. 26, 1852; was secretary of state for foreign affairs from Dec. 28, 1852, till Feb. 20, 1853, when he retained a seat in the cabinet, but held no appointment; was lord president of the council from June 12, 1854, till Feb. 7, 1855; was appointed to be the unpaid charity commissioner for England and Wales, July 8, 1854; was sent on a special mission to Vienna, Feb. 11, 1855; was appointed one of a committee of council to superintend the application of any sums of money voted by parliament for the purpose of promoting public education, Feb. 28, 1855; was secretary of state for the colonies from March to July, 1855; was appointed secretary of state for foreign affairs, June 18, 1859; and one of the committee of council to superintend the application of any sums of money voted by parliament for the purpose of promoting public education, July 6, 1859; was in attendance upon her majesty when she proceeded to Coburg in Sept. and Oct. 1860; prime minister, Nov. 1865.
RUSSELL, H. H.—Was clerk of the works in the convict establishment, Bermuda; appointed Dec. 1856; which office was abolished on the break up of the establishment in 1863.

RUSSELL, H. S. O.—Appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, 1852; acting assistant government agent at Jaffna, 1854; assistant agent, Colombo, 1855; acting assistant government agent, Putlam, 1856; district judge, &c. Chilaw, 1860; acting assistant agent at Badulla, 1861; district judge, Negombo, 1862; acting assistant agent, &c., Putlam, Oct. 1862; assistant government agent, district judge, &c., Ratnapoora, 1863.
RUSSELL, R.—Registrar of the court of chancery and clerk of patents, Jamaica; appointed 1848.
RUSSELL, RICHARD.—Auditor-general of Trinidad; appointed 1861; acted as colonial secretary in 1863.
RYNEVELD, D. J. VAN.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of the Stellenbosch division of Cape Colony; appointed July, 1834.
ST. HELENA, 2nd BISHOP of. Founded 1859.—RIGHT REV. THOMAS EARLE WELBY.—Appointed rector of Newton, Lincolnshire, in 1847, and arch-
deacon of George Town, Cape of Good Hope, 1856; consecrated bishop of St. Helena in 1861.

ST. JOHN, JAMES AUGUSTUS.—Appointed colonial surveyor, Labuan, 1851; justice of the peace, 1852; was superintendent of convicts from April 1853 to Sept. 1860; was appointed acting auditor of the public accounts, Aug. 1861; acting clerk to the legislative council, Feb. 1862; acting police magistrate, Feb. 1863.
ST. PERNE, J. G.—Clerk to the stipendiary magistrate, Savanne, Mauritius, Sept. 1853; appointed district clerk, 1860.
SALMON, J.—President of the legislative council of Jamaica; appointed 1855.

SALMON, J. A.—Was appointed clerk to the commissioners for the settlement of claims to grants of land, Sierra Leone, Dec. 1858; governor of Freetown gaol, 1859; 4th writer colonial secretary's office 1860, and 3rd writer 1861.
SALTWELL, G. R.—Was 15 years in the secretary's office of the general post-office, London; appointed postmaster of Mauritius, June, 1860.
SANDAPA, A.—Appointed clerk in the internal revenue department at Mauritius, April 1839; immigration cashier, 1847; and clerk of direct taxes. August 1854.
SANDERS, EDWARD HOWARD.—Formerly an officer in the Austrian army. Appointed assistant gold commissioner and stipendiary magistrate of the colony of British Columbia, April 1859.

SANDWITH, HUMPHRY, C.B.—Was inspector-general of hospitals in the Turkish service; served with the Turkish army on the Danube in 1853, and was appointed, June 23, 1854, to serve under the orders of Colonel Beatson, and was attached to the staff of Brigadier-general (now Sir William) Williams during the defence of Kars in 1855; was granted, Feb. 7, 1856, her majesty's royal licence and permission to accept and wear the 3rd class of the Imperial Order of the Medjidie, which his imperial majesty the sultan was pleased to confer on him as a mark of his imperial majesty's approbation for his distinguished services before the enemy during the defence of Kars; was appointed a C.B. May 10, 1856; was attached to Earl Granville's embassy to Russia in July, 1856; was granted, April 30, 1857, her majesty's royal licence and permission to accept and wear the insignia of a knight, 5th class, of the Imperial Order of the Legion of Honour, which his majesty the emperor of the French was pleased to confer upon him, as a mark of his imperial majesty's approbation of his distinguished services before the enemy during the late war; was appointed colonial secretary of the Mauritius, Feb. 28, 1857. Resigned.
SANGSTER, C.F.A.—Appointed clerk and accountant to the police department, Hong Kong, 1860; promoted to 2nd clerk in registrar-general's office, 1864.

SANGUINETTI, FREDERICK.—Is a second-class clerk in the office of the financial secretary, Jamaica. Was appointed in Sept. 1863, a clerk in the office of the secretary to the main road commissioners; appointed clerk in the office of the executive committee in Feb. 1864; on change of constitution, appointed to present office by Sir J. P. Grant, K.C.B., 16 Sept. 1866.

SARGEAUNT, W. C.—Appointed clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, Feb. 1848; was permitted whilst on the establishment of that office to hold the appointment of colonial secretary of Natal from 1853 to 1858; was a member of the executive and legislative councils of that colony, a justice of the peace for

the same, and lieut.-colonel of the Natal carbiniers, a corps of cavalry yeomanry; returned to the colonial office in 1858, and was appointed acting lieut.-governor of St. Vincent in 1859; returned to the colonial office, and was promoted an assistant clerk, 1860; appointed crown agent for the colonies, 1862.

SAUNDERS, F.—Appointed to the customs' department, Liverpool, 1823; removed to Halifax, Nova Scotia, 1825; St. Andrew's, New Brunswick, 1828; collector of customs, St. George's, Bermuda, 1833; comptroller of customs, Ceylon, 1836; principal collector of customs, Ceylon, 1843; and treasurer of that colony, 1861; member of the executive and legislative councils; retired on a pension, 1865.

SAUNDERS, F. R.—Appointed assistant surveyor, Ceylon, 1857; acting assistant agent, Trincomalee, July, 1860, in which appointment he was confirmed, 1862; acting landing-surveyor, Colombo; resumed duties at Trincomalee, 1862; again acting land surveyor, Colombo, 1863; acting assistant government agent at Kaigalle, 1864; promoted to be assistant to government agent at Kandy, August, 1865; acting district judge, &c., Kornegalle, Aug. 1865; district judge, &c., Ratnapoora, continuing to act at Kornegalle, Nov. 1865.

SAUNDERS, ROBERT.—Senior member of the executive council, Montserrat; appointed landing waiter and searcher to the customs' department, St. Kitt's, 1828; removed to Montserrat as collector of the customs in 1840, which office he held until 1855 (when the imperial customs were abolished); he was also the collector of the colonial tariff during that period, he then retired with a pension. Is a non-elective member of the legislative assembly; a justice of the peace and coroner; he has, as senior member of the executive council, administered the government upon four occasions during the absence of the administrator, i. e. from Jan. 28 to Sept. 1860, from May 14 to 30, 1862; from 7 Jan. to 11 April, 1865, and from 26 March, 1866, to
SAVERIMONTOU, LOUIS.—Appointed clerk in civil status office, Port Louis, Mauritius, Feb. 1, 1857.
SAVERIMONTOU, M.—Appointed clerk in the colonial secretary's office, Mauritius, Aug. 1857.
SCHEMBRI, J., L.L.D.—Appointed one of her Majesty's judges of the court of appeal, Malta, Sept. 1860.

SCHOLTZ, C. F.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Oudtshoorn division, Cape of Good Hope; appointed July, 1858; was clerk to the commissioner at Worcester district from 1828 to the date of his present appointment.
SCHONNBERG, D.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of the Piketberg division of the Cape Colony; appointed 1854; is also chairman of the immigration board.

SCHRADER, REV. G.—Was appointed acting colonial chaplain at Nuwera Ellia, Ceylon, Oct. 1852; colonial chaplain, Kandyan district, 1853; colonial chaplain, Trinity Church, Colombo, 1861; St. Paul's Church, Kandy, 1863; returned to his duties at Colombo, 1864; acting colonial chaplain, Galle, 1865.

SCHMIDT, LOUIS EDWARD.—Appointed in Aug. 1861, clerk to stipendiary magistrate, Moka, Mauritius; Sept. 13, 1861, clerk in the colonial secretary's office; July, 1863, corresponding clerk; registrar, April, 1866.
SCOTT, CHAS. W.—Employed as an extra clerk
in the colonial office from 1840; appointed clerk in the registry department in 1852.
SCOTT, J. B.—Appointed extra clerk, audit office, Natal, Sept. 1860.
SCOTT, J. R.—Appointed clerk in the custom-house, Natal, June, 1846; and landing surveyor in that department, April, 1863.

SCOTT, JOHN.—Was employed by the foreign office in the affairs of the North American boundary commission in 1840; was appointed, in 1843, secretary to the boundary commission, established in conformity with the Ashburton Treaty of 1842, to define and mark out on the spot the boundary agreed upon in that treaty, which commission terminated in 1848; was appointed surveyor-general of Labuan in 1848; became acting lieut.-governor and treasurer in 1849; and was appointed lieut.-governor of the colony, Nov. 1850; and lieut.-governor of Natal, March, 1856.
SCOTT, STEPHEN, M.D.—Resident surgeon Public Hospital, British Guinea; is acting medical inspector of estates hospitals.

SEALY, JOHN.—Called to the bar at the Middle Temple, Nov. 1833; was solicitor-general of Barbados, from 1842 to 1846, when he was appointed attorney-general of that colony. Is also chancellor of the diocese; was for many years a member of the house of assembly; resigned his seat, and was appointed a member of council, in 1858.

SELBY, PRIDEAUX.—Appointed a writer in the Ceylon civil service, March, 1865; has held an acting appointment as police magistrate, Point Pedro, since 1863; acting commissioner of requests and police magistrate, Jaffna, Dec. 1865; police magistrate, Chavagacherry, continuing to act at Jaffna, Feb. 1866.

SELF, HENRY MAXWELL.—Was appointed special magistrate at Mauritius, Jan. 1835; stipendiary magistrate, March, 1839; clerk in colonial secretary's office, April, 1841; stipendiary magistrate on mission to Rodrigues, 1841; assistant protector of immigrants, Mauritius, Aug. 1843; stipendiary magistrate, 1844; district magistrate, 1852; and senior district magistrate, Port Louis, Aug. 1857; and law officer of the colony.
SEMPER, J. R.—Queen's counsel, Montserrat; appointed 1844. Is a member of the executive council, and solicitor-general, Antigua.

SENDALL, W. J.—Educated at Christ College, Cambridge, where he was first-class in classics, and also took mathematical honours; graduated, B.A. 1858; appointed assistant master in Queen's College, Colombo, Ceylon, Nov. 1859; inspector of schools, Sept. 1862.
SENNANAYEKE, REV. C.—Appointed colonial chaplain of Morottoo, Korlewelle, Ceylon, Sept. 1852; colonial chaplain, Galkisse and Millagraye, 1861.
SEON, WILLIAM.—Clerk to the police magistrate and petty debt court, Georgetown, British Guiana.
SERRURIER, L. A.—Parliamentary clerk, colonial office, Cape of Good Hope, 1863; has been 20 years in the colonial service.
SEYMOUR, FREDERICK.—President of Nevis, 1853; and superintendent of Honduras and lieut.-governor of the Bay Islands, 1857 to 1863, when he was appointed Governor of British Columbia.
SEYMOUR, JOHN FERRIS.—Appointed a clerk in the colonial secretary's office, at Mauritius, Feb. 1853; senior clerk in the customs' department, Aug. 1860; chief clerk, May, 1863.
SHAND, CHAS. FARQUHAR.—Advocate, LL.D.
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

Edinburgh, took academical honours every year, and repeatedly the first; author of "The Practice of the Court of Session," &c.; was an advocate depute, and counsel for the lords of the treasury, and the hon. commissioners of woods and forests in Scotland; chairman of the united industrial or ragged schools; director of the philosophical institution; and captain in the volunteer rifles, Edinburgh; appointed chief justice of the Mauritius in May, 1860; is first judge of the vice-admiralty court, Mauritius, and receives certain fees as such.
SHARPE, HENRY.—Provost-marshal, Grenada; appointed 1855.
SHARPE, H. E.—Called to the bar at the Middle Temple, 1819; was appointed attorney-general of Barbados, 1826; and chief justice of Saint Vincent, Oct. 1846.

SHARPE, W. E. T.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin, where he obtained a scholarship, and graduated B.A. 1857; appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, 1857; acting assistant to the government agent at Kandy, 1858; assistant government agent, Kurnegalle, 1860; and assistant agent, Kandy, 1861; acting assistant agent at Kaigalle, 1863; acting assistant agent, Matelle, 1864; promoted to district judgeship of Balticaloa, continuing to act at Matelle, Aug. 1865.
SHAW, COMMANDER, R.N.—Private secretary to the governor of Antigua; appointed, 1863.

SHAW, HENRY.—Appointed treasurer of St. Vincent, Feb. 1864; acting clerk of courts and keeper of records, 1850 (Honduras); Queen's advocate, full commission, May 30, 1851; coroner, Feb. 1857; nominee of the Crown in the legislative assembly, 1861; was acting treasurer of St. Vincent from July, 1860, until his confirmation in that appointment in 1864.
SHEARD, HENRY.—Employed for several years in striking dies for Messrs. Watt, Birmingham; appointed die engraver, Hong Kong mint, June, 1865.

SHEPSTONE, T., Sen.—Appointed, Jan. 8, 1835, head-quarter interpreter of the Kafir language at the Cape, and served on the staff of Sir Benjamin D'Urban, governor and commander-in-chief, during the Kafir war of 1835; appointed clerk to the agent general for native tribes on the frontiers of the Cape Colony, 1836; sent to accompany the first military expedition from the Cape to Natal, 1838; created justice of the peace under the Act 6 & 7 Will. IV., to 25th degree south latitude; returned to the Cape Colony in 1839; appointed resident diplomatic agent to the Hlambe tribes, and superintendent of Fingoes at Fort Peddie, on the frontiers of Cape Colony, 1839; appointed diplomatic agent to and over the tribes inhabiting the colony of Natal, 1845; appointed captain-in-chief of the native force in Natal, 1848; appointed to control, revise, and direct the administration of justice among the natives in that colony, according to native law, 1849; proposed and personally carried out a scheme for the taxation of the native population in the colony, which yielded about 10,000 l. , the first year, and now amounts to upwards of 16,000 l. per annum; appointed judicial assessor, Natal, under ordnance No. 1, 1855; was captain of the native police corps, Natal, from its appointment in 1848 to 1851, when it was disbanded; and has been engaged on various special missions and services in the colony not here enumerated; appointed secretary for native affairs, 1856; and member of the executive and legislative councils in that colony, 1856.
SHEPSTONE, JOHN.—Assistant magistrate, P. Maritzburg, Natal, 1865.
SHEPSTONE, H.—Appointed private secretary to the lieut.-governor of Natal, March, 1857; and clerk to the executive council in that colony, Aug. 1863; immigration agent, Natal, 1865.
SHEPSTONE, T., jun.—Appointed secretary to the chief justice, Natal, Jan. 1861.
SHEPSTONE, W.—Appointed clerk to the attorney-general, Natal, April, 1859; clerk of the peace, Pietermaritzburg, 1865.
SHERMAN, THOMAS.—Formerly usher and process server of the police court, Hong Kong; promoted to be bailiff and usher of the supreme court, Feb. 1863.
SHERVINGTON, T. MILDMAY.—Attorney-general of Grenada; appointed Sept. 1863.

SHERVINGTON, JOHN.—Entered the treasury department in 1841; appointed clerk, by commission, on July 1, 1852; landing surveyor, Nov. 1861; notary public, July 8, 1862; surveyor and admeasurer of shipping, Dec. 16, 1861; lieutenant in the Antigua militia artillery, May 14, 1858; adjutant, Oct. 18, 1860; captain and acting brigade major, Feb. 8, 1862; major in command and brigade major on the staff, March 27, 1863.

SHERWOOD, W. H., M.D.—Colonial surgeon, Gambia, March, 1859; is a member and clerk of councils; registrar of the courts of appeal and ordinary; has acted for some months as Queen's advocate and police magistrate; and is coroner for Gambia.
SHIER, DAVID, M.D.—Medical inspector of estates' hospitals, British Guiana. Was previously chairman of the board of poor-law commissioners.

SIBBALD, JOHN.—Commander R.N. on half-pay; midshipman in H.M.S. 'Dolphin' on the coast of Africa, 1838; promoted for good services to the rank of lieutenant; first lieutenant to H.M.S. 'Terror' in Sir J. Ross's Antarctic expedition, 1843; was in command of the Dundalk district coast guard service from 1849 to 1854; appointed clerk and registrar of the courts of justice in the Falkland Islands, Nov. 1855.

SIERRA LEONE, 4th BISHOP of.—Dr. E. H. BECKLES.—The diocese of Sierra Leone (patent 22 May, 1852) comprises the colonies and dependencies on the west coast of Africa, between 20° N. and 20° S. latitude, specially the colonies of Gambia, Sierra Leone and Gold Coast.
SILVA, J. M. A.—Clerk to the auditor-general, Hong Kong; appointed 1859. Entered the colonial service at Hong Kong, 1856; clerk in registrar-general's office, 1858.
SIMMS, CHAS.—Chief clerk and registrar of the supreme court, and clerk of the central circuit court of Newfoundland; appointed 1848; retired on pension, 1863.
SIMPSON, J.—Late lieutenant and paymaster of the West York rifle regiment of militia; resigned Dec., 1862; appointed assistant postmaster-general, Hong Kong, Dec. 11, 1862.
SINCLAIR, D.—Appointed public teacher at D'Urban, Natal, June, 1863.

SINGLETON, FRANCIS CORBET.—Clerk of the legislative council, government resident of the Murray district in Western Australia from 1840 to 1847; member of the legislative council of the colony of Western Australia, nominated by the crown, from 1844 to 1847; auditor general of South Australia from May 8, 1847 to Jan. 1851; clerk of the executive council, South Australia, from Dec. 1850 to Dec. 1851; clerk of the legislative council, South Australia, as a nominated
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
council, from Dec. 1850 to Aug. 1851; clerk of the legislative council, under partly-elected council, from Aug. 1851 to Feb. 1857; clerk of legislative council under Constitution Act, from Feb. 1857.
SKEEN, WM. — Appointed government printer, Ceylon, June 15, 1849.

SKINNER, T. — Was formerly a major in the army, joined his regiment in Ceylon in 1819; was selected by Sir Edward Barnes to open a section of the road between Colombo and Kandy, 1820; employed from this time till 1837 on the staff, in laying out lines of communication throughout the colony, and in carrying out a trigonometrical survey and sketch of the mountain zone, comprising the Upper Kandyan provinces, and subsequently the greater portions of the island. The result of these surveys is published in two maps, by the late General John Fraser, deputy quarter-master-general of Ceylon; appointed to the charge of the combined departments of surveyor-general and civil engineer, Ceylon, 1838; commissioner of roads, 1841; to which, in 1850, the civil engineer department was added; acting auditor-general, 1859 and again 1865.

SKINNER, T. F. B. — Appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, Oct. 1860; acting assistant agent, Kurnegalle, April, 1862, in which appointment he was confirmed in the following May; assistant agent at Nuwera Ellia, 1863; acting district judge, Bathialoa, January, 1866; acting district judge, Negombo, April, 1866.

SLADE, JAMES H. — Was secretary to the government agent at Seychelles from 1831 to 1837; clerk to the justice of the peace court, 1836 to 1840; deputy registrar of the court of appeal at Mauritius from 1846 to 1852; appointed registrar of vice-admiralty court in that island, July, 1852.
SMALE, J. — Attorney-general, Hong Kong; appointed 1860; called to the bar, Inner Temple, April, 1842; chief justice Hong Kong, February, 1866.

SMEDLEY, EDWARD GERARD. — Was appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, 1862; acting police magistrate, Upper and Lower Dumbara, 1864; commissioner of requests and police magistrate, Kayts, 1865.
SMIDT, DE A. — Assistant surveyor general, Cape of Good Hope, appointed Nov. 1863; has been in that department since 1848.

SMITH, C. CLEMENT. — Educated at St. Paul's School, and at Corpus Christi College, Cambridge; graduated as B.A. 1862; elected student interpreter, Hong Kong, March, 1862; appointed registrar-general 13th March, 1865; acting police magistrate, June, 1865.
SMITH, DANIEL T. — Receiver-general and treasurer of Turks Islands; appointed 1851. Is a member of the executive council and comptroller of customs.
SMITH, JOHN LUCIE. — Called to the bar, Middle Temple, Nov. 1847; was solicitor-general of British Guiana from 1852 to 1855; appointed attorney-general, 1855; and acting chief justice, 1863.

SMITH, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1860.) — SIR HENRY SMITH. — Speaker of the House of Commons in Canada; knighted by the Prince of Wales at the parliament house, Quebec, under the authority of letters patent granted by the crown.

SMITH, K.C.M.G. and C.B. (Created 1860.) — SIR PETER. — Entered the colonial office, 1810; appointed chief clerk, 1843; retired on pension, 1860; was secretary to the order of St. Michael and St. George.
SMITH, RICHARD CONOLLY. — Clerk in the court of first instance, Aug., 1847; district magistracy clerk, Feb., 1852; appointed clerk to the Rivière du Rempart district, Mauritius, May, 1860.
SMITH, WM. T. — Chief clerk in the receiver-general's office, British Guiana; appointed 1860.
SMYTHE, WILLIAM H. — Chief clerk in customs' department, St. Helena.

SNAGG, SIR WM., KNT. BACH. (Creat. 1859.) — Called to the bar at the Middle Temple, 1829; was appointed solicitor-general of Grenada, May, 1835; attorney-general of Grenada, July, 1847; and chief justice of Antigua and Montserrat, 1856. Knighted by patent.
SORZANO, MANUEL. — Keeper of maps and surveys and escribano of the court of intendant, Trinidad.

SOUTHEY, R. — Treasurer general, Cape of Good Hope, appointed 1861; entered the colonial service at the Cape, 1847; was civil commissioner and resident magistrate of the Swellendam division, Feb. 1850; appointed secretary to the lieut.-governor, March, 1855; acted as colonial secretary from May. 1852, to May, 1854, and from Aug. 1860 to Jan. 1862; appointed colonial secretary, Nov. 1864.

SPALDING, WARNER R. — Formerly held a commission as captain of cavalry during the Crimean war under General Beatson and others; afterwards appointed to the military train, and served in England for several years; appointed stipendiary magistrate at New Westminster, and in April 1859, postmaster-general of British Columbia; still retains his commission as justice of the peace.
SPERLING, R. A. — Appointed assistant civil engineer and commissioner of roads, Ceylon, 1857.
SPITTAL, WILLIAM. — Has been in the customs' department at the Cape since 1837; was appointed landing-surveyor, Aug. 1856.
SPROTT, REV. G. W. — B.A., Glasgow; appointed colonial chaplain, Scots Kirk, Kandy, Ceylon, Sept. 1857; retired from the service, 1866.

SPYKER, J. B. — Accountant, colonial engineer's and road departments, Cape of Good Hope, appointed 1860; was in the government bank from 1837 to 1838; in the audit office from 1838 to 1843; in the road department from 1848.

ST. HELENA, 2ND BISHOP OF. — DR. T. F. WELBY, 1862. — St. Helena was part of the See of Capetown previously to the 6th June, 1859, when it was constituted a diocese, together with the islands of Ascension and Tristan d'Acunha: the population is about 7,000.

STANLEY, Privy Councillor; appointed 1858. — RIGHT HON. EDWARD HENRY SMITH-STANLEY, commonly called LORD STANLEY. — Educated at Rugby, and at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he was 1st class in classics, 1848, taking also mathematical honours; elected M.P. for Lynn Regis, Dec. 1848; appointed a deputy-lieut. of Lancashire, 1848; was under-secretary of state for foreign affairs from March to Dec. 1852; appointed secretary of state for the colonies, Feb. 1858, and "Her Majesty's commissioner for the affairs of India," May, 1858; secretary of state for India and president of the council of India, Aug. 1858, to June, 1859.
STANLEY, WALMSLEY. — Chief resident engineer under the railway department of Mauritius, Nov. 1861.
STARKEY, R. D. — appointed to be a cadet in Hong Kong, Civil Service, July, 1865.
STAVELEY, A. W. — Late captain in Her Majesty's 44th regiment, Dec. 1860; clerk in the
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
colonial secretary's office, Mauritius; Jan. 1860, corresponding clerk in that department, and head clerk in despatch branch, Jan. 1, 1863.
STAWELL, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1857.)— SIR WILLIAM FOSTER STAWELL.—Chief justice of Victoria; knighted by patent.
STEADMAN, JAS.—Postmaster-general, New Brunswick.

STEELE, T.—Appointed a writer in the Ceylon civil service, Sept. 1856; attached to government agent's office, Kornegalle, Nov. 1856; acting assistant government agent at Galle, 1857; acting assistant government agent at Kornegalle, March, 1859; acting assistant government agent at Galle, May, 1859; confirmed Dec. 1859; acting district judge at Chilau, and acting police magistrate and coroner for the same district, and justice of the peace for the North-Western province, Sept. 1863; acting police magistrate at Kandy, 1863; confirmed in that office, May, 1865.

STEPHEN, T. B.—Appointed to the office of the registrar-general, London, 1840; transferred to the customs' department the same year; appointed landing surveyor at Galle, in Ceylon, 1845; assistant collector of customs at Jaffna, 1855; landing surveyor at St. John's River, Ceylon, 1861; deputy collector of customs, and landing surveyor, 1861; acting principal collector, 1863; and is a member of the legislative council whilst so acting; resumed duties, 1864; acting principal collector, 1865; and confirmed in that year.

STEPHEN, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1846.)—SIR ALFRED STEPHEN.—Was called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1823; appointed solicitor-general of Van Diemen's Land in 1825; attorney-general of that colony in 1832; one of the judges of New South Wales in 1839; and chief justice there in 1845, on which occasion he received the honour of knighthood.

STEPHENSON, M. G.—Acting public treasurer and comptroller of navigation laws, Grenada; has been employed in the treasury department since 1853; colonial revenue officer in 1849; chief clerk of the treasury in 1854; and acting treasurer, Feb. 1863.

STERLING, PAUL IVY.—Graduated at Trinity College, Dublin; entered King's Inn, Dublin, and Gray's Inn, London; called to the Irish bar, Michaelmas term, 1829; appointed attorney-general in China, 1843; and one of the judges of the supreme court of Ceylon, 1855. Retired on a pension, 1863.

STEUART, JOHN.—Master of the supreme court of the Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1848; was acting high sheriff from 1856 to 1862; was high sheriff of the colony from 1829 to the date of his present appointment.

STEVENS, JOSIAH.—Entered the Bengal engineers 1852; was present at the siege of Delhi and Lucknow, in 1857; as warrant officer has an Indian medal with two clasps for Delhi and Lucknow; appointed overseer of public works in India, 1858; inspector of works on the Bristol and North Somerset railway, 1863; and in the same grade on the Brighton and South Coast railway, 1864; appointed as inspector of works, Bahamas, September, 1865.

STEVENTON, WILLIAM F., M.D., F.R.C.S., England.—Public medical attendant, Montserrat; formerly on the medical staff of the army; was for some years surgeon to Mining Company's staffs in South America and in Jamaica; author of, 1. "Fixation of the Globe in operations for Cataract." 2. "An Account of the Flora and Fauna, in Lat. 8°10', N., Long. 80°85', W." 3.

"Tropical Infusoria." Appointed, in 1860, public medical attendant of Montserrat.
