Is a member of the executive council, and as such administered the government from the 16th to the 23rd July, 1863, during the temporary absence of Mr. President Robinson; principal medical officer in the public hospital, Jamaica, 1866.
STEWARD, G.—Appointed 4th master, Colombo academy, Ceylon, 1862; 3rd master same year.
STEWART, A., C.B.—Judge of the vice-admiralty court, Nova Scotia, and is a companion of the bath.

STEWART, C. H.—Admitted an advocate of the supreme court of Ceylon, 1846; called to the English bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1863; was appointed deputy Queen's advocate, Jaffna, 1845, and deputy Queen's advocate, Kandy, in 1848; deputy Queen's advocate and one of the law officers of the crown for the island, 1852; allowed private practice; acting district judge, Colombo, from January to June, 1860; acting 2nd puisne judge of the supreme court, 1864.

STEWART, FREDERICK.—Was educated at Aberdeen, when he graduated M.A. with 1st class honours in intellectual and moral sciences in 1859; was appointed inspector of schools, and head master of central school, Hong Kong, Dec. 1861.

STEWART, G.—Held the office of deputy Queen's advocate, western province, Ceylon, 1847; acting commissioner of requests, &c., Bentotte, from 1854 to 1858; appointed commissioner of requests, &c., Harrispattoo, 1858; and acting commissioner of requests, &c., Gampollo, 1861; in which appointment he was confirmed, 1862; acting district judge, Tangalle, 1864; acting commissioner of requests, Colombo, 1865.
STEWART, HENRY COCKBURN.—Appointed private secretary to Sir H. Robinson, Governor, Ceylon, July, 1865.
STEWART, R. H.—Harbour-master, Trinidad; appointed 1838.

STOCK, WILLIAM.—Was clerk in the treasury office at Mauritius, Feb. 1848; clerk in the audit office, Nov. 1850; and appointed second superintendent of distilleries, May 1854; chief superintendent, September, 1862.
STONE, A. H.—Master and registrar of the civil court, and clerk of the peace, Western Australia; appointed 1832; is also commissioner of the court of requests and official assignee.
STONE, G. F.—Attorney-general of Western Australia, Feb. 1860. Is a member of the executive council; was crown solicitor and inspector of weights and measures, 1852 to 1860; and sheriff, 1834.
STONE, JOHN.—Stipendiary magistrate for the Port of Spain, and western district of the county of St. George, Trinidad; appointed 1855.

STORKS, THE RIGHT HON. SIR HENRY KNIGHT, G.C.M.G. and G.C.B.—Entered the army as ensign in 1828; promoted lieutenant, 1832; captain, 1835; major, 1840; lieut.-colonel, 1848; colonel, 1854; served in the 61st, 14th, and 38th regiments; served in the Ionian Islands from 1840 to 1842; proceeded to the Cape of Good Hope on special service in 1846; was appointed assistant adjutant-general, and served in the Kaffir war till its termination in 1848; was mentioned in general orders and promoted to the rank of lieut.-colonel for services in the field; has received a medal for the Kaffir war; in 1849 proceeded to the Mauritius as military secretary; was private secretary to the acting governor in part of 1850-51; appointed to the command of Her Majesty's forces at Smyrna, in Jan. 1855; appointed brigadier-general in July,
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

1855, and removed to Scutari, where he held the command of Her Majesty's forces, and of all the military establishments, from the Bosphorus to Smyrna inclusive, with the rank of major-general; superintended the evacuation of Turkey by the British army at the termination of the Russian war; in 1856 was placed on the list of officers receiving rewards for distinguished services; appointed K.C.B. in Jan. 1857; appointed secretary for military correspondence at the war-office on the 1st of Feb. 1857; appointed in May, 1857, member of the royal commission to inquire into the sanitary condition of the British army; member of the royal commission to inquire into the system of promotion of officers of the higher ranks in the army in 1858; member of a committee to examine and report on the scheme of Sir Charles Trevelyan, for abolishing the purchase of commissions in the army in 1858; was secretary to a secret military committee which sat in 1858, and of which General H.R.H. the Duke of Cambridge was president, to examine into and report on the defences of England; appointed lord high commissioner of the Ionian Islands on the 2nd February, 1859, in succession to the Right Honourable William Gladstone, M.P.; received the grand cross of the order of St. Michael and St. George, from Her Majesty the Queen, on the 2nd February, 1859; governor of Malta, Nov. 1864; governor of Jamaica, December, 1865; and president of the royal commission appointed to enquire into the origin and circumstances of the disturbances in that Island; returned to Malta end of 1866; privy counsellor, November 1866.
STRACHEY, WILLIAM.—Appointed by Earl Grey in 1848, to be précis writer to the Colonial office; was formerly in the Bengal Civil Service.

STRAHAN, LIEUTENANT GEO. C.—Entered the royal artillery, Oct. 1857; was appointed, Jan. 1859, aide-de-camp to the Right Hon. W. E. Gladstone, M.P., when lord high commissioner of the Ionian Islands; and Feb. 17th, was appointed in the same capacity by Sir Henry Storks, G.C.M.G., K.C.B.
STRINGFELLOW, T.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Fort Beaufort, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1850. Was clerk to the civil commissioner at Albany, 1841.
STRUBEN, A. B.—Appointed second clerk to the resident magistrate, D'Urban, Natal, 1858, and first clerk and sub-accountant, D'Urban in that colony, Sept. 1863.
STUART, JOHN.—Clerk of the house of assembly, Newfoundland; appointed 1855. Also secretary to the board of works; appointed 1858.

STUBBS, C. F.—Appointed acting clerk of courts at the Gambia on the 24th June, 1858; acting clerk of customs, 23rd May, 1859; lieut. R. G. artillery; served under colonel d'Arcy in the Baddiboo war, was at the taking of Kinty Canda and Sabba, and the storming of the latter town on the 21st Feb. 1861; appointed postmaster 5th April, 1861; resident manager of British Combo. and justice of the peace, June 1, 1862.
SUEUR, J. A. LE.—Was a clerk in the colonial office at the Cape of Good Hope, 1818; promoted to be first clerk, March, 1839; chief clerk, 1847; and appointed postmaster general, Dec. 1851.

SUEUR, J. J. LE.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of the Worcester division of the Cape Colony; appointed Jan. 1850; was formerly magistrate of the district; appointed in 1828; was collector of taxes at Cape Town, 1841; civil commissioner and resident magistrate of the Swelendam district, 1847.
SUEUR, LE J. J.—First clerk of the Post-office at the Cape of Good Hope; appointed Oct. 1, 1845.

SULLIVAN, AUGUSTUS.—Entered the army, Dec. 1855, and served with Her Majesty's 1st West India regiment, in the West Indies, Bahamas, and the West Coast of Africa; appointed police magistrate and provost-marshal, Oct. 1861.
SULLIVAN, J. A.—Provost-marshal-general, and usher of the black-rod, Jamaica; appointed by letter patent, 1825.

SURTEES, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1859.)—SIR STEPHENSON VILLIERS SURTEES.—Educated at University College, Oxford, where he graduated S.C.L. 1826, B.C.L. 1831; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1831; was for some time advocate-general at St. Lucia; appointed vice-president of the court of appeal in Mauritius, 1835; and was subsequently chief justice of that island, and judge of the vice-admiralty court there; retired on pension, 1860.
SUTHERLAND, ROBERT.—Was appointed stipendiary magistrate in the island of St. Vincent, Sept. 1833.

SUTTON, H. C. MANNERS.—Educated at Harrow and Magdalen College, Cambridge, where he graduated in 1861; appointed private secretary to the governor of Trinidad, Sept. 6, 1864; private secretary to governor of Victoria, 1866.

SUTTON, CHARLES.—Entered the army, 1846; rose to be a lieutenant without purchase in Dec. 1853; acting barrack-master from 1st Sept. 1860, to 23d June, 1862, when he retired; present at the assaults on Sebastopol, on the 18th June and 8th Sept. 1855. Has a Crimean medal and clasp, and Turkish medal; inspector of prisons, Mauritius, 1862; inspector of police, Bahamas, June 1864.

SUTTON, HON. JOHN H. T. MANNERS.—Educated at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he graduated M.A. 1835; was under-secretary of state for the home department from Sept. 1841 to July, 1846; is registrar of the faculty court; was returned M.P. for the borough of Cambridge, Sept. 1839, but unseated on petition; sat for that borough from 1841 to 1847; appointed lieut.-governor of New Brunswick, June, 1854, which government he held until Oct. 1861; appointed April, 1864, to be governor of Trinidad; governor of Victoria, April, 1866.

SWAN, J.—Appointed second assistant colonial secretary, Ceylon, Oct. 1855; acted as principal assistant colonial secretary and clerk to the councils from Feb. 16 to June 1, 1862; and again from December, 1863.

SYDNEY (AUSTRALIA), 2nd BISHOP OF.—Founded 1847.—RIGHT REV. FREDERIC BARKER, D.D.—Educated at Grantham School and at Jesus College, Cambridge, where he graduated A.B. 1829, M.A. 1835, D.D. 1854; became incumbent of Upton, Cheshire, 1830; of St. Mary's, Edge Hill, Liverpool, 1835; and of Baslow, Derbyshire. 1854; consecrated 1854; is author of "A Commentary on Twenty-six Psalms," &c.; is metropolitan bishop in Australia, subject to the general superintendence of the Archbishop of Canterbury. The diocese originally included the whole of Australia, and bore that name; but in 1847 it was restricted to the central portion of the colony, and called by its present title, while additional bishops were nominated for Newcastle, Adelaide, Melbourne, and Perth; by patent, 19th October, 1854. The bishop is a metropolitan bishop, whose suffragans are the bishops of the above dioceses.
SYMONS, C.—Appointed resident magistrate, Freemantle, Western Australia, 1862; has held various offices in the colony since 1839.
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
SYMONS, J. P.—Was employed in various offices under the government of Natal from 1850 to 1855; appointed master of the supreme court, July, 1855, and auditor of that colony, Aug. 1863.
TACHE, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1858.)—SIR ETIENNE PASCHAL TACHE.—Commanding the 2nd military district, Lower Canada; appointed A.D.C. to the Queen, with the hon. rank of colonel in the army, 1860.

TALBOT, CLERE.—Appointed a clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, Oct. 1826; promoted to assistant clerkship, July, 1843; transferred to the war department and appointed a senior clerk, 1854.
TANNER, B.—Appointed manager of Combo, Gambia, June, 1864.
TAP, A. E.—Appointed medical sub-assistant to Ceylon governor, 1828; second class, 1842; first class, 1845; medical assistant, 1858.
TARGET, FELIX N.—Appointed inspector of roads at the Mauritius, May, 1855.
TARLETON, WILLIAM.—Police magistrate, Tasmania, July, 1857; entered the service on March 1, 1842.

TASMANIA, 1st BISHOP OF. Founded 1842.—RIGHT REV. FRANCIS RUSSEL NIXON, D.D.—Educated at St. John's College, Oxford, of which he became a fellow; was 3rd class in classics, 1827; and graduated M.A. 1841; D.D. 1842; is author of a "Visitation Sermon, 1840," and of "Lectures on the Church Catechism." The diocese includes the island of Van Diemen's Land (or Tasmania) and its dependencies; resigned 1864.

TASMANIA, 2ND BISHOP OF. Founded 1842.—RIGHT REV. CHARLES HENRY BROMBY, D.D.—Educated at Uppingham and St. John's College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A., 1837, his name appearing in both triposes, M.A. 1840, D.D. 1864; consecrated 1864; is author of numerous educational works, including "The Church Student's Manual," "History and Grammar of the English Language," 13 volumes of "Papers for the Schoolmaster," &c.; became incumbent of St. Paul's, Cheltenham, 1843; and principal of the Cheltenham Normal College, 1847, in the erection of which he took a prominent part.
TATHAM, R.—Appointed commissioner of the loan board, Ceylon, 1855; and assistant civil engineer and commissioner of roads, 1858.

TAUNTON, 1st BARON. (Creat. 1859.) Privy Councillor; appointed 1835 (Great Britain).—HENRY LABOUCHERE.—Educated at Christ Church, Oxford, where he was 1st class in classics, 1820; and graduated M.A. 1820; was lord of the admiralty from 1832 till 1834; vice-president of the board of trade, and master of the mint, from April, 1835 to March, 1839; under-secretary for the colonies from March to Aug. 1839; president of the board of trade and master of the mint, from Aug. 1839 to Sept. 1841; chief secretary for Ireland, from July, 1846 to July, 1847; again president of the board of trade from July, 1847 to Feb. 1852; secretary of state for the colonies from 1855 to Feb. 1858; elected an elder brother of the Trinity House, 1850; was M.P. for St. Michael's from 1826 till 1830, and sat for Taunton from 1830 to 1859; is a deputy-lieut. of Essex.

TAYLOR, E. B. A.—Was private secretary to Mr. Matthew, when governor of the Bahamas; also to Mr. Gregory and Sir A. Bannerman, whilst holding that office, and to Mr. Bayley; is police magistrate, New Providence, Bahamas.
TAYLOR, HENRY, D.C.L.—Appointed clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, Jan. 1824; promoted senior clerk, 1825; author of
"Philip von Artevelde," "Edwin the Fair," "The Statesmen," "Notes on Life," "St. Clement's Eve," &c.

TAYLOR, RICHARD.—Retired half-pay rifle brigade; entered Cape colonial service, Jan. 1849, as finance clerk to chief commissioner of British Kaffraria; appointed resident magistrate of King William's Town, Aug. 1862; also a member of the Kaffrarian board, justice of the peace, paymaster of levies, member of criminal session court, and president of the same, 1861.

TELF AIR, CHAS.—Stipendiary magistrate, Rivière du Rempart, Mauritius, Oct. 1850; civil commissioner and justice of the peace, 1850; district clerk, 1852; district magistrate, Plaines Wilhems and Moka, 1855; district magistrate, Grand Port, 1857; civil commissioner and government agent, Seychelles, 1858; district magistrate, Grand Port, 1860; and district magistrate, Rivière du Rempart, 1863.

TEMPLE, CHRISTOPHER.—Was educated at Shrewsbury, and Magdalen College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A. in 1833, and M.A. in 1840; called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, Jan. 1836; appointed deputy Queen's advocate, Ceylon, Oct. 1840; district judge, Colombo, June, 1845; acted as one of the judges of the supreme court with scarcely any interruption, from July, 1846 to Oct. 1, 1856, when he was confirmed in the latter office.

TEMPLE, ROBERT.—Commissioner of requests and police magistrate of Madawelletenne and Kurnequalle, Ceylon, June, 1850; acting assistant government agent and police magistrate, &c., at Neuera Ellia, 1853; confirmed in those offices, 1855; acting assistant government agent at Kurnegalle, 1863; acting police magistrate, Galle, April, 1865; deputy fiscal for the Western Province, continuing to act at Galle, October, 1865.

TEMPLER, F. B.—Was formerly a lieut. in the 29th Regt. and sold out of the army in 1845; appointed commissioner of requests, &c. Avishawelle, Ceylon, 1845; assistant agent, Nuwera Ellia, 1849; district judge &c., Caltura, 1852; acting principal assistant colonial secretary and clerk to the councils, May, 1863; acting government agent, &c., Central Province, Dec. 1863; principal assistant colonial secretary, and clerk to the councils; continued to act as government agent, &c., for the Central Province May, 1865.
TEMPLER, GEORGE.—Appointed a writer in the Ceylon civil service, 4th June, 1865; extra assistant government agent, Kandy, 1866.

TEMPLER, P. A.—Appointed writer to the government of Ceylon, Sept. 1863; deputy fiscal, Kandy, 1864; police magistrate at Pantura, Oct. 1865; acting assistant government agent, Trincomalie, Nov. 1865; acting assistant Government agent, Kandy, January, 1866; acting police magistrate, Gampola, April, 1866; assistant collector and landing surveyor, Jaffna, April, 1866; acting assistant government agent, Colombo, July, 1866; acting district judge, Ratnapoora Sept. 1866.

TENNENT, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1845.)—SIR JAMES EMERSON TENNENT, LL.D.—Was educated at Trinity College, Dublin; called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1831, but never practised; was elected M.P. for Belfast in 1832 and 1835; defeated at the general election in 1837, but subsequently seated on petition; returned again at the election in 1841, but unseated on petition; regained his seat in 1842, and held it till 1845; sat for Lisburn in 1852; was secretary to the India board from Sept. 1841 to July, 1845; civil secretary to the colonial government of Ceylon from July, 1845, to
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

Dec. 1850; secretary to the poor law board from Feb. to Nov. 1852; appointed secretary to the board of trade, Nov. 1852; has published "Travels in Greece," "A History of Modern Greece," "Letters from the Ægean," works on Ceylon, &c.; was knighted on proceeding to Ceylon in 1845, and created a knight commander of the Greek order of the Saviour in 1842; is a deputy-lieutenant of Sligo, and was also of Fermanagh, but resigned in 1856; is a magistrate for Antrim and Down.

THIBOU, ISAAC BYAM.—Elected member of the assembly (for the city, Antigua), in 1845, re-elected in 1852, 1859, and 1862; appointed a magistrate and visiting justice of the jail in 1858; a member of the executive council and administrative committee and board of audit in 1863; also a commissioner of the treasury; acted as provost marshal for some months in 1863 and 1864.

THIBOU, J. S.—Assistant clerk, royal engineers' department, Antigua, Aug. 1846, to July, 1854; supernumerary clerk, colonial secretary's office, Aug. 1860; clerk, Oct. 1, 1863; registrar of marriages, 1863; chief clerk, colonial secretary's office, 29th July, 1864.
THIBOU, J. T.—Appointed second clerk, colonial secretary's office, Antigua, 29th July, 1864.
THOMAS, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1859.)—SIR JOHN THOMAS.—Formerly speaker of the house of assembly at Barbados; knighted by patent.

THOMSETT, HENRY G.—Entered the royal navy, 1848, and served actively for twenty-one years; was engaged in a combined attack of British and French naval and land force under Commodore Fanshawe on a horde of pirates at Basis, Jeba River, West Africa, 12th December, 1849; during two periods of service on the African station, assisted in the capture of twenty-seven slave vessels in the bight of Benin and coast of Loando; commanded H.M.'s ship 'Princess Charlotte' at Hong Kong, from February, 1858, to Sept. 1861; acting harbour master, 17th March to 26th Nov. 1860, and again 1st March to the 31st Aug. 1861, when he was appointed harbour-master, marine magistrate, and emigration and customs officer, at Hong Kong, July, 1861, and resigned active service in royal navy.

THOMPSON, EDWARD DEAS, C.B.—Emigrated to New South Wales in 1828, having received from Mr. Huskisson the appointment of clerk of the executive and legislative councils, which he continued to hold until Dec. 31, 1836; on Jan. 1, 1837, he was promoted by Lord Glenelg to the office of colonial secretary and registrar of records of New South Wales, and remained in the exercise of that appointment for nearly twenty years, having resigned it on June 5, 1856, on political grounds, when responsible government was first introduced into the colony. He held a seat in the executive council from Jan. 1, 1837 to Oct. 31, 1859; and in the late legislative council from Jan. 1, 1837 till its dissolution in 1856; since that period he has been a member of the present legislative council or upper house of the legislature; he held a seat in the colonial cabinet, but without office, from Oct. 3, 1856 to Sept. 6, 1857; during which time he was vice-president of the executive council, and in that capacity represented the government in the legislative council; he has been a member of the senate of the Sydney University since its establishment in 1850; and was appointed vice-chancellor of that institution in 1862; he has been a member of the board of trustees of the Australian Museum since its incorporation in 1853; and crown trustee since 1856; in 1856 he
was appointed a civil companion of the most honourable order of the Bath; appointed chancellor of the Sydney University, 1865.

THOMSON, HENRY BYERLY.—Educated at University College, London, and Jesus College, Cambridge; graduated in honour's, 1844; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, May, 1849; is author of "The Law of War," "The Choice of a Profession," and other works; appointed Queen's advocate of Ceylon, May, 1858; acted as puisne judge of the supreme court from April to Nov. 1861. Allowed private practice. Appointed junior puisne judge of Ceylon, Jan. 1863.
THOMPSON, WILLIAM.—Appointed interpreter to the courts, Cape Coast Castle, 1858.
THORNE, JAMES H.—Chief clerk in the provincial secretary's office, Nova Scotia.

THORNHILL, FRANCIS.—Appointed stipendiary magistrate of the parishes of St. James and St. Thomas, Barbados, May, 1838; previous to the termination of the slave apprenticeship; appointed police magistrate for the parish of St. James, Aug. 1839. Acting police magistrate, St. Peter's, April, 1862; police magistrate for St. Michael's, Sept. 1862; acting inspector-general of police, Oct. 1862.
THORNTON, J.—1st clerk in harbour-master and marine magistrate's department, Hong Kong; appointed 1860. Clerk in colonial secretary's office in that colony from 1856 to 1860.
THURSTAN, Rev. J.—Acted as colonial chaplain, Nuwera Ellia, Ceylon, from Oct. 1847 to Feb. 1849; appointed chaplain of Galkisse, 1852; and principal of the industrial school, Colombo, 1861.
THURSTON, JAMES OTTY.—Indoor clerk in the treasury department at St. Kitt's; appointed Dec. 1860.

THWAITES, G. H. K., F.L.S., &c.—Was for some time lecturer on botany and vegetable physiology, at the school of medicine of Bristol; appointed director of the royal botanic gardens, Paradenia, Ceylon, in June, 1859.
TILLEY, S. L.—Provincial secretary, New Brunswick.

TIMMINS, CAPT. O. F.—Entered the army as ensign in the 82nd Regt. on 28th Aug. 1840; promoted to lieut. in May, 1843; and to captain in March, 1850; passed through the senior department of the royal military college with two extra certificates in Nov. 1850; retired from the army in June, 1853; and was appointed captain in Hart's militia on 21st July, 1853; proceeded to Victoria in 1856, as private secretary to His Excellency Sir Henry Barkly, K.C.B., the position he now holds.
TINLEY, THOS.—Appointed July, 1855, under the Master Shipping Act, shipping-master at the Cape.
TINLING, CHAS.—Postmaster at Barbados; appointed 1851. Is also marshal of the vice-admiralty court.
TINLING, I. J.—One of the judges of the court of appeal at Barbados; appointed 1838. Is also inspector of prisons.
TITZCK, S. F.—Stipendiary magistrate, Tobago, appointed 1862; was acting provost marshal from Aug. 1857 to Sept. 1858; appointed escheator and receiver, 1860; is also notary public.

TODD, CHARLES.—Member, British Meteorological Society; fellow of Royal Astronomical Society; assistant astronomer, Cambridge, 1848; assistant astronomer, Royal Observatory, Greenwich, 1854; observer and superintendent of telegraphs, South Australia, 1855.
TODD, REV. G. H.—Rector of the united
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
parishes of St. Anthony and St. Patrick, Montserrat.
TONNOCHY, M. S.—Educated at Blackheath Proprietary School, and Trinity College, Cambridge; elected student-interpreter, Hong Kong civil service, March, 1862; acting sheriff and coroner, June, 1865.

TOMLIN, G.—Formerly a non-commissioned officer in the army, employed in the convict service, Bermuda, in 1859; promoted chief clerk, which appointment he held until 1863; appointed superintendent of the convict hulk, Stonecutter's Island, Hong Kong, Nov. 17, 1863.
TOOKEY, CHARLES.—Assayer to the mint in Hong Kong, appointed 1864.

TORONTO, 1st BISHOP OF. Founded 1839.— RIGHT REV. JOHN STRACHAN, D.D.—Was archdeacon of Upper Canada previous to consecration; is archdeacon of York, rector of Toronto, and principal of King's College, all in Upper Canada; the diocese included the whole of Canada West, but the dioceses of Huron and Ontario have since been taken from it.
TORRENS, R. R.—Was registrar general of lands, South Australia, and a member of the assembly; retired.
TOUSSAINT, A. H.—Appointed medical assistant, Ceylon, 1862.
TOWNSEND, GEORGE PEREGRINE.—Deputy post-master, British Guiana.
TRAPANI, GIO B., LL.D.—Clerk to the council and assistant in secretary's office, Malta; appointed 1859. Has been in the service since 1838.

TRAVERS, FREDERIC T.—Entered the royal artillery, March, 1839; promoted to be captain, 1848; brevet-major, for distinguished services in India, March, 1858; lieut.-colonel, April, 1860; was assistant instructor in surveying and military field-works at the royal military academy, Woolwich, 1847; proceeded to New Zealand in 1849, and commanded the royal artillery in that colony until Nov. 1855; was private secretary to the governor of New Zealand from Oct. 1854 to Sept. 1855; proceeded to the Cape of Good Hope, Nov. 1855, and joined the staff of Governor Sir G. Grey; appointed secretary to the high commissioner, 1856; volunteered for service in India, Aug. 1857, and commanded the royal artillery at the relief of Lucknow and the general action at Cawnpore; received his appointment at the Cape, June, 1858, from which time he performed the duties of military secretary, and secretary to the high commissioner, until Sir G. Grey's appointment to New Zealand; appointed military secretary to Sir P. E. Wodehouse, governor of the Cape, 1861.
TREACHER, J. G.—Colonial surgeon, Labuan; appointed 1848. Member of the legislative council; acting treasurer, 1862.

TRENCH, D. P.—Entered the colonial service on March 14, 1848, as auditor of accounts in the prison department of Jamaica; appointed second commissioner of audit on March 21, 1855; inspector and director of the public hospital and lunatic asylum, Jan. 11, 1859, when that office was first created; and inspector of the revenue department on the creation of that office in March, 1865.
TRILL, S.—Entered Kaffrarian civil service, April, 1856; appointed assistant civil engineer, Feb. 1858; appointed civil engineer, Dec. 1861.
TROTT, JOHN HENRY.—Provost-marshal, general of Bermuda, was appointed by commission, 16 Jan. 1839. He is also receiver of crown quit rents of the colony.

TROTTER, HENRY.—Was private secretary to Major Blackall, when governor of Dominica, from April, 1852, to Nov. 1857, during which period, and up to Feb. 1859, he also held an appointment in the treasury department of that colony; appointed to a clerkship in the commander-in-chief's office, Horse Guards, in Feb. 1859, which he resigned in May, 1861, on being appointed deputy comptroller of the convict establishment at Bermuda; acting comptroller, March, 1862, and as such, had the entire control of that establishment until its dissolution in June, 1863; appointed inspector of post offices in Ceylon, Oct. 1865, after having been qualified for the appointment in the General Post Office.
TROUCHET, LOUIS G.—Clerk in the customs' department, Mauritius, May, 1851; senior stipendiary clerk, Port Louis, Sept. 1858.

TRUTER, JAMES L.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of the Calvinia division of Cape Colony; appointed 1855; was clerk of the peace at Paarl, 1839; clerk to the civil commissioner of the district, and postmaster, 1848.
TRUTCH, JOSEPH W.—Appointed chief commissioner of lands and works, and surveyor-general of British Columbia, May, 1864; is a member of the executive and legislative councils of that colony.
TUCKER, JAMES.—Assistant colonial secretary, Bermuda; appointed 1859.

TUCKER, L. G.—Appointed inspector of police, Essequibo, British Guiana, June, 1853, and justice of peace at the same time; acting commissary of taxation, 1855; inspector of police, July, 1856 to June, 1857; promoted to be a stipendiary magistrate and special justice of the peace, and served as such, in district E. Demerary, and in the Rio Demerary district, till Feb. 1859; acting police magistrate, Georgetown, and pro-sheriff for a short time; promoted, Feb. 1859; commissary of taxation, inspector of weights and measures, and inspector of roads and bridges, Berbice; served as such up to Jan. 1864, when his office was abolished, and he was appointed assistant government secretary and assistant receiver-general for Berbice.
TUDOR, J. EARLE.—Justice of peace, St. Christopher, 1833; member of council, 1847; surveyor of roads, 1852; executive council, 1858; president administering government, Aug. 29, to Oct. 12, 1862.
TULLY, WILLIAM.—Appointed inspecting-surveyor, Tasmania, Jan. 1859.
TUPPER, CHARLES, M.D.—Provincial secretary, Nova Scotia; and a member of the executive council.
TURNER, A. H.—Writer, Ceylon civil service, 1866; acting assistant government agent, Trincomalie, in the same year.
TUZO, THOS. S.—Clerk of the councils and registrar in chancery, Bermuda; appointed 1849.

TWYNAM, W. C.—Appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, 1845; assistant government agent at Jaffna, 1848; acting assistant government agent, commissioner of requests, and police magistrate, Matelle, 1852; resumed duties at Jaffna, 1854; assistant government agent, &c., Hambantotte, 1854; assistant government agent, district judge, &c., &c., Manaar, 1856; superintendent of pearl fisheries, 1862.

TYLER, K.H. (Creat. 1833); Knt. BACHEL. 1838.—SIR GEORGE TYLER.—Entered the navy in 1806; in 1811, when engaged in the boats of H.M.S. 'Spartan,' in cutting out French vessels from under the batteries in Quiberon Bay, was severely wounded, and lost his right arm, for
Digitized by
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

which he receives a pension of £200 a year; became a vice-admiral in 1857; was lieut.-governor of St. Vincent's from 1833 to 1840; received the Guelphic order on proceeding to St. Vincent's; was knighted for his conduct as governor during the general emancipation of the negroes; was M.P. for Glamorganshire from Feb. 1851 to April, 1857.
UNWIN, W.—Appointed clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, July, 1825; promoted assistant senior clerk, Aug. 1839; retired 1866.

USHER, HENRY CHAS.—Appointed clerk in the colonial secretary's office, Honduras, June, 1859; clerk in public treasury, July, 1865; and has been, from June, 1866, performing the duties of treasurer of that colony.
VAN BREDA, A. J.—Teller, treasury, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1854; was in the high sheriff's office from 1840 to that date.
VAN CUYLENBURG, P. H.—Appointed medical sub-assistant to the government of Ceylon, 1835; second class, 1843; medical assistant, 1858.
VANDERSTRAATEN, J. L., M.D.—Medical assistant, Ceylon, 1863.
VANDORT, W. G., M.D.—Medical assistant, Ceylon, 1864.

VANE, GEORGE.—Served in the royal navy, from June, 1831 to March, 1834, and in the Foreign office from April to August, 1834; appointed to the imperial customs' department at Liverpool, 12 August, 1834; ditto at London, 1839; comptroller of customs at Jaffna, Ceylon, August, 1846; comptroller of customs, Colombo, Sept. 1848; appointed deputy comptroller, on transfer from the imperial service to the civil service, Jan. 1853; principal comptroller of customs, May, 1861; superintendent of the pearl fisheries, 1857 to 1862; acting treasurer, July, 1862 to July, 1864; comptroller of customs, July, 1864; acting treasurer, May, 1865; confirmed, Sept. 1865; is member of the executive and legislative councils, and justice of the peace for the island.
VAN KINSCHOT, WILLIAM.—Accountant in registrar's department, at British Guiana.
VAN LIER, H. R.—Chief clerk, audit office, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1860; has been in that office from 1837.
VAN SCHELLEBECK, H. C.—Was registrar's clerk at Mauritius, 1847 to 1852; clerk to the first puisne judge, 1852 to 1857; appointed district clerk, Port Louis, May, 1857.
VARIAN, HENRY.—Appointed commander of Ceylon government steamer 'Pearl,' 1863.
VAUGHAN, JAMES THOS.—Commissary of taxation, county of Essequibo, British Guiana; superintendent of rivers and creeks, Berbice, 1863.

VELLA, GIOVANNI.—Entered the colonial service, May, 1827; was a clerk in the land revenue office and chief clerk in comptroller of contracts' department, and in the treasury, Malta; appointed collector of land revenue in that colony, Jan. 1859. Is ex-officio member of council.
VELLA, PAOLO, LL.D.—Appointed a magistrate of judicial police, Malta, July, 1859.
VESCONTES, ISAAC LE.—Financial secretary, Nova Scotia; is a member of the executive council.
VESEY, NATHANIEL, T.—Harbour-master and shipping-master, George Town, British Guiana.
VETCH, G. A.—Was appointed to the surveyor-general's department, Ceylon, 1857; appointed assistant surveyor.
VICTORIA (HONG KONG), 2ND BISHOP OF.—RIGHT REV. CHARLES R. ALFORD, D.D.—For many
years principal of the metropolitan training institution, Highbury. Appointed Dec. 1866.

VIGORS, P.—High sheriff, Cape of Good Hope, Oct. 1862; was appointed clerk in the customs' department at the Cape, Nov. 1846; clerk, colonial office, May, 1849; promoted to be chief clerk, June, 1857.
VINCENT, G.—Appointed draughtsman, survey department, Western Australia, 1863.
VIRAPA, ANDRE.—Appointed chief clerk in the colonial commissariat at Mauritius, March, 1842.

VIVIAN, LIEUT.-COLONEL JOHN L.—Formerly in the army; was lieutenant in the 3rd royal Surrey regiment of militia, from Oct. 1853 to June, 1855; served, during the Russian war, with the Osmanli cavalry under the command of Major-General Smith; is a lieutenant-colonel in the Turkish army; was superintendent of the Carmarthenshire county constabulary; lieutenant in the royal London regiment of militia, from Dec. 1857 to June, 1859; was appointed in June, 1859, superintendent of police for the island of St. Christopher; is a justice of the peace for the colony; ex-officio police magistrate for the rural districts; and a lieutenant-colonel in the militia protective force; chief constable of Swansea, 1863.

WADE, THOMAS FRANCIS.—Was appointed an ensign in the 81st regiment, November 2, 1838; exchanged into the 42nd Highlanders, August 23, 1839; was promoted to be lieutenant, November 16, 1841; exchanged into the 78th Highlanders, November 17, 1841; and sold out June 22, 1847, having served in China, at Chin Kiang Fu, and Nanking in 1842; acted as interpreter in the Canton dialect to the supreme court of Hong Kong in 1843; was appointed assistant Chinese secretary in 1847; vice-consul at Shanghae, 1852; acted as inspector of customs for the Chinese government at Shanghae in 1854; was appointed Chinese secretary at Hong Kong, Nov. 1855; was attached in that capacity to the Earl of Elgin's mission to China in 1857, 1858, and 1859; was appointed Chinese secretary to the mission in China, Aug. 29, 1859; was attached as Chinese secretary to the Earl of Elgin's special embassy to China in 1860; was present at the operations which took place at Pehtang and at the Taku forts on the Peiho; he accompanied the cavalry brigade from the Peiho to Tientsin.
WAKEFORD, H.—Was private secretary to the governor of Western Australia, and clerk of the councils, from 1855 to 1861, when he was appointed police magistrate of Perth.
WALBANK, M. W.—Chief clerk and registrar of the supreme court, and clerk of the central circuit court of Newfoundland; appointed 1863. Is a member of the local bar.

WALCOTT, ROBERT B., M.D.—Educated at Reading; graduated at the University of Paris as B.L. in 1838, and at the University of London as M.B. in 1845, and as M.D. in 1862; appointed one of the public vaccinators of the Island of Barbados, Aug. 1850, and acted as such until the office was abolished in Dec. 1853; appointed one of the district medical attendants to the police force, July 1854; as acting coroner for the district of St. Joseph and St. Andrew, of said island, May, 1855; and as full coroner for the same district, July 1855, on the death of the former coroner; was one of the commissioners of Census, 1861; is medical lecturer, &c., at Codrington College, and medical attendant of poor at St. Joseph's parish; holds commissions as surgeon to the 11th regiment of Barbados Militia, 1854, and
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
as assistant surgeon to the regiment of Barbados Yeomanry Cavalry, 1860.

WALCOTT, STEPHEN.—Was appointed civil secretary to the government of Canada, 1835; secretary to the colonial land and emigration commissioners, London, March, 1840; secretary and 2nd commissioner, July, 1860.

WALES, CAPT. DOUGLAS.—Harbour-master at Port Louis, Mauritius; appointed 5th Feb. 1856; formerly captain in the mercantile navy. Receives fees as surveyor to the vice-admiralty court, as government surveyor under passenger act, and fees as admeasuring officer.

WALKER, C. P.—Assistant government agent for eastern province, Ceylon, 1837; assistant government agent for the southern province, Hambantotte, 1838; district judge, Colombo, 1843; assistant agent, Ratnapoora, 1846; assistant agent for northern province, and district-judge, Manaar, 1847; district judge, &c., of Galle, 1854.

WALKER, JAMES, C.B. (Creat. 1862.)—Was for several years a clerk on the establishment of the colonial office, Downing Street; appointed registrar and clerk of the courts in Honduras, 1837; treasurer of Trinidad, 1839; and acted as colonial secretary of that island from June, 1839, to Sept. 1840; accompanied Sir Henry Macleod as secretary on a special mission to British Guiana, 1840-41; was appointed colonial secretary of Barbados in 1842; administrator of the government of Grenada, 1856; of the government of St. Vincent, 1857; of the government of Barbados and the Windward Islands, 1859; and lieut.-governor of Trinidad, 1860; appointed C.B. 1860; and governor-in-chief of the Windward Islands, 1861.

WALKER, WILLIAM.—Government secretary, and secretary to the court of policy, British Guiana, 1847; holds the commission of lieut.-governor since 1847; has administered the government four times, namely, from May, 1848 to Feb. 1849, from May, 1853 to March, 1854, from July, 1857 to May, 1858, and from May, 1861 to Jan. 1862. Entered the service, Jan. 1836, as private secretary to lieut.-governor Colebrooke in Bahamas; was also auditor of the public bank, and secretary to the board of public instruction; was member of the house of assembly for the district of Exuma; rejoined Sir William Colebrooke at Antigua, Dec. 1837; was first inspector of prisons, and stipendiary magistrate, of Antigua and Barbuda, and subsequently provost-marshal; in 1860 represented the colony as delegate at the International Statistical Congress in London; in August, 1862, was sent to Washington on a special mission relative to the introduction of liberated slaves from the United States to the colony and received the thanks of the court for the satisfactory manner in which that duty was performed; retired on pension, 1866.
WALLACE, J.—Clerk in the receiver-general's office, Nova Scotia.
WALMSLEY, J.—Government agent on the borders of the Zulu country, Natal, 1854.

WALPOLE, JOHN.—Was appointed a clerk in the colonial office, March, 1830; assistant to agent-general for emigration, April, 1838; and assistant secretary to the colonial land and emigration commissioners. January, 1840. Retired on a pension, March, 1864.
WALTLEY, GEORGE.—Provost-marshal at St. Christopher; appointed 1851.
WAMBEEK, J., Lic. Med. and Surg. Bengal College.—Appointed medical assistant to the government of Ceylon, 3rd class, 1843; 2nd class,
1845; 1st class, 1853; assistant colonial surgeon, 1858; appointed, in addition, in 1862, superintendent of civil medical duties at Jaffna; retired on a pension, 1866.

WARBURTON, PETER E.—Entered royal navy, 1826; entered Hon. East India Company's service, 1831; assistant adjutant-general in India, 1839; from 1839 to 1853 filled the posts of deputy adjutant-general of the army, deputy judge-advocate general, assistant quarter-master, general of a division; appointed commissioner of police for the province of South Australia, 1853.
WARD, E. W.—Captain royal engineers; appointed April 26, 1853, deputy master of royal mint, and chief officer of the branch mint at Sydney.
WARD, SWINBURNE.—Was private secretary to Sir H. Ward, while governor of Ceylon; appointed civil commissioner of Seychelles, Oct. 1862.
WARDE, H. LIONEL.—Appointed private secretary to Sir Charles Darling Victoria, in Nov. 1863; formerly held an appointment in the inland revenue department, Somerset House.
WARE, WALTER HOWARD—Stipendiary magistrate of British Guiana; appointed 1837. Was previously in the government secretary's office.
WARNER, C. W., C.B.—Attorney-general, Trinidad; appointed 1844.
WARNER, T.—Registrar of the courts, Trinidad; now acting as emigration agent for Trinidad at Calcutta.
WARREN, CHARLES.—Entered the customs' department at the Mauritius, June, 1842; appointed tide-surveyor, Oct. 1848.
WARREN, JOHN H.—Surveyor-general and chairman of board of works, Newfoundland; appointed 1861.
WARREN, LEWIS.—District-commissary of taxation, British Guiana.
WATERMEYER, E. B.—Called to the bar at the Inner Temple, May, 1847; appointed a puisne judge at the Cape of Good Hope, May, 1858.
WATKINS, J. J. ASTON.—Registrar of the supreme court, registrar of the vice-admiralty court, and registrar of births, deaths, and marriages, in Tasmania, Nov. 23, 1860.
WATSON, HENRY.—Acting administrator-general, Demerara and Gosequibo, 1863; has been acting clerk of Inferior Court of Criminal Justice.
WATSON, ALEXANDER.—Was formerly accountant in the bank of British North America; appointed acting treasurer of Vancouver's Island, Dec. 1861; confirmed in the appointment, April, 1852.
WATSON, CARL.—Third clerk in the Treasury, St. Kitts, 25th November, 1864.
WATSON, EDWARD HUGH.—Sub-commissary of taxation, British Guiana.
WATT, REV. J.—Appointed colonial chaplain of the Scot's Kirk, Kandy, 1866.
WATTERS, CHARLES.—Solicitor-general, New Brunswick.

WATTLEY, JOS. K.—Clerk of assembly, Dec. 1852; puisne judge, Nov. 1856; acting chief justice, Oct. and Nov. 1861; again, Jan. 1862; again, Oct. 1862; acting solicitor-general, Nevis, Oct. 1861; was formerly registrar in admiralty, St. Kitt's; Queen's counsel, May, 1861; is secretary to board of health, and to water commission. Has held various honorary appointments; mayor of Basse-terre, Feb. 1862; acting chief justice and judge of complaint court from Aug. 1863. Is a barrister.
WATTS, FREDERICK.—In 1836 was placed in the commission of peace for Barbados, for the purpose
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

of assisting the stipendiary magistrates under the Emancipation Act to appraise the unexpired services of the apprenticed labourers; acted as general justice of the peace until 1839, when he was appointed acting police magistrate of St. George, coroner of St. Philip from 1839 to 1841; police magistrate of St. Philip, 1841; in 1842 was elected member of assembly for St. Philip; retired in consequence of an act passed which prohibited his representing the constituency he registered; in 1861 sat as acting judge of the assistant court of appeal; is major in the Barbados yeomanry cavalry.
WAY, BENJAMIN.—Chief magistrate of Lagos; appointed Aug. 1863.
WEARING, WILLIAM A.—B.A., St. John's College, Cambridge; barrister-at-law, Lincoln's Inn, 1847; crown solicitor of the province of South Australia, 1856.

WEBB, J. H.—Was appointed inspector of public schools in the Bahamas in 1847; is acting secretary to the board of education, and a justice of the peace for the colony; appointed, 1865, registrar of records of Courts of Ordinary and Chancery in the Bahamas.

WEBB, NATHANIEL.—Entered the colonial service in 1838 as clerk of the inferior court, Bahamas; appointed assistant private secretary to Major-General Sir Francis Cockburn, governor of the Bahamas, in 1839, and continued to hold that office until 1843; promoted to be private secretary, 1844; appointed junior clerk to Legislative Assembly, 1840; served in New Providence Militia till that corps was disbanded; appointed superintendent of New Providence Asylum, 1846; member of the legislative assembly, 1863.
WEBB, YARICK—Landing waiter and searcher, Bahamas, July 20, 1849.

WEBBE, G.—Was educated at Eton and Christchurch, Oxford; was chief justice of Nevis from 1829 to 1847; appointed treasurer in 1850; is also judge of the court of complaints, and chief loan commissioner of that island; and president of the legislative council. Retired.
WEDGEWOOD, E. H.—Appointed, after a competitive examination, a clerk in the office of secretary of state for the colonies, April, 1860; promoted to 1st junior class, 1 Oct. 1866.

WELLINGTON (NEW ZEALAND), 1st BISHOP of. Appointed 1858.—CHARLES JOHN ABRAHAM.—Formerly archdeacon of Waitemata (1852); educated at King's College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A. 1837, M.A. 1840; was for some years assistant master at Eton.

WELLS, JOHN.—Clerk of the assembly, Grenada; appointed 1851; first appointment under the government, 1847; acted as special stipendiary magistrate during the absence of Admiral Hutcheson until that gentleman's resignation in 1861.
WELLS, WM., M.D.—Medical officer and coroner for the parish of St. David, Grenada; is a member of assembly and speaker.

WET, DE, J. P.—Chief clerk, surveyor-general's department, Cape of Good Hope; appointed May, 1855; was in the department of the protector of slaves, 1831 to 1833; in the government bank, 1833 to 1841, and in his present department since that time.
WHITE, JOHN.—Chief clerk of gold commissioners' office, Cariboo, British Columbia; appointed March, 1863; formerly of the general register office, Somerset House.
WHITE, WILLIAM.—Appointed a clerk in the

General Post Office, St. Martin's-le-Grand, Feb. 1846; resigned April, 1854; appointed a clerk in the General Post Office, Quebec, Canada, Dec. 1, 1854; promoted to be secretary of the General Post Office in Canada, Jan. 21, 1861.

WHYTE, JOHN CHARLES.—Graduate of Trinity College, Dublin, where he took honours in classics; called to the bar of Ireland, March, 1847; appointed second police magistrate of Hong Kong, August, 1862; acted as judge of the court of summary jurisdiction in that colony from April, 1863, to October, 1864, again from April, 1865, to the present time; appointed a provisional member of the legislative council in June, 1865.

WIGLEY, F. S.—Queen's counsel, St. Kitt's, 1832; chief justice Virgin Islands, 1835; member of legislative counsel, St. Kitt's, on resignation as chief justice at Tortola; member of executive council, 1855; solicitor-general, St. Kitt's, 1859; attorney-general, and member of administrative committee, 1860; police magistrate, Basseterre, Sept. 16, 1865.

WILDE, W.—Called to the bar at Gray's Inn, June, 1822, of which inn he is one of the benchers; appointed chief justice of St. Helena, 1836; is also judge of the vice-admiralty court, and member of the council of that island.

WILDMAN, EDWARD T.—Appointed clerk in colonial secretary's office, South Australia, 1850; clerk, engineer department, 1851; colonial secretary's office, 1852; chief clerk, waste lands' office, 1857; chief secretary's office, 1858; secretary to commissioner of crown lands and immigration, 1859.

WILDMAN, JAMES LUSHINGTON.—Private secretary to the governor of Trinidad, 1849; collector of customs in that island, 1853 to 1855; appointed senior stipendiary magistrate, Port Louis, Mauritius, Nov. 1860.
WILKIN, WM.—Member of the committee of public works, Montserrat; appointed 1863.
WILKINS, L. M.—One of the puisne judges in Nova Scotia.
WILKINS, V.—Was police magistrate at St. Lucy, Barbados, 1853-6.

WILCOCKS, E. J. R.—Served an apprenticeship of five years as pupil teacher at the Ridgway District National School; Queen's scholar in the Diocesan training school at Exeter; in 1856 was appointed 4th class schoolmaster at Bermuda, and was eventually promoted to be principal; in Jan. 1864, was appointed assistant master of central school, Hong Kong.
WILLIAMS, C. H.—Appointed resident magistrate, Tugela division, county Victoria, Natal, 1853.

WILLIAMS, G. S.—Educated at St. John's College, Oxford; and was appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, 1861; acting assistant government agent, Colombo, 1862; commissioner of requests and police magistrate at Point Pedro, Jan. 1865; assistant government agent, Kandy, and acting district judge at Ratnapoora, Nov., 1865.

WILLIAMS OF KARS, 1st BART. (Utd. Kgdm.) Creat. 1856; K.C.B. (Military) 1856; C.B. (Civil) 1852.—SIR WILLIAM FENWICK WILLIAMS, educated at the royal military academy at Woolwich; entered the artillery as 2nd lieut. 1825, and became capt. in 1840; employed in Turkey from 1840 to 1843 as British commissioner at the conferences preceding the treaty signed at Erzeroum in 1847; was British commissioner for the settlement of the Turko-Persian boundary, June, 1848; received the brevet of colonel in that year for his military and diplomatic services; in Aug. 1854, while the British army was at Varna, he was appointed British com-
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

missioner with the Turkish forces, and received the local rank of brigadier-general in Dec. 1855; was granted a pension of 10000L. a-year for life, a baronetcy, the order of the Bath, the Turkish order of the Medjidie of the 1st class, the freedom of the city of London and a sword, the honorary degree of D.C.L. from Oxford, &c., for his gallant defence of Kars in 1855; appointed commandant at Woolwich, June, 1856; M.P. for Calne, July, 1856; governor of Nova Scotia, 1865.
WILLINGTON, J.—Assistant justice at Tobago; appointed 1859; is president of the legislative council.
WILLIS, RICHARD H.—Appointed collector of customs, Launceston, Tasmania, Aug. 1861.

WILLISFORD, F. C.—Was appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, 1862; acting commissioner of requests and police magistrate of Upper and Lower, Doombera, 1862; acting court of requests, Avishawalle, 1863; commissioner of requests at Calpentyn, June, 1864; landing and tide-surveyor at Galle, May, 1865; and acting collector of customs, southern province, July, 1865, acting landing surveyor, Colombo, June, 1866.
WILMOT, ROBERT C. E.—Clerk of executive council, Oct. 1, 1852; and clerk of legislative council, Tasmania, Nov. 1, 1856.
WILSON, C.—Appointed assistant surveyor, Ceylon, Nov. 1844; retired on a pension, 1863.
WILSON, H.—Port-captain at Table Bay, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1862; was assistant port captain from 1848.
WILLSON, R. M.—Acting superintendent of police, St. Kitts, April, 1856, till June, 1857; drill instructor July, 1860, till Dec. 1862; inspector of police (provisional), July, 1863.

WILSON, S. H. K.—Secretary to chief justice J. Wilson at the Mauritius, 1854; joined H.M.'s 85th light infantry in August, 1855; lieut. April, 1857; acted as paymaster, quartermaster, and adjutant from April, 1859, to Sept. 1861; appointed aide-de-camp to Gen. Wynyard, Oct. 1861, then acting governor of the Cape; exchanged to 96th regt. in 1864; garrison adjutant, King William's Town, Br. Kaffraria, June, 1864 to Aug. 1864; district adjutant of Natal, Sept. 1864; private sec. to Lieut.-governor Maclean, C.B., 31st Dec. 1864; and clerk to the executive council, 31st Dec. 1864.

WILSON, W., C.E.—Assistant engineer on the East Indian railway from 1859 to 1863; appointed assistant surveyor-general in Hong Kong, June, 1863; elected associate of institution of civil engineers, 1863; appointed surveyor-general of Hong Kong, 1865.

WING, CHARLES.—Was appointed clerk in the custom-house, London, Jan. 1838; clerk in the customs at Mauritius, April, 1839; acting collector, April, 1857; and appointed collector of customs in that island, Oct. 1858; is a member of the legislative council and comptroller of customs and navigation laws.
WINZER, J.—Served for 16 years on the ordnance, transferred to the surveyor-general's department, Ceylon, 1856; appointed assistant surveyor, 1859.

WISE, REV. J., B.A.—Appointed colonial chaplain, Nuwera Ellia, Ceylon, June, 1848; acting chaplain, St. Peter's Church, Colombo, 1852; colonial chaplain, St. Paul's Church, Kandy, 1853; appointed commissary of the diocese of Colombo in the absence of the bishop, 1861; archdeacon of Colombo, 1862; retired on a pension, 1864.
WODEHOUSE, SIR PHILIP E., K.C.B. (Creat. 1862.)—Entered the Ceylon civil service as a writer, May, 1828; promoted to be assistant colo-

nial secretary and clerk of the executive and legislative councils, Oct. 1833; district judge of Kandy, 1840; government agent for the western province, 1843; was appointed superintendent of Honduras, 1851; in Feb. 1854, governor of British Guiana; was employed in 1858 on a special mission to the government of Venezuela; and in 1861 was appointed governor of the Cape of Good Hope; is also high commissioner in South Africa.

WODEHOUSE, W. H.—Educated at Trinity College, Cambridge; appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, Oct. 1858; commissioner of requests, &c., Chavagacherry, 1859; assistant agent, Trincomalie, 1860; 2nd temple land commissioner, June, 1860; was also private secretary to puisne and chief justice from 1853-6.
WOEKE, M. H. V.—Chief clerk of deeds registry, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1845; has been in that department since 1831.
WOLF, THOMAS DE.—Clerk to the financial secretary. Nova Scotia.

WOLFENDEN, RICHARD.—Appointed Oct. 26, 1863; superintendent of government printing office, British Columbia, a non-commissioned officer of the corps of the Royal engineers; served five years as a clerk in the lands and works department; was superintendent of the printing department under Col. Moody.

WOLFF, SIR HENRY DRUMMOND, K.C.M.G.—Entered the foreign office in 1846; was attached, 1852, to Her Majesty's legation at Florence, where he served for a short time as acting chargé d'affaires; was attached, 1856, to the Earl of Westmoreland's special mission to Brussels to congratulate the king on the twenty-fifth anniversary of his accession, and with Lord Westmoreland accompanied the king on part of his progress through the kingdom; was appointed by royal decree a knight of the order of Leopold of Belgium, but was not allowed by the queen to accept the decoration, it not being conferred for military services; was for some time an officer in the 1st or western regiment of Norfolk militia; was appointed in Feb. 1858, assistant private secretary to the Earl of Malmesbury, secretary of state for foreign affairs; in Nov., private secretary to the Right Hon. Sir Edward Bulwer Lytton, secretary of state for the colonies; was named by the Queen in April, 1859, C.M.G., and appointed to the honorary office of king of arms of St. Michael and St. George; was appointed in June, 1859, public secretary to the lord high commissioner of the Ionian Islands; in the same year a member of a commission presided over by the lord high commissioner, to inquire into the state of public departments; in 1860, was named member of a commission to inquire and report on the state of the Ionian finances; was delegated to represent the Ionian government at the international statistical congress held in London in 1860; was appointed, 1861, by the Ionian government, vice-president of a commission to organize, in 1862, an exhibition of the works of Ionian art and industry, and in the same year member of a commission to inquire into and prepare a project of law for regulating the system of public instruction in the Ionian Islands; was commissioner for the Ionian Islands at the International Exhibition of 1862; and was created K.C.M.G.
WOOD, FREDERICK A. PERCY.—A captain in the royal marines, on half-pay; appointed receiver-general of the settlements on the Gold Coast, 1861.
WOOD, READER, J.—Colonial treasurer and
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
commissioner of customs, New Zealand; is a member of the executive and legislative councils.
WOOD, W.—Appointed tide-waiter, customs' department, Natal, 1854, and 2nd landing-waiter, 1860; and 1st landing-waiter in that department April, 1863.

WOODFORD, G.C.B. (Creat. 1852); G.C.M.G. 1832.—SIR ALEXANDER GEORGE WOODFORD.—Served in Holland and Denmark; was aide-de-camp to Lord Forbes on the staff in Sicily in 1809, and subsequently with the Coldstream guards in Portugal and Spain; was at Waterloo in command of that regiment, at the capture of Paris, and at Cambrai during the three years' occupation of France; became a general in 1854, and colonel of the 40th foot in 1842; received a medal and two clasps for his services at Salamanca, Vittoria, and Nive; was lieut.-governor of Malta in 1825; was subsequently commander of the forces in the Ionian Islands; was governor of Gibraltar from 1835 to 1842; appointed lieut.-governor of Chelsea Hospital, Nov. 1856; received the orders of Maria Theresa and St. George, 4th class, for his services at Waterloo; was aide-de-camp to George IV.
WOODGATE, A.—Deputy postmaster, Nova Scotia.

WOODS, WILLIAM WHITFIELD.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin; entered the public service in 1828 as a clerk in the education office, Ireland; in 1836 was appointed to the office of the ecclesiastical commissioners, Ireland; and in 1844 to the office of public works, Ireland, and superintended the relief department under that board during the famine of 1845-46; and, subsequently, the land improvement department in 1856; on the abolition of the office he was transferred to the treasury department, London; and in 1859 was appointed assistant librarian to the colonial office.
WOODS, I. TYNDAL L.—Employed in the colonial office from 1860 to 1864, when he was appointed to be colonial secretary at Labuan.

WORTHINGTON, G. E.—Appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, 1859; commissioner of requests and police magistrate, Chavagacherry, 1861; acting assistant government agent, Kurnegalle, Oct. 1863; assistant at Trincomalee to the government agent of the eastern province, Aug. 1865.
WOUTERSZ, W. A.—Appointed medical sub-assistant, Ceylon, 1828; medical sub-assistant, 2nd class, 1842; medical sub-assistant, 1st class, 1845; medical assistant, 1858.

WRAY, MAJOR HENRY, R.E.—Entered the corps of the royal engineers as 2nd lieutenant, Dec. 20, 1843; was promoted to be lieutenant, April 1, 1846, and captain, Feb. 17, 1854; was employed in Western Australia under the colonial office, in connection with the convict service, from Sept. 1851 till May, 1858; was acting comptroller-general of convicts from Feb. 1856 till Jan. 1858; was appointed, Dec. 15th, 1859, British commissioner for marking the boundary between Guatemala and British Honduras, as defined by the convention of April 30, 1859; was given the local rank of mayor in Central America, Sept. 25, 1860.

WRIGHT, THE VEN. H. PRESS.—Archdeacon of Colombia, M.A. St. Peter's College, Cambridge; Deacon, 1841; priest, 1842; incumbent of St. Mary's, Leeds, 1845-46; chaplain to the forces, Ionian Islands, 1846-54; principal chaplain to the army in the East, 1854-56; chaplain to field-marshal H.R.H. the Duke of Cambridge, K.G., 1856; promoted by special gazette "for distinguished services in the field," Jan. 1 1859 chaplain to the
forces, 1st class, 1861; archdeacon of Columbia, Feb. 11, 1861;

WRIGHT, W. D.—Appointed a writer to government of Ceylon, 1853; assistant government agent, Kandy, 1854; acting district judge of Matura, 1859; and landing surveyor at St. John's River, Colombo, 1861; acting deputy collector of customs, Colombo, March 1862; acting principal collector of customs, July 1862; is a member of the legislative council; again deputy collector, 1863; again landing surveyor, St. John's River, July 1864; acting assistant government agent, Matura, Nov. 1864; acting deputy collector, Colombo, May, 1865.
WRIGHT, WILLIAM.—Advocate-general, New Brunswick.
WYATT, WILLIAM.—Protector of the Aborigines, South Australia, 1837; coroner of the province, 1847; chief inspector of schools, 1851.
WYLDE, C. S.—Revenue officer attached to customs' department, British Columbia; appointed April, 1859.

YEATES, DOUGALD.—Appointed stipendiary magistrate and coroner at Tobago, April, 1862; is also inspector of prisons, and a member of the privy council; was acting stipendiary magistrate in 1850; and held the office of public treasurer from 1851 to 1855: first entered the public service in the customs' department, 1849.
YEATES, WILLIAM.—Harbour-master and pilot, Tobago; appointed 1845; acting colonial secretary, 1866.
YOUNG, REV. A. (M.A. Aberdeen).—Appointed Scotch chaplain, Kandyan province, Ceylon, March 1859: and colonial chaplain, Dutch Presbyterian church, Colombo, 1861.
YOUNG, DONALD.—Sub-commissary of taxation, British Guiana.

YOUNG, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1847).—SIR HENRY EDWARD FOX YOUNG.—Was entered as a student of the Inner Temple, but has been uninterruptedly employed in the colonial civil service; was treasurer, secretary, puisne judge, and member of council at St. Lucia (1834); government secretary and member of the court of policy in British Guiana from 1835 to 1847; knighted on his return to England; appointed lieut.-governor of the eastern districts of the Cape of Good Hope, Feb. 1847; lieut.-governor of South Australia, June, 1847; governor-general and commander-in-chief of Tasmania, Sept. 1854.

YOUNG, 2nd BART. (United Kingdom, Creat. 1821); Privy Councillor (Great Britain and Ireland), 1852; G.C.M.G. 1855.—RIGHT HON. SIR JOHN YOUNG, son of the 1st baronet, born 1807; succeeded his father in 1848.—Was educated at Corpus Christi College, Oxford, where he graduated B.A. 1829; called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1834; was a lord of the treasury from Sept. 1841 to May, 1844; secretary to the treasury from May, 1844 to July, 1846; chief secretary to the lord-lieut. of Ireland from Dec. 1852 to March, 1855; was lord high commissioner of the Ionian Islands from March, 1855 to Feb. 1859; created a G.C.M.G. on receiving that appointment; created K.C.B. on retiring from the office; appointed governor-general and commander-in-chief of New South Wales, 1860. Was M.P. for Cavan from 1831 to 1855.

YOUNG, J. D.—Appointed district road officer at Batticaloa, Sept. 15, 1851; from Jan. to June, 1852, employed as superintending officer on the Matelle road; returned to Batticaloa, June, 1852, and served there till May, 1855; assistant civil engineer at Galle, and confirmed in that appoint-
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
ment in 1858; especially employed in 1859 in constructing dam, &c., to the canal at Ovrebokhe.

YOUNG, CHARLES, LL.D. of Newton University.—Called to the bar of Nova Scotia, 1837; appointed to legislative council, Prince Edward's Island, 1840, and continued therein till 1862; queen's counsel, 1847; member of the executive council and attorney-general, 1851; surrogate and judge of probate, 1852; president of legislative council, 1853; again attorney-general, 1858; administered the government after the departure of Sir Dominick Daly, and until the arrival of lieut-governor Dundas, 1859.

YOUNG, JAMES JUDSON.—Employed from Feb. 1856 to March 1857 as an extra clerk at Somerset House, in the department of the accountant-general of the navy; appointed Jan. 1860, clerk in the office of the colonial secretary, British Columbia; also acted as private secretary to Sir J. Douglas, from Sept. 1861, to date of his retirement in 1864.
YOUNG, WILLIAM A. G.—Was for some years in the royal navy, and served as naval secretary to several flag-officers upon different foreign stations; on the last occasion was secretary to the

commodore, the Hon. F. J. Pelham, of the Baltic fleet from Feb. 1855 to June, 1856; appointed secretary to the North American boundary commission, 1856; and colonial secretary and auditor of the colony of Vancouver's Island, March, 1859.
YOUNG, WILLIAM.—Chief justice of Nova Scotia.

YULE, J. N.—Was appointed resident at Swan River, Western Australia, 1838; acting protector of natives, 1843; acting clerk of the council, 1849; acting colonial secretary, 1850; and appointed police magistrate at Perth, 1852. Retired on a pension, 1862.
YULE, JOHN ALEX.—Appointed to be superintendent of the civil hospital, Hong Kong, June, 1864.
ZAMMIT, ANTONIO, LL.D.—Appointed magistrate of judicial police, Malta, Jan. 1829. Is a supplementary judge of the criminal court.

ZIMELLI, HECTOR.—Served as clerk in chief secretary's office, Malta, 1834; was clerk in audit office, and superintendent of public works department, and appointed superintendent of police in that island, Jan. 1855.

BEGS to inform Carriage Purchasers in the Colonies, and Gentlemen who are about to proceed thither, that he has on view a large stock of New and Second-hand Carriages of the best make, and having in the course of a long experience in the Trade built Carriages for all parts of the World, he is fully aware of the requirements of different Climates, such as the use of properly-seasoned and well-selected woods, with due attention to the construction of wheels (which in a hot climate, if not properly made, so often fail); also providing sufficient ventilation, and making a vehicle not only durable but luxurious in the inside, as in tropical climates a comfortable and easy lounge is so great a desideratum .

Sir Bernard Burke's (Ulster King of
Arms) Genealogical and Heraldic Dictionary of the Peerage and Baronetage of the British Empire. New edition, corrected throughout, complete in one volume, royal 8vo., price 38s.

This Peerage and Baronetage is the only complete book of the kind extant. It gives the historical and genealogical account and present state of all the Peers and Baronets, with their armorial bearings engraved and described; it furnishes full details respecting all the Archbishops and Bishops and their families; it enters fully into the subject of precedence, and sets forth all the Rolls, Authorizations, and Usages, which regulate it; Foreign Titles borne by British Subjects; Peerages recently Extinct and Dormant; the Orders of Knighthood, with their Insignia depicted and explained; the List of Knights and Knights Bachelors; the Mottoes of all the Peers and Baronets, with Translations and Illustrations; and an Alphabetical List of the Seats and Mansions. The above are the main features of the work, and such a combined and immense amount of information as this book contains would be vainly sought for in any other Peerage or Baronetage past or present.
Sir Bernard Burke's (Ulster King of

Arms) Genealogical and Heraldic Dictionary of the Landed Gentry of Great Britain and Ireland. Fourth Edition, complete in one volume. Bound in cloth, £2 16s., uniform with the Peerage and Baronetage. Or in Parts, viz., Part I (A to J), 25s.; Part II (K to Z, and Supplement), 30s.

"A work of this kind is of a national value. Its utility is not merely temporary, but it will exist and be acknowledged as long as the families, whose names and genealogies are recorded in it, continue to form an integral portion of the English constitution."—Post . "The whole forms a fine and full account of the Gentry of these realms—of that class 'who,' says Sir Bernard in his preface, 'possess, though undistinguished by hereditary titles, an undeniable right, from antiquity of race, extent of property, and brilliancy of achievements, to take foremost rank among the lesser nobility of Europe.'"— Illus. Lon. News .
Sir Bernard Burke's (Ulster King of
Arms) Genealogical History of the Dormant, Abeyant, Forfeited, and Extinct Peerages of the British Empire. Illustrated by Steel Engravings of the principal Arms. New edition, royal 8vo., price £2 2s.
In this new edition the history of each title is continued to the present time; and the various dignities are traced down to their existing representatives.

The Foreign Office List for 1867,

forming a complete British Diplomatic and Consular Handbook, exhibiting the rank, standing, and various services of every person employed in the Foreign Office, the Diplomatic Corps, and the Consular Body. Also Regulations respecting Examinations, Passports, Foreign Orders, &c. With Maps, showing where Her Majesty's Ambassadors, Ministers, Consuls, and others, are resident abroad; together with a List of Foreign Diplomatic and Consular Representatives within the Queen's Dominions. Compiled by EDWARD HERTSLET, Librarian and Keeper of the Papers, Foreign Office. New edition, corrected throughout. Bound in cloth, price 5s.

"The Foreign Office List gives the most careful notices of every person depending on, or in any way connected with, the Foreign Office, from the Minister to the youngest supernumerary clerk in his department, and from the Ambassador to the Cabinet Messenger. It moreover enlivens the dull monotony of official detail with the biographical notices of the functionaries whose names it records. It is to be found in all the Chancelleries abroad; no Attaché of a Mission, great or small, thinks his library complete without it; and it is one of the first objects that attracts the eye of the British traveller as he waits for his passport visa in a Consul's office." Times (from our own Correspondent).

Directory for the Civil Departments of the British Army, containing the Names, with brief Memoirs of all the Officers and others serving in the War Office, the Horse Guards, the Commissariat, Store, Purveying, Barrack, Chaplains', and Royal Engineer Civil Departments; also in the Manufacturing Departments and Educational Establishments of the Army, as well as of the Officers on the Retired List of those several services. Compiled from Official and other Documents, under permission of the Secretary of State for War, by DENHAM ROBINSON, of the War Office.
New edition, corrected to the present time, sewed, price 4s. 6d.
The Colonial Office List for 1867,

comprising Historical Notices of the Colonial Dependencies of Great Britain, together with Statistical and other general Information, including the Names and Services of the Principal Officers under the several Colonial Governments. It embodies the Colonial Regulations, the Rules respecting the Examination of Candidates for the Colonial Office, for Ceylon Writerships, and Hong Kong Cadetships; the Pension Minutes of several Colonies, and the Imperial Act under which Pensions are granted to Colonial Governors.
It also contains a beautifully executed Map, showing the British Dominions in every part of the World.

We have never read any book on any subject on which so much is said, and said so well in a shorter space. * * * There will be a great neglect of duty if Miss Nightingale's admirable work is not so carefully studied that it becomes, in fact, the manual of every sick room." — Times . "These 'Notes' are addressed to women, and every woman in the country ought to read them." — Daily News . "The work is but an inexpensive pamphlet, not costing the price of one bottle of medicine, but it is destined, we trust, to reduce the doctors' bills in many families. * * * To every class, from the highest to the lowest, these 'Notes' are addressed." — Examiner . "Every father of a family should change his silver for these 'Notes' (their price is fixed so moderately he need not change his gold for them), and every member of a family should both hear and try to profit by them. "We wish it may find its way not only into every hospital and workhouse, but into every school and nursery in the kingdom." — Saturday Review .

SIMPLICITY, DURABILITY, CHEAPNESS, POWER.

MESSRS. HARRISON & SONS, Wholesale Stationers, Parliamentary Printers, &c., beg to call attention to the Patent Duplex Press, for Stamping Notarial, Official, Corporate, and other Seals, &c., which for portability, durability, power, and simplicity of construction, and consequent cheapness is unrivalled.
So little force is needed to work the Patent Duplex Press that it requires no fixing, and a stand is not necessary; it may be placed on an ordinary table or desk.

It is constructed without screw or springs: the power being produced by a cam acting on a bell crank lever. From the extreme simplicity of its mechanism it can be supplied at one half the price of an ordinary screw press.
The Patent Duplex Press can be fitted with a lock so as to prevent its being used by an unauthorised person.

THIS WORK consists of a collection of the most important Official Documents respecting the Political and Commercial Relations existing between Great Britain and Foreign Powers, and between each other; Constitutional and Commercial Laws and Institutions; Protocols of Conferences; Treaties (Political, Territorial, Commercial, and Slave Trade); Laws; Constitutions; Ordinances; Proclamations; Orders in Council; Colonial Laws, &c., with a selection of Papers laid before the British Parliament, and before American, French, and other Foreign Legislatures, bearing on International Questions, such as Mediation, Arbitration, Blockade, Boundary, Claims, Enlistments, Navigation of Rivers, Declarations of War, &c.

To facilitate reference, the Work is comprehensively and elaborately indexed. Each volume has a Table of Contents and an Alphabetical Index. Volume XLIII (containing upwards of 1,100 pages,) consists of a Chronological and Alphabetical Index to the preceding forty-two Volumes. This arrangement not only allows of a ready reference to all the papers respecting a particular subject or country, but also to all records and transactions in any year.

The subsequent Volumes are in course of preparation.
